The story centrally illustrates R' Yehoshua's virtue — his scrupulous concern for the community's kashrut combined with deliberate self-affliction (feigning illness) to avoid shaming the shochet — while R' Mordecai's supernatural discernment vindicates the shochet; virtues are clearly foregrounded, though the miraculous sensing element also pushes toward a wonder-tale type.

The story's primary thrust is the Besht's miraculous foreknowledge and orchestration of events (a wonder-tale), not the illustration of virtues such as humility, charity, or faith, even though bitachon and care for orphans appear incidentally.

The story centrally illustrates the tzaddik Rabbi Shimshon's virtues — uprightness of heart (choosing a poor bride over a wealthy one), and piety/faith (prioritizing a mitzvah of circumcision over the king's royal summons) — and his virtue is precisely what triggers his miraculous deliverance, though the narrative's dramatic emphasis falls as much on divine providence and poetic justice (the bishop's downfall) as on the tzaddik's character per se.

The story's central point is to demonstrate the Besht's holiness, fearlessness, and faith in God — explicitly contrasted with the sorcerer's craft — as he saves an infant and defeats a powerful evil-doer through divine power alone.

The story is primarily a wondrous-death narrative (the Besht's passing on Shavuot), not a virtue-illustration story; faith and equanimity appear incidentally within the death-scene frame rather than being the narrative's point.

The story's central point is to illustrate the Baal Shem Tov's extraordinary spiritual power and virtue — his ability to commune with and elevate the souls of the dead during prayer, demonstrating his unique mystical capacity far beyond that of the learned R. Gershon, who collapses when he attempts the same.

The story's primary thrust is demonstrating the Besht's supernatural powers (clairvoyance, kefitzat ha-aretz, prophecy) and his conflict with a hostile community, with virtues such as piety (knife inspection) appearing only incidentally rather than as the story's central point.

The story's central point is to illustrate the Besht's virtues — his spiritual perception, compassion, and selfless intervention to rescue a fallen woman's soul at the urgent request of her righteous ancestor, traveling miraculously through the night to save her.

The story's central point is a legal/moral puzzle about transferring sin and the consequences of avoiding repentance, not an illustration of the Maharsha's personal virtues — he appears as a rabbinic judge/authority figure but the narrative focus is on the two merchants and their theological/ethical dilemma.

The story centers on the Besht's ecstatic prayer states, miraculous physical phenomena (trembling water, grain), and spiritual warfare against the Satan — it is primarily a wonder/power tale rather than one whose point is to illustrate moral virtues such as humility, charity, or faith.

The passage is a homiletical Torah teaching interpreting a Psalm verse about prayer substituting for Temple sacrifices — it contains no narrative depicting a tzaddik's personal virtues such as humility, charity, or faith.

The story explicitly highlights R' Nachum's humility (ברוב ענוותנותו) as a named virtue — he declares he saw nothing and did not faint, framing his lack of vision as personal unworthiness rather than spiritual superiority, making the illustration of a tzaddik's virtue (humility) a direct and central point of the narrative.

The story's primary point is demonstrating the Besht's miraculous/supernatural powers (diagnosing spiritual states via pulse, supernaturally revealing stolen goods) and the humiliation of the arrogant doctor, not to illustrate virtues such as humility, charity, or faith as its central theme — the love-of-God motif is present but incidental to the narrative arc.

The story illustrates the tzaddik's homiletical cleverness (a witty re-reading of the Shulchan Aruch), not his personal virtues such as humility, charity, or faith — it is a teaching/wisdom anecdote, not a virtue exemplum.

The story centers on the Besht's miraculous power to grant a male heir and his prophetic foreknowledge in choosing what is truly good for his follower (son over wealth), directly illustrating his virtues as a tzaddik.

The story's central point is to illustrate the Baal Shem Tov's extraordinary spiritual insight and wisdom — he perceives the visitor's hidden desire, teaches him esoteric lore, then erases it to guide him toward sincere divine service, demonstrating the tzaddik's virtues of discernment, spiritual power, and pastoral care.

The story centers on the Besht's virtues: his charity (pledging his tallit to redeem the goldsmith's debt), self-sacrifice (losing a horse worth ten coins to save the rabbi), and spiritual wisdom (counseling against excessive fasting in favor of joyful divine service).

The story's point centers on the slaughterhouse boy's selfless care for the dying maggid and the maggid's posthumous intercession, not on illustrating the Besht's virtues — the Besht's role (immediately visiting the sick boy) is functional rather than the moral focal point.

The story centers on the Alter Rebbe's supernatural perception and divine wisdom (clairvoyance about Paul I's death, helping Alexander I identify the murderers) and his political imprisonment and release — not on illustrating moral virtues such as humility, charity, or faith.

The story's central point is demonstrating the Besht's supernatural ability to read minds (knowing the divine name in Rabbi Nachman's thoughts), not illustrating virtues such as humility, charity, or faith.

The story centers on spiritual lineage and the recognition of a hidden tzaddik (nistar): a poor man is sent by his dying rebbe to find Moshe Shtagel, who secretly teaches him Torah, and ultimately the Rizhiner is identified as tzaddik ha-dor — there is no narrative of protecting anyone from illness, hostile authorities, or demons.
The story centers on the Besht's hidden life and gradual revelation to the community, with no narrative of the tzaddik protecting anyone from illness, hostile authorities, or demons.

The story explicitly depicts the Besht confronting and subduing a possessing spirit (dybbuk) in a woman and later healing a mentally ill person (משוגע), both of which are canonical instances of the tzaddik protecting/rescuing someone from demonic affliction and illness.

The story's central narrative is the Besht healing the gravely ill Maggid through sustained spiritual and physical intervention (sitting with him reciting Psalms, administering crushed diamond, midnight Kabbalah sessions, multiple mikveh immersions) — protection from illness by the tzaddik is the explicit through-line.
The story centers on the Besht raising and rectifying souls of the dead during prayer, not on protecting a person from illness, hostile authorities, or demons.

The story's central narrative is a biographical account of the Besht's self-revelation and hidden ascetic years; while it contains incidental episodes of exorcism, rain-prayer, and healing, none of these constitute the story's organizing center.

The central narrative is a repentance-and-redemption story about a Jewish informer (מסור), not a tzaddik protecting someone from danger; the rabbi appears only peripherally at the end to prevent posthumous disgrace and receive a dream revelation.

The central narrative is about the tzaddik granting a childless man a son and later transforming the wild son into a Torah scholar through advice and miraculous intervention — a blessing/wonder story, not one whose core is protecting someone from illness, hostile authorities, or demons.

The story centers on the Besht revealing a hidden sin (striking one's father) and prescribing repentance, not on protecting anyone from illness, authorities, or demons; the man ultimately dies for abandoning the penance, making protection the inverse of the narrative's thrust.

The Besht's role is to provide clairvoyant information about the delayed son's whereabouts (via the Zohar), not to protect anyone from illness, hostile authorities, or demons; the son's delay is due to a broken wagon, and the central narrative is about the Besht's supernatural sight and a skeptic's conversion.

The central narrative is about karmic debt, reincarnation, and moral accountability — the Besht acts as a revealer of hidden spiritual truths and a moral judge, not as a protector shielding anyone from illness, hostile authorities, or demons.
The story's central theme is the Besht's mystical practice of elevating souls of the dead during the 'מחיה מתים' prayer; no one is being protected from illness, hostile authorities, or demons.

The story's central narrative is the Besht healing the gravely ill Maggid through prolonged spiritual and physical remedies (reciting Psalms for weeks, administering ground diamond, midnight Kabbalistic session), making illness-protection the explicit core of the account.
The story's central narrative is the Besht's death (histalkut) on Shavuot; the brief episode of rebuking a wandering soul that harms a girl is an incidental detail, not the story's driving concern.
The Besht's role is to reveal a reincarnation truth (gilgul) and provide spiritual counsel, not to shield the scholar from any concrete danger such as illness, hostile authorities, or demons.

The story centers on the Besht accompanying a rabbi to a new post, a Shabbat scholarly debate, and a moral lesson about Torah study (the Besht removes and restores Rabbi Mikhl's learning to rebuke pride) — there is no central narrative of the tzaddik protecting someone from illness, hostile authorities, or demons.

The central narrative depicts the Maggid of Mezeritch supernaturally protecting a Jewish boy from forced apostasy by non-Jews — reciting Torah seven times until a miraculous storm wind physically carries the boy to safety, an unambiguous tzaddik-rescues-someone-from-danger story.

The story's central narrative is the Besht transmitting and then erasing esoteric knowledge (animal/bird speech) from the Mokhiah — there is no element of protecting anyone from illness, hostile authorities, or demons.

The Besht attempts to protect the martyrs and promises they will be saved, but they are killed; the central narrative is not successful protection but rather the Besht's grief, a mystical encounter with the martyred souls, and a theological explanation of why heaven concealed the decree and why the tzaddik's protection failed.
The central narrative is about the tzaddik punishing/distancing a ward who mocked holy figures, not protecting anyone from danger such as illness, hostile authorities, or demons.

The central narrative is a blessing-for-children (פרי בטן) story in which Rabbi Pinchas of Koritz overrides the Shpola Zeyde's oath through his own counter-oath, resolved by a heavenly court — not a story of a tzaddik shielding someone from illness, hostile authorities, or demons.

The central narrative celebrates a pious woman's humility and intercessory virtue rather than the tzaddik's protective intervention; the doctor's healing results from the woman's own advocacy (meliẓa), with the Besht serving as a witness and catalyst rather than the protective agent.

The story's central narrative is the prolonged dispute (מחלוקת) between the Rav and R' Baruch of Medzhybizh; the healing of Vitled's daughter is a significant but embedded sub-episode, not the structuring core of the story.

The central narrative is the tzaddik orchestrating a shidduch (matchmaking) between the avrech and the wealthy woman; the illness-protection episode (the woman revealing the tzaddik threatened her with death unless she agreed to marry) is a subordinate backstory element revealed at the end, not the story's main focus.

The story's central episode depicts Rabbi Mordechai of Lechovitch brought before the governor on the informer Avigdor's accusations, whereupon the governor inexplicably dismisses the charges and expels the informer — a clear narrative of the tzaddik being miraculously protected from hostile authorities.

The story centers on Rabbi Yisrael Palitchker's spiritual quest and missionary journey to recruit disciples for the Maggid of Mezeritch; no tzaddik protects anyone from illness, hostile authorities, or demonic danger.

The story centers on martyrdom, heavenly apparitions, and divine theodicy: the Besht attempts to assure the victims' survival but they are killed anyway, and the narrative focuses on his grief, the souls' nocturnal visit, and the explanation for why heaven withheld the decree — not on the tzaddik successfully protecting anyone from danger.

The central narrative depicts the Besht protected from a hostile authority threat (a commissioner threatening to put him in iron chains over a fabricated debt), resolved when R' Leib Kreminer's intervention causes the nobleman to burn the promissory note and actually pay the Besht.
The central narrative concerns the tzaddik vindicating his wife's honor and exposing an adulteress by summoning a dead witness — not protecting anyone from illness, hostile authorities, or demons.

The central narrative concerns the Besht's spiritual authority and posthumous vindication, the mechanics of Gan Eden, and the consequences of breaking a promise — the Besht's pledge to ease the dying man's entry into Paradise is a secondary motif, and it partially fails; there is no sustained depiction of the tzaddik shielding someone from illness, demons, or hostile authorities.

The story centrally depicts the Besht orchestrating a resolution to a real threat from a hostile commissioner (who threatens to haul R' Leib away in iron chains over a debt), thereby protecting both R' Leib and himself — though the narrative is equally weighted toward the Besht's miraculous foreknowledge and the financial miracle, making protection one of several interwoven themes rather than the sole focus.

The story centers on the Besht exposing a hidden sin (striking one's father) and prescribing repentance; the man's death results from abandoning that repentance regimen, not from the tzaddik's failure or success in protecting him from any external danger.

The central narrative concerns the Besht's guidance to a childless man (fertility advice, son's moral transformation, and a miraculous commercial encounter with the prophet Yonah ben Amitai) — not the tzaddik shielding anyone from illness, hostile authorities, or demonic threat.

The danger and rescue in this story flow *toward* the tzaddik (the Besht and R' Tzvi are captured by bandits and saved by a mysterious captain identified as Elijah), not *from* him — the Besht does not protect another person from danger, which is what the tag requires.

The tzaddik's role here is spiritual intercession (promising Gan Eden entry to a dying man) and establishing his own credibility among skeptics, not protecting anyone from danger — the sick man dies, and the narrative centers on oath-breaking and its posthumous consequences rather than a protection episode.

The story centers on Rabbi Leib severing ties with a ward who mocked distinguished tzaddikim — a tale of punishment for disrespect, not of the tzaddik shielding anyone from illness, hostile authorities, or demonic danger.

The story centers on the Besht demonstrating clairvoyant powers and escaping a hostile community via miraculous travel (kefitzat ha-aretz) to protect himself from embarrassment, not on him protecting another person from illness, hostile authorities, or demons.

The story centers on the validation of a shochet's kashrut through competing rabbinic judgment and Rabbi Mordechai's spiritual discernment — no tzaddik protects anyone from illness, hostile authorities, or demons; the brief illness is self-invoked by Rabbi Yehoshua as a ruse and is incidental, not the narrative's core.

The central narrative is the Besht's death/passing (הסתלקות) on Shavuot; attempts to intercede on his behalf fail, and the brief episode of the wandering soul harming a girl is entirely incidental to the main story rather than a protective act that drives the plot.
The story's central narrative is the conversion of a skeptical wealthy man through the tzaddik's demonstrated clairvoyance — not protection of anyone from illness, hostile authorities, or demons.
The story centers on the Besht's hidden life of piety and his gradual public revelation to the community, not on protecting any person from illness, hostile authorities, or demonic forces.

The central narrative is a hidden-tzaddik-revealed and rabbinic-succession story — the tailor's sanctity is disclosed posthumously through his authority to appoint a rabbi; there is no depiction of the tzaddik protecting anyone from illness, hostile authorities, or demons.

The central narrative is a fertility miracle — Rabbi Pinchas of Koretz's oath grants a childless hasid a son, overriding the Shpola Zeyde's counter-oath; while there is an element of reversing a harmful decree, the story's focus is on intercession for offspring, not on the tzaddik shielding someone from illness, hostile authorities, or demons.

The story centers on the Besht escorting a rabbi to his post, a scholarly dispute, and the Besht rebuking and spiritually correcting a disputatious dayan by temporarily removing his Torah knowledge — not on protecting anyone from illness, hostile authorities, or demons.

The story's central narrative is the Besht's prophetic knowledge and miraculous provision of a wagon; the childbirth intervention (מקשה לילד) touches on danger but is a concluding episode, not the dominant focus of the story.

The central narrative is the tzaddik orchestrating a marriage match (shidduch) through elaborate financial and social maneuvers; the illness from which the woman is protected is a secondary detail revealed only at the story's end, not the driving focus of the plot.

The tzaddik (Besht) is a witness and teacher of the innkeeper's bitachon, not a protector — the innkeeper's danger (debt/captivity) is resolved through divine providence and a chance buyer, with no protective intervention by the Besht.

The central narrative is the Besht proving his ruach hakodesh before the Council of Four Lands by revealing hidden knowledge (the Parnas's forgotten prayer, the possessed bed); no one is being protected from danger — the demonic element is incidental, a parting demonstration of perception rather than an act of protection.

The central narrative concerns the Besht teaching his disciple R. David Pirkus the virtue of bitachon (trust in God) — protection from financial ruin and the merchant's wife's healing are outcomes that illustrate the lesson, not the story's organizing principle.

A central episode depicts the young Besht protecting a group of children from a sorcerer who transforms into a werewolf (וואקילאק), explicitly confronting and killing the demonic threat to safeguard his charges — a textbook tzaddik-protects-from-demon narrative.

The story's central narrative is about social recognition of the Besht (ignored guest → honored guest), with the healing of the rabbi's prayer-obstruction (likely a spiritual/demonic interference during Shema) serving as the mechanism for that recognition rather than as the primary focus of the story.

The entire narrative centers on the Besht intervening through intense prayer and spiritual self-sacrifice (accepting pulsa de-nura) to save a gravely ill child from death — a textbook tzaddik-protects-from-illness story.

The central narrative depicts Rabbi Baruch of Mezhibuzh protecting a Hasid from financial ruin at the hands of hostile non-Jewish carriers who planned to steal his goods, sacrificing his own this-worldly and other-worldly reward to avert the loss — a clear case of a tzaddik protecting someone from danger (hostile actors/financial harm).

The central narrative turns on the tzaddik exposing a fraudulent get and stopping an illegal second marriage at the chuppah, thereby protecting the woman and her true husband from grave spiritual and legal harm — classic tzaddik-as-protector structure.

The story's central content is the Besht's prophetic/visionary powers (foreseeing R. Eliezer of Amsterdam's death, identifying a Sabbatean heretical book, dreaming of potential apostasy) and an encounter with a Shabbatean figure in which the Besht himself is nearly cast into the abyss — not a narrative whose core is the tzaddik shielding an identified person from illness, hostile authorities, or demonic attack.

The story's central narrative is a didactic journey teaching the virtue of bitachon (trust in God), not a tzaddik actively protecting someone from illness, hostile authorities, or demons — R' Mikhl's role is that of a spiritual guide who dispatches the man to learn from an exemplar, not a protector intervening against a threat.

The story depicts a tzaddik comforting his weeping disciples before his death with an eschatological promise about future tzaddikim — there is no narrative of protection from illness, hostile authorities, or demons.

The story depicts a skeptical scholar visiting the Maggid of Mezeritch and receiving a teaching about why different tzaddikim serve God through different attributes — there is no narrative of the tzaddik protecting anyone from illness, hostile authorities, or demonic threat.
The story is a parable about spiritual devotion and closeness to God, with no narrative of a tzaddik protecting anyone from illness, hostile authorities, or demons.

The story recounts a visionary dream in which the Besht is seen with spiritual figures (Elijah, the Ari), and a discussion of a tzaddik's soul as a spark of Rabbi Yochanan ben Zakkai — there is no narrative of a tzaddik protecting anyone from danger, illness, authorities, or demons.

The tzaddik perceives an imminent threat from Tatar raiders approaching Nemirov, responds by commanding protective psalm recitation (Psalm 91 × 91 times), and the enemy camp subsequently turns back — making communal protection from hostile forces the story's culminating point, even though the causal link between his ritual action and the outcome is implicit rather than stated.

The central narrative depicts the tzaddik actively healing sick children afflicted with skin disease (שחין) using his hands and poultices, which directly fits the 'tzaddik protecting from illness' definition of the tag.

The story centers on a scholarly debate between a Hasidic rabbi and a Mitnagdic Gaon (R' Yehoshua Zeitleles of Shklov), with the tzaddik demonstrating Talmudic mastery — there is no depiction of protection from illness, hostile authorities, or demons.

The Besht issues a prophetic warning rather than actively protecting R. Moshe — the danger ultimately prevails (R. Moshe is taken to Venice against his will and dies in prison), making this a story of fulfilled prophecy, not of tzaddik-mediated protection.

The story is a hagiographic account of R' Aharon Halevi Horowitz's discipleship under the Rebbe — covering spiritual succession, transmission of wisdom, and a marriage-negotiation anecdote — with no depiction of the tzaddik protecting anyone from illness, hostile authorities, or demons.

The story centers on the Besht and his disciples being led astray and tested as divine punishment, with no outsider or skeptic being drawn close to a tzaddik — the hidden tzaddik figure (the coarse man) serves a punitive/redemptive role toward the Besht himself, not a rapprochement arc.

The scholar begins as someone who has never sought the Besht's help and is persuaded by his wife to go; after the encounter and the revelation about his gilgul, he becomes a devoted disciple of the Besht — a clear narrative arc of an outsider drawn close to the tzaddik.

The Hasid is already a devoted follower of the Shpoler Zeyde from the outset; the narrative arc concerns competing tzaddik oaths and miraculous child-granting, not an outsider or skeptic being drawn close to a tzaddik or Hasidism.

The man is reluctantly summoned and momentarily accepts the Besht's authority and teshuvah regimen, but the narrative arc centers on his subsequent abandonment of the regimen and consequent death — a cautionary tale about teshuvah, not a story whose arc is the drawing of an outsider/skeptic into closeness with the tzaddik.

R' Gershon begins the story as a skeptic who questions and implicitly mocks the Besht's prolonged, ecstatic prayer, but through the Besht's explanation and the transfer of kavvanot he moves to direct participation — experiencing the souls himself — constituting a rapprochement arc, though it is partial (he faints and is gently mocked at the end, and he is already the Besht's brother-in-law rather than a true outsider).

The story is a chain of testimonies about the Besht's extraordinary trembling and the Divine Presence during prayer; none of the narrative episodes depict an outsider or skeptic being drawn close to the tzaddik through the events — all figures (R' Avraham, R' Ze'ev Kutzes, the Maggid, R' David) are already within the Hasidic circle.

The story consists of testimonies about the Besht's extraordinary trembling prayer and spiritual power; all characters (R' Avraham, R' Volf Kutzes, the Maggid, R' David the cantor) are already community insiders or associates, and no outsider or skeptic undergoes a narrative arc of being drawn close to the tzaddik or Hasidism.

The story's arc centers on a theological lesson about true self-sacrifice (mesirat nefesh), with all characters — the Hasid, R. Avraham of Khmelnik, and the narrator R. Menachem Mendel — already insiders; there is no outsider or skeptic being drawn close to a tzaddik or to Hasidism.

Rabbi Aryeh Leib is already described as close to and beloved by the Besht before the story begins; the narrative arc is a teaching/lesson about the limits of esoteric knowledge, not the drawing of an outsider or skeptic into closeness with the tzaddik.

The man is momentarily drawn to the Besht and accepts a teshuvah regimen, but the narrative arc ends in his abandonment of that repentance and his death — the story's moral centers on the consequences of reneging on teshuvah, not on an outsider being successfully brought close to the tzaddik.

Although R' Gershon's skepticism toward the Besht is mentioned (calling him an am ha'aretz and urging divorce), the story's narrative arc centers on the Besht's hidden ascetic period and gradual self-revelation at age 36 — R' Gershon's potential rapprochement is neither depicted nor resolved within this story.

The protagonist is already a devoted Hasid of the Shpoler Zeyde from the story's outset; the narrative arc concerns a childless Hasid seeking a blessing and the conflict between two tzaddikim's oaths, not the drawing of an outsider or skeptic into Hasidism.

The innkeeper undergoes dramatic repentance upon hearing Torah teaching but dies in the forest and never actually draws close to the tzaddik; the central disciple is already a devoted Hasid, and no outsider/skeptic is brought into the Hasidic fold through the story's arc.

Rabbi Gershon begins as a skeptic who questions and mocks the Besht's prolonged prayer, but through the narrative — the Besht's explanation of his soul-raising work and Rabbi Gershon's own overwhelming direct experience of the same kavvanah — he is brought to recognition of the Besht's spiritual power, with the closing rebuke confirming his prior mockeryas the arc's starting point.

The messengers are already committed Hasidim whose faith in their rebbe is repeatedly affirmed; the drunkard is never drawn toward the tzaddik or Hasidism — the narrative arc is about revealing a hidden merit (pidyon shvuyim + resisting sin) that explains a paradox, not about converting or drawing in any outsider or skeptic.

The story centers on the Besht's grief over martyrs and his mystical nocturnal encounter with their souls; Rabbi David functions as a witness/narrator figure already present in the Besht's court, not as an outsider or skeptic undergoing a drawn-close arc.

Although the son-in-law is an outsider/skeptic who initially refuses to visit and eventually travels to Rabbi Elimelech, the narrative arc culminates in a rebuke of his scholarly pride rather than in his being drawn close — he departs confused and dejected ('בפחי נפש') with no depicted transformation or acceptance of Hasidism.

The story centers on a prolonged dispute between two leading tzaddikim (the Alter Rebbe and R. Baruch of Medzibozh); the embedded episode of Veitl involves a woman already sympathetic to Hasidism, and her husband (aligned with R. Baruch) explicitly refuses to heed the Rebbe — the arc moves away from, not toward, rapprochement with a tzaddik.

The story's arc is a personal repentance (teshuvah) narrative — the moser is moved to abandon his sins through his own prayer, not drawn close to a tzaddik or to Hasidism; the rabbi appears only peripherally after the protagonist's death.

The story's central arc is the arrest and imprisonment of Rabbi Shneur Zalman and the political conflict with the mitnagdim; while gentile officials (the sardiot, the fortress general, Emperor Pavel) show respect or awe toward the Rebbe, none of these constitute a Jewish outsider/skeptic being drawn close to a tzaddik or Hasidism, and the one figure who is a nephew of the Vilna Gaon (Leyb of Yanovitch) is already described as a sympathizer, not a skeptic undergoing rapprochement.

The narrative arc is the opposite of rapprochement: the young man (an insider who mocks R' Baruch and R' Ephraim) is permanently expelled by R' Leib, who refuses all reconciliation — there is no drawing close to the tzaddik or Hasidism.

The story's arc is a miraculous rescue of a Jewish boy from apostasy through the Maggid's spiritual power; no outsider or skeptic is drawn close to a tzaddik or to Hasidism — the disciples are already followers, and the boy is simply returned to his mother.

The story is a hagiographic death-narrative (histalkut) of the Besht told entirely from within his inner circle of disciples; no outsider or skeptic is present, and no arc of being drawn close to the tzaddik occurs.

The story depicts an otherworldly soul-journey and heavenly intercession by the Besht on behalf of a sick child who already belongs to the Hasidic world (son of R. Mikhl of Zlotchov); there is no outsider or skeptic being drawn close to a tzaddik or to Hasidism.

The story's arc centers on the Besht recognizing and affirming the piety of 'die fromme Rivele' — there is no outsider or skeptic who is drawn close to the tzaddik or to Hasidism; all parties (the woman, her sons, the townspeople) are already within or positively oriented toward the Hasidic world.

The story explicitly traces a Torah scholar who had never previously turned to the Besht, is persuaded by his wife to seek him out, follows the Besht's instructions, receives a startling revelation about his soul's past, and ends as one of the Besht's disciples — a textbook rapprochement arc.

The wealthy merchant explicitly refuses to visit the Besht ('לא רצה ליסע לבעש״ט בשום אופן') and is skeptical of his advice throughout, but the miraculous fulfillment of the Besht's predictions (the son's birth and transformation) and the supernatural Shabbat encounter with Yona culminate in the merchant immediately journeying to the Besht — a clear arc from resistance to full rapprochement.

The story's arc centers on demonstrating the power of a single mitzvah (pidyon shvuyim), not on drawing an outsider or skeptic close to the tzaddik — the messengers are already faithful Hasidim, and the drunkard is never himself converted or brought near Hasidism.

The story depicts the opposite of rapprochement: the young man who mocks the tzaddikim (Reb Baruch and Reb Efraim) is permanently rejected and distanced by his rebbe, never reconciled or drawn close to Hasidism.

The story's central arc is the Besht's hisgalus (self-revelation) — his hidden ascetic life, the possessed woman's disclosure, his exorcism and rain miracle, and his eventual public role; R' Gershon's initial skepticism is mentioned as background but the narrative never resolves into his being drawn close to the Besht.

The innkeeper undergoes dramatic repentance (teshuvah) but never actually reaches the tzaddik or is drawn into Hasidism — he dies in the forest before any such rapprochement occurs; the narrative arc centers on divine providence and the power of Torah/mussar to inspire repentance, not on an outsider being brought close to the tzaddik or Hasidism.

The story contains no outsider/skeptic drawn toward the Besht or Hasidism; its episodes center on the Besht's clairvoyance (R. Eliezer's death), warning against Sabbatean literature (Chemdas Yamim), and a spiritual battle with Shabbetai Tzvi — none of which follows a rapprochement arc.

The story depicts the Apter Rebbe investigating and ultimately validating a hidden righteous innkeeper — a 'nistar' recognition narrative — not an outsider or skeptic being drawn close to a tzaddik or Hasidism.

R' David is a witness-observer at the door of the Besht's room during a mystical night vision, but the story's arc is a martyrology/theodicy narrative — the Besht's grief over the Pavlytch martyrs and the heavenly explanation for why revenge was not taken — with no portrayal of R' David as an outsider or skeptic being drawn close to the Besht or Hasidism.

The story depicts a supernatural battle between the Baal Shem Tov and the Samael (Satan) to retrieve a hidden secret, with no outsider or skeptic character being drawn close to Hasidism — the only secondary figure (R' Leib Trestinetzer) is already a fellow tzaddik and collaborator from the outset.
The story's arc is about the Apter Rebbe investigating and ultimately validating a hidden tzaddik (the innkeeper), not about an outsider or skeptic being drawn close to a tzaddik or to Hasidism.

The story explicitly depicts a wealthy skeptic from Risha who had never entered Hasidism ('מעולם לא דרכו רגליו על מפתן החסידות') travelling to the Besht out of curiosity, then being wholly transformed — falling prostrate before him and acknowledging his sin — after the Besht's parable reveals his own guilt, making this a textbook rapprochement arc.

The story's arc centers on the Besht neutralizing a spiritual accusation (קטרוג) by causing the priest an involuntary emission, not on drawing the priest close to Hasidism or converting him to a follower.

The embedded central narrative explicitly depicts an 'איש המוני' (common outsider) from a family that 'never traveled to Hasidic rabbis,' who approaches the tzaddik Rabbi Aharon of Strashele only out of desperation (demonic affliction), is healed and spiritually transformed through the encounter, and becomes a devoted follower who makes pilgrimage to the tzaddik's grave 17–18 years after his death — a textbook rapprochement arc.

R' Meir is already a willing companion and respectful associate of the Besht; his anxiety about money is a practical worry, not skepticism toward Hasidism, and there is no arc in which an outsider or skeptic is drawn close to the tzaddik through the events.

The story's arc centers on the Mokhiakh's independent righteous action (nullifying decrees for the rabbi) being discovered and praised by the Besht; no outsider or skeptic is drawn close to the Besht or Hasidism through the events.

The innkeeper moves from ignorance of the Besht to gratitude and praise of God after his child is saved, but this is a framing episode rather than the story's central arc, which is dominated by the prolonged sorcery-battle between the Besht and the nobleman-sorcerer; the sorcerer himself is blinded and defeated rather than drawn close.

The narrative arc is one of miraculous rescue and teshuvah — a fallen woman is saved from apostasy and led to repentance — not rapprochement in the folkloristic sense of an outsider or skeptic being drawn close to the tzaddik or Hasidism through the story's events.

The narrative arc is the inverse of rapprochement: the tzaddik exposes the wealthy man's guilt across reincarnations and deliberately turns away from him at the end, rejecting rather than drawing him close.

The story's protagonist (R' David of Mikleiev) is already a devoted Hasid of the Besht; the narrative arc is a hidden-tzaddik-vindicated/galut-atonement type, not one in which an outsider or skeptic is drawn close to a tzaddik or to Hasidism.

The story's protagonist (Menachem Mendel of Vitebsk) is already a devoted student of the Maggid of Mezeritch from childhood — not an outsider or skeptic — and the narrative arc concerns his spiritual formation, hidden humility, and destined path to the Holy Land, not a drawing-near to Hasidism from outside.

The story's central arc is the wealthy merchant (an outsider/skeptic) being drawn to the Besht through the miraculous revelation of his private ordeal, culminating in his fulfillment of his vow and return home joyful — a classic rapprochement narrative.

The story's entire arc culminates in the townspeople of Iași witnessing the tzaddik's miraculous power (summoning a dead man's testimony) and being drawn from skepticism/hostility to awe and recognition of who he truly is.

The protagonist (Rabbi Aryeh Leib the preacher) is already an established follower who is close to the Besht; the story depicts a specific pedagogical encounter and the withdrawal of esoteric knowledge, not the drawing of an outsider or skeptic into the tzaddik's circle.

The story's entire arc depicts the wife—an outsider who initially resisted and ignored the tzaddik (Reb Leib Sarah's)—being progressively drawn to accept his guidance through supernatural interventions (parental dream, life-threatening illness), culminating in her full recognition of him as a great tzaddik.

The story explicitly begins with a wealthy skeptic who refused to visit the Besht under any circumstances (לא רצה ליסע להבעש״ט בשום אופן), and its entire arc traces his gradual conversion — through fulfilled prophecy, miraculous events, and final joyful return to the Besht — into a convinced believer.

The narrative arc is about the downfall and punishment of informers/opponents of Hasidism — not about any outsider or skeptic being drawn close to the tzaddik; the governor's dismissal of the slander case is incidental rather than a rapprochement arc.

The story's arc centers on the Besht recognizing and confirming the exceptional piety of the already-devout 'di frume Rivele' — no outsider or skeptic is drawn closer to the Besht or Hasidism through the narrative.

The narrative arc centers on the Besht's Passover miracle, his self-sacrificing oath, and his thwarted voyage to the Land of Israel; the Polish traveler who aids him already knows and seeks out the Besht, so there is no outsider/skeptic being drawn close to the tzaddik or to Hasidism.

The story centers on Rabbi Yehoshua (already a recognized rabbinic and Hasidic figure, explicitly described as 'כפוף לו מכבר' — already deferential to Rabbi Mordechai) accepting the tzaddik's superior spiritual discernment about the shochet's kashrut; there is no outsider or skeptic toward Hasidism whose narrative arc ends in being drawn close to a tzaddik or to Hasidism.

The story's arc centers on the Besht witnessing and validating the piety of Reb Shabtai (a simple bookbinder) and prophesying the birth of the Kozhnitzer Maggid — there is no outsider or skeptic being drawn close to Hasidism; all characters are already within the Hasidic world.

All participants (R' Ber, R' Pinchas, the Maggid) are already embedded insiders in the Hasidic world; the narrative arc is a miracle of healing and blessing, not an outsider/skeptic being drawn close to a tzaddik.

The messenger is an unwitting visitor who never recognizes the tzaddik until after he leaves, but the story's arc is about the tzaddik's hidden piety and hospitality (hachnasat orchim), not about drawing an outsider/skeptic closer to Hasidism.

The tax collector comes to the Besht already as a petitioner seeking help, not as a skeptic or outsider; the narrative focuses on the Besht expelling the evil spirit (קליפה) through amulets and spiritual power, with no arc of drawing an outsider close to Hasidism.
The narrator is already a disciple/insider who describes his own religious practices and deference to the Rabbi; no outsider or skeptic is drawn toward Hasidism over the course of the story.
The story depicts a tzaddik's mystical prayer practice and self-reflection on his spiritual level, with no outsider or skeptic figure being drawn close to a tzaddik or to Hasidism.

The mother-in-law's brief arc from ignorance/mockery to admiration (ויהי לצחוק בעיניה … נסעה לביתה בחדוה רבה) contains a rapprochement element, but it is a minor introductory frame; the story's primary narrative arc concerns the young wife's prophetic dreams and supernatural guidance from the deceased Maggid across years of widowhood, not an outsider being drawn close to a tzaddik.
The text is entirely a Torah homily (derush) interpreting a Mishnah passage about the Temple and redemption, with no narrative arc, no outsider/skeptic character, and no rapprochement with a tzaddik.
R' Shmeryl is already a devoted follower who regularly hosts and visits the Besht; no outsider or skeptic is drawn close through the story's events.
The text is a homiletical Torah teaching (derasha) with no narrative arc, no outsider or skeptic character, and no depiction of anyone being drawn close to a tzaddik or to Hasidism.

The Torah scholar already knows the Besht and deliberately seeks him out for help; there is no first encounter or discovery of the tzaddik by a neutral character — the narrative centers on the reincarnation (gilgul) revelation, not on finding the tzaddik.

The story centers on an informer's repentance and death; the local rabbi/tzaddik is a peripheral figure who appears only at the end to rule on burial dignity and perform dream-inquiry — there is no narrative of a neutral character discovering or first encountering a tzaddik.

The story's frame narrative explicitly depicts the wealthy man of Risha making his first-ever journey to encounter the Besht in Mezhybizh — he had long heard of the tzaddik but never visited, and the 'finding' of the tzaddik is a central, sustained narrative event, not incidental.
The story depicts R' Gershon visiting his brother-in-law the Baal Shem Tov (already known to him as such), not a neutral character's first discovery/encounter with an unknown tzaddik.

The story explicitly states the Hasid encountered Rabbi Pinchas of Koretz 'before his revelation' (טרם התגלות), and the Hasid — a neutral figure relative to this tzaddik — independently recognizes his holiness through observation, making the discovery of the as-yet-unknown tzaddik a central narrative element.

The merchant is not a neutral character discovering the Besht for the first time — he is explicitly described as an opponent of the Besht (מאותן אנשים שהיו עדיין נגד הבעש״ט), and the story's core is his conversion from skepticism to belief, not a neutral first-encounter/finding of a tzaddik.

While a neutral innkeeper encounters the Besht incognito and mediates his introduction to the gvir, this is merely a framing device; the story's central narrative is the miraculous healing/resurrection of the bride and the legal dispute with the deceased wife's spirit, not the discovery of the tzaddik.

The story's narrative centers on a hidden tzaddik (nistar) being sent to by the Rebbe of Mezhbizh for a communal emergency — the messengers are deliberate emissaries dispatched by an already-known tzaddik, not neutral characters who independently discover a tzaddik for the first time.

The story's central narrative is the Besht's journey to Constantinople and his failed attempt to reach the Land of Israel; the Polish traveler who seeks him out already knows of him by name and the encounter is a subplot device (providential Passover provision), not a 'first discovery' of the tzaddik by a neutral character.

The story focuses on Rabbi Leib Sarahs's miraculous deeds (magical travel to Vienna, supporting hidden tzaddikim); while townspeople come to recognize him, the narrative is not structured around a neutral character's first discovery of a tzaddik, and the episode where Rabbi Leib recognizes young Rabbi Mordechai in Berdichev has Rabbi Leib himself (a tzaddik) as the discoverer, not a neutral party.

The story's narrative centers on R' Leib disowning a protégé who mocked R' Baruch and R' Ephraim; the emergence of those tzaddikim is merely background context, not a first-encounter/discovery by a neutral character.

The story depicts the Besht already established in a community and actively summoning a passerby to reveal his sin — there is no neutral character 'discovering' or 'finding' the tzaddik for the first time.

The story's central narrative is the Rav orchestrating an elaborate shidduch for the avrech, not a neutral character's first discovery of a tzaddik — the avrech already seeks out the Rav from the opening, and the wife's encounters with the elder are a subplot revelation rather than a 'finding' narrative.

The story centers on a prolonged dispute between two tzaddikim and a healing miracle for a woman who already knew the Rebbe; no narrative of a neutral character first discovering/encountering a tzaddik is present.

While the innkeeper initially does not know the Besht and there is a first-encounter moment, the story's central narrative is the magical contest with the sorcerer, not the discovery/finding of the tzaddik — the advent element is merely incidental framing for the main plot.

The story narrates the downfall of two informers (מוסרים) who opposed Hasidic leaders, with an embedded episode of a governor siding with Rabbi Mordechai of Lechowitz — there is no first-encounter 'finding' of a tzaddik by a neutral character.

The story depicts the Besht arriving in town and recognizing a righteous woman (die frume Rivele) — it is the tzaddik who discovers her, not a neutral character discovering the tzaddik for the first time.

The story depicts a known tzaddik (R' Wolf of Zbarazh) being received by community notables during a journey — the wealthy man comes to greet him along with others, not as a neutral discoverer encountering an unknown tzaddik for the first time.

The story is a miracle tale about rescuing a widow's son from forced apostasy through the Maggid of Mezeritch's spiritual intervention; there is no first encounter or 'finding' of a tzaddik by a neutral character.
The story depicts an already-known Besht being summoned to Slutsk by a wealthy woman; there is no neutral character encountering or 'finding' him for the first time.

The story centers on the Besht rescuing a wayward woman at the request of her deceased ancestor — no neutral character discovers or first encounters the tzaddik; the Besht is already a known, established figure throughout.

The tzaddik is already established as the avrech's master from the outset; no neutral character discovers or first encounters the tzaddik — the innkeeper is directed toward him secondhand but never actually meets him.

The Besht is already publicly known and established before the story begins ('כי אז כבר נתגלה הבעש״ט ז"ל בנפלאותיו'), and the scholar approaches him on his wife's advice — there is no 'first encounter/finding' of the tzaddik by a neutral character.

The story is a collection of testimonies about the Besht's trembling during prayer; the passing mention of Elijah coming 'for the first time' to the Maggid of Mezeritch is merely a framing device for relaying the story, not the narrative's central arc of a neutral character discovering a tzaddik.

The story's narrative center is the Rebbe's repeated rebuke of the son-in-law's scholarly pride and his eventual moral lesson, not the discovery or first-finding of a tzaddik by a neutral character — the father-in-law already knows R' Elimelech, and the son-in-law's first visit is merely the setting for a didactic teaching on humility.
The story narrates the Besht's final day and death on Shavuot — it contains no first-encounter or 'finding' of a tzaddik by a neutral character.

The story's core narrative is about a childless Hasid who encounters R. Pinchas of Koretz at a fair, but this encounter is incidental to the main plot (undoing a vow of barrenness); the story is not structured around the 'first finding/discovery' of the tzaddik as its central theme.

The story's main narrative is a healing/exorcism miracle attributed to R' Aharon of Staroshelye — a common man goes to an already-known tzaddik for relief from demonic affliction; while this involves a first visit to the rebbe, the narrative focus is the miracle itself, not the discovery/finding of the tzaddik by a neutral character.
The story centers on a disciple's disgrace for mocking R' Baruch and R' Efraim, not on any character's first discovery/encounter with a tzaddik.

The story depicts the conversion of an active opponent/mocker of the tzaddik (the Lublin nagid), not a neutral character's first discovery of him — the nagid already knows of the rebbe and has been blaspheming him, and the narrative centers on a wager-test and his subsequent repentance rather than an initial 'finding' of the tzaddik.

The story centers on the Besht's ecstatic prayer and trembling, with the single mention of a 'first visit' to the Maggid of Mezritch serving only as a transmission frame, not as the narrative's central event of discovering a tzaddik.

The story depicts Rabbi David of Mikliev, already an established disciple of the Besht making regular pilgrimages to him; there is no first/neutral encounter with a tzaddik — the 'recognition' scene in Slonim is an unmasking of a disguised, already-famous tzaddik, not a discovery of one.

The avrech already knows and repeatedly seeks the Rabbi (Reb Leib Sarah's) from the outset; the story's central narrative is the tzaddik orchestrating a shidduch, not a neutral character's first discovery of a tzaddik.

The story depicts an already-known and famous Baal Shem Tov being sought out by a wealthy man (not a neutral character discovering an unknown tzaddik); the narrative is about a miraculous healing, not a first encounter/finding of the tzaddik.
The story is a hagiographic account of R. Ezra's pious practices and his posthumous dream-appearances; no narrative of a neutral character discovering or first encountering a tzaddik is present.

The story's central narrative is a reunion between R' Shimshon and his lost first wife (found among the poor at a charity feast), not a neutral character's first discovery of the tzaddik; the matchmaking encounters with the innkeeper and the Viennese magnate are incidental framing, not the narrative focus.
The story presupposes an established relationship — the merchant already knows to send a letter to the Besht; there is no first encounter or discovery of the tzaddik by a neutral character.

The entire narrative arc is a first-encounter/discovery story: the skeptical melamed (neutral character who did not respect the Besht) experiences the Besht's supernatural knowledge through a dream and the third Sabbath meal, recognizes him as a true tzaddik, and draws close to him — a textbook 'advent' structure.

The story recounts the political denunciation and arrest of Rabbi Shneur Zalman by Avigdor before Tsar Paul — a persecution narrative with no depiction of a neutral character discovering or first encountering a tzaddik.

The story depicts a visit by Rabbi David Purkes — already a known associate in the Besht's own community — to the ailing Besht before his death; there is no first-encounter or 'finding' of the tzaddik by a neutral character.
The text is a reproduced letter by Moshe Yehuda Leib of Sassov on the themes of humility and communal peace — there is no narrative of a neutral character discovering or first encountering a tzaddik.
The story recounts R' Gershon's near-drowning during a ritual immersion at sea and his miraculous rescue — it is not a first-encounter narrative in which a neutral character discovers a tzaddik.

The story depicts a dispute between R"G and a local sage in Tzfat over coins and a false oath, with the Besht intervening via letter — there is no first-encounter or 'discovery' of a tzaddik by a neutral character.
The story recounts a shofar-blowing substitution and Levi Yitzchak's mystical reaction; there is no first encounter or discovery of a tzaddik by a neutral character.
The story depicts an ongoing dispute between two established tzaddikim (the Rav and R' Baruch of Tulchin) with no scene of a neutral character first discovering or encountering a tzaddik.

The text is a homiletical Torah teaching (derush) attributed to Rabbi Menachem Nachum of Makarov with no narrative whatsoever, let alone a scene of a neutral character discovering or first encountering a tzaddik.

The dying person is a disciple/student, not a tzaddik; the Besht visits him as a healer and intercessor, so the central narrative is about the Besht's supernatural powers and the student's afterlife journey — not the death of a tzaddik.
The story depicts an ecstatic prayer episode by R' Moshe during Rosh Hashana davening, with no deathbed scene, last teachings, or succession narrative.

The story centers on the Besht diagnosing a sin (striking one's father) and prescribing repentance; the death mentioned is a consequence of abandoning that repentance, not a deathbed scene, last teachings, or succession narrative.

The story is a teaching about true self-sacrifice (mesirat nefesh), narrated by R. Menachem Mendel of Lubavitch with an embedded anecdote about R. Baruch of Mezbizh; there is no deathbed scene, last teachings in a dying context, or succession narrative.

The story depicts the Besht interceding in a communal dispute and a preacher secretly convening a minyan to nullify judgments — there is no death scene, deathbed, last teachings, or succession of a tzaddik.
The story depicts the rabbi of Shargorod's encounter with the Besht, his expulsion from town, and his subsequent repentance — there is no deathbed scene, last teachings, or succession narrative.
The story depicts the Besht healing a gravely ill man by driving away the Angel of Death — there is no deathbed scene, last teachings, or succession of the tzaddik himself.
The story centers on the Besht's grief and mystical intercession over martyred Jews, not on his own death, deathbed scene, last teachings, or succession.

The story depicts a living Besht teaching and then erasing mystical knowledge of animal speech from a preacher during a journey — there is no deathbed scene, last teachings at death, or succession narrative.
The story centers on the Besht's clairvoyance and a sinner's failed repentance leading to his own early death — not a deathbed scene, last teachings, or succession of a tzaddik.

The story depicts a healing/honor episode — the Besht corrects a rabbi's spiritual flaw and is subsequently seated at the head of the feast — with no deathbed scene, last teachings, or succession narrative.
The story depicts the Besht being summoned before the Council of Four Lands and demonstrating his spiritual powers, with no deathbed scene, last teachings, or succession narrative.

The story centers on a Torah scholar's visit to the Besht for spiritual guidance and the revelation of his past gilgul (reincarnation), with no deathbed scene, last teachings, or succession narrative.

The story centers on a childless Hasid, competing oaths between the Shpola Zeyde and R. Pinchas of Koritz, and the miraculous birth of a son — there is no deathbed scene, last teachings, or succession narrative.
The story centers on the Besht's grief and mystical vision upon learning of Jewish martyrs' deaths, not on the tzaddik's own death, deathbed scene, last teachings, or succession.
The story centers on a dream/vision of a deceased Hasid's soul being offered on a heavenly altar, not on the death or deathbed scene of a tzaddik.

The story depicts a Shabbat encounter between R' Pinchas of Korets, his skeptical father R' Abraham Abba, and the Besht; the Gan Eden reference is a dream vision, not a death scene — there is no deathbed, last teachings, or succession narrative.
The story depicts the Besht alive and actively performing mystical prayer (raising souls of the dead), with no deathbed scene, last teachings, or succession narrative.
The story depicts the Besht healing R. Leibish and driving away the Angel of Death — a miraculous cure narrative, not the death or deathbed scene of a tzaddik.

The story depicts the Besht healing a gravely ill person (R. Yosef) by ordering bloodletting over a doctor's objection — a healing miracle narrative — with no death, deathbed scene, last teachings, or succession.

The story centers on Rabbi Chaim ben Betzalel's secret marriage and humility, culminating in a revelation about Elijah the Prophet visiting him — there is no deathbed scene, last teachings, or succession narrative.

The dying figure is an unnamed student described incidentally as 'ish tov v'tsaddik,' not a prominent tzaddik whose death is the central event; the story's actual focus is validating the Besht's spiritual authority through his foreknowledge of heavenly matters and the posthumous dream-revelation, with no deathbed teachings or succession.
The story depicts the Besht's mystical prayer practice of elevating souls of the dead, not a deathbed scene, last teachings, or succession narrative.
The story depicts the Besht traveling with disciples, attending a feast, and performing a private spiritual healing for a rabbi — there is no deathbed scene, last teachings, or succession narrative.
The story centers on the Baal Shem Tov getting lost in the forest and spending Shabbat with a crude hidden tzaddik as spiritual atonement; no deathbed scene, last teachings, or succession occurs.

The story depicts the Besht resolving a spiritual obstruction before Kol Nidre by engineering a nocturnal emission in an old priest; there is no deathbed scene, last teachings, or succession narrative.

The story depicts a nocturnal visit by the tzaddik to his study house to perform a spiritual intervention (sweetening divine judgments) — there is no deathbed scene, last teachings, or succession narrative.

The story centers entirely on the Besht's ecstatic prayer experiences (trembling, divine presence, defeating the Adversary on Yom Kippur) — there is no deathbed scene, last teachings, or succession narrative.

The story consists entirely of accounts of the Besht's intense, trembling prayer experiences; the one mention of 'like a dying person' (כמו גוסס ח"ו) is a simile for his ecstatic state during prayer, not an actual deathbed scene, final teachings, or succession narrative.
The story recounts a travel episode with the Baal Shem Tov involving charity and healing, with no deathbed scene, last teachings, or succession narrative.

The story centers on a blood-libel accusation and the Besht's miraculous intercession to protect the community; the death of a Torah scholar (R' Chaim of Brod) is mentioned only incidentally as the reason the Besht was not forewarned, not as the narrative's central subject.

The story depicts a Shabbat encounter between R' Pinchas of Koritz, the Maggid of Mezeritch, and the Besht (including R' Pinchas's skeptical father), with no deathbed scene, last teachings, or succession narrative anywhere in the text.

The story centers on an ordinary layman's late-life repentance and post-mortem appearances to R' Yitzchak, not on the death of a tzaddik — the dying figure is not a tzaddik, and the deathbed scene is incidental rather than the narrative's focus.
The story centers on the Maggid's illness and his meeting and spiritual apprenticeship with the Besht, not on any tzaddik's death, deathbed scene, or succession.

The story centers on Rabbi Zusha's overwhelming God-fearing experience and his prayer to have it removed; the near-death moment is incidental and he survives, with no deathbed scene, final teachings, or succession.
The story depicts the Besht being summoned before the Council of Four Lands and demonstrating his spiritual powers; there is no deathbed scene, last teachings, or succession narrative.
The story depicts the hidden life and public revelation of the Besht as a recognized tzaddik, with no deathbed scene, last teachings, or succession narrative.
The story centers on the pious woman Rivele and her interactions with the Besht, with no deathbed scene, last teachings, or succession narrative involving a tzaddik's death.

The death of R' Eliezer of Amsterdam is incidental background information demonstrating the Besht's clairvoyance — there is no deathbed scene, last teachings, or succession narrative; the central focus is the Besht's visionary powers and warnings about a heretical book and Shabbtai Tzvi.

The story depicts R' Leib disowning his foster son for mocking other tzaddikim, with no deathbed scene, last teachings, or succession narrative — it ends with a social/moral rupture, not the tzaddik's death.
The story depicts the Maggid's illness and his miraculous healing by the Besht, culminating in a mystical transmission of Torah — there is no deathbed scene, final teachings, or succession narrative.
The story depicts the Besht miraculously becoming invisible to a hostile nobleman; there is no deathbed scene, last teachings, or succession narrative.

The story centers on the death of a sick lay follower, not the tzaddik: Rabbi Mordechai of Chernobyl remains alive throughout, and the narrative's focus is on how the tzaddik's Kabbalat Shabbat prayer aids the deceased follower's soul in the afterlife.

The story centers on the pious woman Rivele and her virtuous deeds, her encounter with the Besht, and her interaction with her sons — there is no deathbed scene, last teachings, or succession narrative involving a tzaddik.
The story depicts the Besht's mystical Yom Kippur intercession (heavenly ascent, nullifying a decree against Israel) — there is no deathbed scene, last teachings, or succession narrative.

The story depicts Rabbi Leib severing his adoptive relationship with a young man who mocked other tzaddikim; though set 'at the end of his days,' there is no deathbed scene, last teachings, or succession narrative.
The story depicts the Besht healing a gravely ill man (R' Yosef) who recovers after bloodletting — there is no deathbed scene, last teachings, or succession of a tzaddik.
The story centers on the Besht healing a sick mitnaged who recovers fully; there is no death of a tzaddik, no deathbed scene, no last teachings, and no succession.

The story depicts the Besht neutralizing a heavenly accusation before Kol Nidre prayers by provoking a nocturnal emission in an old priest — there is no deathbed scene, last teachings, or succession narrative.

The story centers on a traveling tzaddik collecting ransom funds and an encounter with a wise elder in a distant land, culminating in a mystical three-day communion — the death of a tzaddik is entirely absent.

The Besht is alive and active throughout — lying in bed while Ein Yaakov is read to him, giving teachings, and explaining a vision of a deceased maggid (R' Yosef); the central narrative is a supernatural apparition/vision episode, not a deathbed scene, final teachings, or succession of the Besht himself.

The text is the Besht's famous epistle (Iggeret ha-Aliyah) describing a mystical soul-ascent vision, not a deathbed scene, last teachings, or succession narrative — the Besht even explicitly notes he was told he would not yet die.

The story centers on the Besht's Yom Kippur mystical prayer journey and his battle against a heavenly decree threatening Jewish learning — there is no deathbed scene, last teachings, or succession narrative.
The story centers on a childless Hasid, competing oaths between two tzaddikim, and the birth of a son — no deathbed scene, last teachings, or succession narrative appears.

The story depicts encounters with the living Besht — a sick visitor healed, a Shabbat prayer observed, and the Besht explaining his anger at his servant as a spiritual intervention; there is no deathbed scene, last teachings, or succession narrative.
The story depicts a mystical confrontation between the Besht and the Samael to retrieve a hidden secret, with no deathbed scene, last teachings, or succession narrative.

The story centers on R' Adam's transmission of mystical writings to the Besht and the Besht's early life; R' Adam's death is mentioned only briefly and incidentally as a plot trigger for the transmission, not as a deathbed scene with last teachings or succession drama.

The story describes the Baal Shem Tov's early spiritual development and how his teacher taught him to recite Psalms to maintain communication with people without losing his devekut — there is no deathbed scene, final teachings in the context of death, or succession narrative.
The story depicts the Besht neutralizing a murderous spirit at a dangerous forest lodging; there is no deathbed scene, last teachings, or succession narrative.

The story is a Torah teaching (homily on a Talmudic passage about suffering) attributed to Rabbi Shalom of Porivtsch; there is no deathbed scene, last teachings in a dying context, or succession narrative — the 'זי״ע' is merely a standard posthumous honorific.
The story depicts a tzaddik judging a group of card-players favorably, with no deathbed scene, last teachings, or succession narrative.

The story centers on the Besht's refusal to heal a sick woman and his deflection of harsh judgments from a sorcerer — there is no death of a tzaddik, deathbed scene, last teachings, or succession motif.
The story depicts the early miraculous healings of the Besht (two young women cured of madness), with no deathbed scene, last teachings, or succession narrative.

The story depicts the tzaddik's imprisonment and interrogation by imperial officials, focusing on his steadfast defense of Jewish law and his petition to the Emperor to present a written defense — there is no deathbed scene, last teachings, or succession narrative.

The story depicts a confrontational visit between Rabbi Nachman of Kosov and the Besht culminating in a demonstration of thought-reading and reconciliation — no deathbed scene, final teachings, or succession narrative is present.

The story depicts a rabbinic legal ruling and its miraculous consequence (a litigant's descendant dies as punishment for false testimony), not a deathbed scene, last teachings, or succession of a tzaddik.
The story depicts the Besht demonstrating superior healing knowledge and exposing a doctor as a thief before a duchess — no death, deathbed scene, last teachings, or succession is present.

The story's climax is the explicit revelation by the woman that the crude, boorish householder is in fact 'צדיק גדול מוסתר במעשיו' (a great hidden tzaddik), disclosed to the Besht and his disciples — making giluy of a nistar the narrative's central turning point.

The story's central narrative concerns the author's wandering in search of livelihood, his discovery of a pious innkeeper devoted to hakhnasat orhim, and the innkeeper's son born of a blessing — there is no giluy (revelation) of a hidden tzaddik to others or himself driving the plot, even though the brother possesses ruach hakodesh and the innkeeper displays exceptional piety.

The story's central narrative is a double revelation: first, a hidden tzaddik (R' Moshe Shtagel) disguised as a drunkard and buffoon is revealed to a disciple sent by the Kozhnitzer Maggid; second, the Rizhiner Rebbe is revealed as the tzaddik of the generation when he correctly identifies the source of the Torah teachings — both revelations are structural and explicit, not incidental.

The story's central narrative is precisely the giluy of the hidden Besht to R' David the maggid: the Besht lives incognito as a simple villager, is involuntarily revealed through the miraculous light (אור גדול) shining upon him at midnight, and then explicitly reveals himself ('גלה א״ע לפניו') when R' David compels him, after which R' David becomes his disciple.

The entire narrative is structured around the concealment and successive revelations of the hidden tzaddik Israel ben Eliezer (the Besht): R' Adam's son discovers and reveals him as the destined recipient of the secret writings, the Besht deliberately hides his true nature while living as a servant and later as a disguised simple man, and the bandits ultimately recognize him as an ish Elohim — making giluy of a nistar the story's central organizing theme.

The story's climax is the revelation of the wealthy man's secret identity as the former bishop-turned-penitent, but he is framed as a baal teshuva (penitent) with a holy soul, not as a hidden tzaddik (nistar), so the giluy here does not fit the story-type's core meaning.

The story's central narrative is the revelation of the hidden tzaddik Rabbi Chaim: he concealed his true identity and spiritual practice for two years, and the climax is his unmasking before the Rema followed by Elijah the Prophet's explicit disclosure that he regularly reveals himself (מתגלים) to Rabbi Chaim precisely because of his hidden, humble conduct.
The story's central revelation is of the wealthy man's sinful past (gilgul guilt) exposed by the Besht — not the disclosure of a hidden tzaddik to others or to himself.

The story depicts the Besht's supernatural perception of a hidden sin (striking one's father), not the revelation of a hidden tzaddik — the Besht is already known and recognized as 'the Rav' throughout the narrative.

The story's central narrative is the revelation of R' Chaim as a hidden tzaddik — he deliberately conceals himself (living secretly in a baker's home, studying in anonymity for two years), and his true greatness is revealed to Rabbeinu (the Rema) first when he is dragged before the rabbinic court, and then definitively when Rabbeinu visits at night and discovers Elijah the Prophet learning with him, with Elijah explicitly explaining that he reveals himself only to humble, concealed righteous men.

The story depicts the Besht's pre-revelation nistar (concealment) period — receiving manuscripts, disguising himself as an ignoramus, and living in isolation — with only incidental partial recognitions (by R' Adam's son, the bandits), none of which constitute a central giluy narrative.

The story's central narrative concerns R' Shimshon's separation from his first wife at a wedding fire, his ingenious scheme to locate her among charity recipients, and his halakhic resolution of the inadvertent bigamy — not the revelation (giluy) of a hidden tzaddik to others or to himself.

The story's central narrative is the gradual and then formal public revelation (hitgalut) of the Besht as a hidden tzaddik — explicitly marked by the phrase 'כשבא העת סמוך להתגלותו' — culminating in the community recognizing and enthroning him as their leader.

The story reveals the scope of the Besht's mystical prayer-practice (elevating souls) to Rabbi Gershon, but the Besht is not a hidden tzaddik being unmasked — he is already known and named; the narrative centers on demonstrating his extraordinary spiritual capacity, not on a giluy of a nistar.

The story's central arc is the discovery and revelation of the hidden tzaddik (the Maggid of Mezeritch): R' Mendel recognizes him as a 'beautiful vessel,' reports him to the Besht who declares long-hidden longing for him, and the Maggid's true stature is confirmed through a dramatic mystical transmission (Torah received 'in thunder and lightning') and culminates in the Besht bowing his own head for the Maggid's blessing.

The Besht is already known by name and was deliberately summoned; the narrative centers on demonstrating his powers to skeptical townspeople, not on uncovering a concealed (nistar) identity — there is no 'hidden tzaddik' whose identity is revealed.

The narrative's central revelation is that the Torah scholar is the reincarnation of a wicked man — not the giluy of a hidden tzaddik; the Besht himself is explicitly already revealed at the story's outset.

The story is a skeptic-conversion tale in which the Besht's clairvoyance (reading the Zohar to locate the missing son) convinces a doubter of his powers; the Besht is already a publicly known tzaddik and the narrative is not structured around uncovering or disclosing a hidden/unknown tzaddik.

The story's central narrative is precisely the revelation of the Maggid (Dov Ber) as a hidden tzaddik — R' Mendel identifies him to the Besht as 'a beautiful vessel,' the Besht declares he has longed for him for years, and the climactic nocturnal Kabbalah session seals the Maggid's recognition and initiation as a tzaddik of the highest order.

The Besht does reveal his identity to the father-in-law mid-story ('know that I am the Besht'), but this disclosure is a functional plot device to justify taking the woman — the narrative's core is the miraculous rescue of a woman from apostasy and her subsequent repentance, not the giluy of a hidden tzaddik.

The story's central narrative is the Besht revealing his hidden spiritual practice — elevating thousands of souls during prayer — to Rabbi Gershon of Kitov, who had no knowledge of it; this is a direct giluy of the tzaddik's concealed powers to another.

The story's central narrative is a demonstration of perfect trust in God (bitachon) by a village innkeeper; although the Besht is sent from heaven to learn from him (implying hidden worth), no explicit revelation of a hidden tzaddik is enacted — the innkeeper's identity is never disclosed or 'revealed' as the plot's focus.

The story centers on the hidden repentance and righteous acts of a former informer (מסור/בעל תשובה), not on the revelation of a hidden tzaddik — the rabbi is already known as a tzaddik, and the revealed figure is a penitent sinner, not a concealed righteous person (nistar).

The story presents a collection of the Besht's clairvoyant deeds and spiritual battles (knowing of R' Eliezer's death, identifying a heretical book, combating Shabtai Tzvi's spirit), but at no point does the narrative turn on the revelation (giluy) of a hidden tzaddik — the Besht is an already-known figure throughout, and no character is unveiled as a concealed tzaddik.

The emergence of R' Baruch and R' Ephraim as public figures (התפרסמות) serves only as the inciting trigger for the main narrative, which centers on R' Leib's severing of his bond with the orphan who mocked them — not on the revelation of a hidden tzaddik as the story's driving arc.

The story's central narrative is about seeking and transmitting (then erasing) mystical knowledge of animal speech — the Besht is already a known and beloved figure to the preacher, not a hidden tzaddik being revealed; the brief glimpse of the Besht's fiery face during his unifications is incidental, not the constitutive narrative event of giluy.

The story explicitly states the events occurred 'טרם התגלות הרב הקדוש' (before the revelation of R' Pinchas of Koritz), and its central narrative is a hasid recognizing and 'uncovering' the hidden tzaddik R' Pinchas through his conduct — a textbook giluy of a nistar.

R. Ezra's saintliness is openly known throughout the story; the narrative centers on post-mortem heavenly judgment and a morality lesson about proper prayer pronunciation, not on the giluy (revelation) of a hidden tzaddik.

The story is primarily a birth-origin narrative for the Besht; while the father functions as a hidden tzaddik whose supernatural abilities are glimpsed by the king, he is not formally 'revealed' as a tzaddik, and the Besht himself ends the story still concealed (the nistar phase), with no revelation of his identity taking place within the narrative.

The narrative climax is the wealthy man's revelation of his hidden identity as the bishop who did teshuvah through the Besht — a concealed penitent/righteous figure living anonymously whose true spiritual story is disclosed to R' Yaakov, fitting the giluy-of-a-hidden-tzaddik pattern, though he is not a classic nistar but a ba'al teshuvah whose identity was concealed.

The Besht is already a publicly known tzaddik; his disclosing his identity to strangers in a distant city is a plot device to accomplish a soul-rescue mission, not a narrative whose central arc is the revelation (giluy) of a hidden tzaddik.

The entire narrative culminates in Reb Baruch revealing to his messengers that the seemingly worthless drunkard is in fact a hidden tzaddik whose decrees are upheld by the heavenly court — a textbook giluy of a nistar.

The story is a composite hagiographic account of R' Aharon of Strashelye that includes his early recognition, a healing/exorcism narrative, and his death — the revelation motifs (e.g., 'התחיל להראות זרוע קדשו') are framing elements, but the primary narrative structure is a wonder-working/healing story, not the unfolding revelation of a hidden tzaddik as the central plot.

The story's climax is the revelation of the hidden tzaddik — the wife discloses to her husband that the old man who just visited is in fact the tzaddik R' Leib Sharas, whose covert orchestration of their match (through visions, illness-threats, and financial manipulation) is only then fully disclosed to both spouses.
The story centers on a miraculous financial rescue orchestrated by the Besht at a wedding, demonstrating his foreknowledge and powers, not on the revelation of a hidden tzaddik to others or himself.

The story's narrative centers on the Besht's fear of Shabbat desecration, a rabbinic disputation, and the Besht stripping R' Michl the dayan of his Torah knowledge to rebuke misuse of learning — R' Michl's resulting repentance and becoming a 'tzaddik gamur' is a transformation/teshuvah arc, not the revelation (giluy) of a previously hidden tzaddik.

The story centers on the Besht's Passover ordeal in Constantinople, his blessing of a childless couple, his self-sacrifice for divine service, and his sea adventure — the fact that he was unrecognized there is incidental background, not a narrative of revealing a hidden tzaddik.

The story's central narrative is the Besht perceiving a divine light (אור) on the pious woman Rivele and revealing her hidden spiritual superiority to the townspeople — a classic giluy of a concealed righteous figure — though the subject is a woman rather than a conventionally termed male tzaddik.

The phrase 'כשנתפרסם הבעש״ט' ('when the Besht became known') serves only as a temporal marker framing a later scene; the story's actual narrative centers on the Besht defeating a witch's demon through prayer, not on the act or process of his revelation to others or himself.
The story depicts the Besht supernaturally discerning a man's hidden sin (striking his father), not the revelation of a hidden or unknown tzaddik to others or to himself.

The story's central narrative is the preacher's (mokhiach's) mission journey to Hamburg under the Besht's instructions; while the rabbi incidentally mentions a dream-vision of the Besht and a prophecy about 'ישראל בן המילדת,' these are supporting details rather than the primary narrative of revealing a hidden tzaddik.

The narrative's central arc is the gradual revelation of the mysterious forest host as Yonah ben Amitai (a hidden supernatural tzaddik-figure): his identity is concealed throughout the Shabbat stay, the merchant's failed inquiries at the Leipzig fair, and finally disclosed only at the city gate when Yonah pays the debt and asks to convey greetings to the Besht — confirming his status as a hidden holy figure known to the Besht.
