The story centers on the Besht's miraculous foreknowledge and financial dealings surrounding a wedding, with no narrative of a hidden tzaddik being revealed to others or to himself.

The story is a heavenly-court / near-death journey narrative (aliyat neshamah) in which the Besht and other known tzaddikim intercede before the celestial tribunal; no hidden tzaddik is revealed to others or to himself.

The story's central narrative concerns an orphan who mocks the emerging fame of R' Boruch and R' Ephraim and is subsequently rejected by R' Leib; the 'becoming known' of those tzaddikim is only incidental background context, not the narrative focus of a giluy.

The entire narrative arc drives toward the revelation of the Besht's hidden tzaddik status to doubting disciples (R' Zeev Kutzes and R' David Purkes), who recognize his greatness only after the deceased's posthumous dream-vision confirms the Besht's place in the heavenly academy — classically ending with 'from that day forward they drew close to him.'

The entire narrative is structured around the revelation of a hidden tzaddik: emissaries discover an apparently base drunkard who turns out to possess divine decree-power, and R. Baruch's closing explanation explicitly unveils the man's concealed righteous status and the heavenly reason for his enforced hiddenness.

The story is explicitly framed as a 'hitgalut' (התגלות) of the holy tzaddik of Lublin to a skeptical wealthy man, whose central dramatic arc is the tzaddik revealing his hidden supernatural knowledge of the nagid's sins and the secret agreement with the Hasid, thereby disclosing himself as a true tzaddik to a former scoffer.

The story's climax is the revelation of the Besht's true stature to the skeptical father (אברהם אבא), who declares 'הוא ולא אחר' after experiencing in a dream what he then hears from the Besht's mouth — a paradigmatic giluy of the hidden tzaddik to a doubter.

The story depicts a conflict between R' Yitzchak (the Mokhiakh) and a dayan, culminating in the miraculous detection of invalid tefillin — a display of supernatural perception, but not a narrative about revealing a previously hidden tzaddik to others or to himself.

The Besht is already a known tzaddik throughout the story; the narrative centers on neutralizing an evil spirit and exposing a servant's murderous intent, with no moment of a hidden tzaddik being revealed to others or himself.

The narrative centers on the imprisoned Rebbe displaying extraordinary wisdom and cunning before his interrogators (including Tsar Pavel); the tzaddik is already publicly known and the story has no giluy arc of revealing a hidden or unrecognized tzaddik to others or himself.
The story is an omen/sign anecdote about the Besht interpreting soiled tzitzit as indicating R. Moshe of Shatnave's death; there is no revelation of a hidden tzaddik.

The story's entire arc turns on the Besht being initially unrecognized and disrespected, then revealing himself as a hidden tzaddik by exposing the visiting rabbi's secret spiritual flaw, after which the rabbi publicly honors him at the head of the table — a classic giluy of the hidden tzaddik to others.

The story explicitly concerns a hidden tzaddik (הנחבאים ונסתרים) who acts to save his community but remains unrevealed — the narrator concludes that 'he was not made famous nor was he revealed as a wondrous man' (ולא נתפרסם גם למן הוא והלאה לאיש פלאי), making this a story of hidden saintly action rather than a giluy narrative.

The story depicts a known tzaddik (the Rebbe of Sasov) performing transcendent dancing witnessed by Mahar"P, but the narrative centers on the spiritual power of his dance rather than on unveiling a hidden or unrecognized tzaddik to others or himself.

The narrative centers on the Besht's revelation of R' Shimshon's hidden greatness to the assembled company — he sees a pillar of Torah light from R' Shimshon's study-house reaching from earth to heaven and declares it, making the hidden tzaddik's stature manifest to others.
The story covers Rabbi Adam's mystical writings, the Besht's marriage and early career, and various miracles, but contains no depiction of a tzaddik's birth or miraculous birth circumstances.
The story concerns Reb Adam's transmission of mystical writings to the Baal Shem Tov and the BeSHT's early hidden life, not the miraculous circumstances of his birth.

The story concerns a childless merchant blessed by the Besht who fathers a son, but the child is not presented as a tzaddik, and the miraculous birth is incidental to the main narrative (the mysterious merchant Yonah and the son's later transformation into a Torah scholar).
The story centers on a hidden tzaddik (Moshe Shtagel) and the search for a spiritual guide after a rebbe's death — there is no depiction of a tzaddik's birth or miraculous birth circumstances.
The story concerns the separation and reunion of a tzaddik with his first wife after a fire and a subsequent marriage, with no depiction of a miraculous birth.

The story is about Rabbi Chaim ben Betzalel's secret marriage and his study sessions with Elijah the Prophet, centered on the theme of humility — it contains no depiction of a tzaddik's birth or miraculous birth circumstances.

The story is entirely about reincarnation (gilgulim) and soul rectification (tikkun) — a young man dies choking at his wedding and the Ari explains the transmigration chain; there is no depiction of a tzaddik's birth.
The story depicts the near-death experience and heavenly journey of R' Michel of Zlotchov's young son, not the birth of a tzaddik or any miraculous birth circumstances.
The story concerns the spiritual journey and recruitment activities of R. Israel Palitsky and his companion, not the miraculous circumstances of any tzaddik's birth.
The story describes a tzaddik's mystical practices (playing with infants to perform yichudim, praying for rain, sacred dancing) with no mention of birth circumstances.
The story concerns R' David of Mikliev's spiritual trial, exile, and vindication — there is no depiction of a tzaddik's birth or miraculous birth circumstances.
The story depicts the Besht's early life activity (praying for rain, combating a witch and her demon) but contains no account of his birth or miraculous birth circumstances.
The story depicts the Besht exorcising two harmful spirits (mazzikim) from a synagogue and protecting children — there is no depiction of a tzaddik's birth.

The story concerns the miraculous reversal of fortune of a Jewish innkeeper under the protection of Rabbi Elimelech of Lizhensk, with no depiction of a tzaddik's birth or miraculous birth circumstances.
The story is a first-person eyewitness account of the Besht healing Rabbi Leibsh by confronting the Angel of Death — a biographical miracle narrative with no parable/mashal structure whatsoever.
The story is a biographical narrative about the Besht's early life and deeds — it contains no parable/mashal told by a tzaddik.
The text is a halakhic ruling with a gematria-based rationale given by R. Pinchas of Koritz, not a parable or mashal with a narrative structure.
The story is a wonder-tale about R' Yitzchak identifying invalid tefillin, not a parable or mashal told by a tzaddik.
The story is a brief testimonial anecdote about R' Moshe the cantor performing a circumcision with a radiant face — it contains no parable/mashal structure told by a tzaddik.

The story is a narrative account of events involving a tzaddik and his disciple; the tzaddik offers an explanatory speech at the end but tells no parable/mashal — there is no embedded mashal structure anywhere in the text.

The text is a maggid's homiletical discourse containing nested cautionary exempla (the Spanish chassid, R' Mordechai Yafe), not a formally structured mashal/parable with the characteristic nimshal comparison frame told by a tzaddik.
The story is a first-person account of an illness and heavenly trial experienced by the Besht, not a parable or mashal told by a tzaddik.

The story is a narrative ABOUT the tzaddikim (Zusha, the Maggid, the Horowitz brothers), not structured as a parable told by a tzaddik; the Maggid's closing candle-metaphor is an embedded saying within the narrative, not the structural frame of the story.
The story is a narrative account of a meeting between Rabbi Nachman of Kosov and the Besht — no parable or mashal is told by a tzaddik within it.

The story's climactic central event is the Besht's public revelation (התגלותו) and formal inauguration as rebbe: the community goes out to meet him, fashions a throne of tree branches in the forest, seats him upon it, and accepts him as their leader ('וקובלו אותו לרב עליהם').
The story centers on a reincarnation tale told by the Baal Shem Tov about two friends and their karmic debt across lifetimes; no tzaddik's inauguration as rebbe is depicted or even mentioned.

The story's central event is the Rav solving a halachic puzzle about a woman's ritual purity (dove's blood on a bedsheet), not his inauguration as rebbe; the brief mention that the Maggid tells him to return in a year is incidental background, not the story's focus.
The story centers on the tzaddik's death, final days, and the miraculous events surrounding his passing — not his inauguration as rebbe.

The story centers on a haunted house and demonic spirit (לץ), with the Besht's intervention serving as an exorcism; the closing recognition of the Besht as a tzaddik is incidental, not a formal inauguration as rebbe.
The passage is a homiletical teaching about charity and lovingkindness, with no depiction of a tzaddik's inauguration as rebbe.
The story depicts the Besht healing a gravely ill man by repelling the Angel of Death on Rosh Hashana eve — there is no inauguration of a tzaddik as rebbe anywhere in the narrative.

The story's central events are Rabbi Shneur Zalman's arrest, imprisonment in St. Petersburg, and the surrounding political intrigue; his assuming the rebbe's seat after the Maggid's death is mentioned only in a single incidental transitional sentence, not as the story's focal event.

The story centers on R' Pinchas of Koretz and the Maggid seeking the Besht's help for a captive, and on R' Pinchas's skeptical father being won over by the Besht's Torah — no tzaddik's inauguration as rebbe is depicted.
The story depicts R' Nachman of Horodenka's experience at the Besht's funeral, not a tzaddik's inauguration as rebbe.
The story depicts the Besht rebuking a rabbi by revealing his hidden sin, with no inauguration of a tzaddik as rebbe.
The story is a hagiographic biographical narrative about the Besht's father's captivity and miraculous deeds, and the Besht's own miraculous childhood — it contains no witticism or joke at its core.
The story describes how the Besht's teacher taught him to recite Psalms daily to maintain speech with people without breaking his devekut — there is no witticism or joke present.
The story is a moralistic dream-vision about the heavenly prosecution of a tax collector who cheated gentiles; it contains no witticism or joke by a tzaddik.
The story is a serious mystical narrative about the Besht visiting a dying man, promising him entry to Paradise, and a post-mortem dream revelation — it contains no witticism or joke.

The story recounts spiritual encounters, discipleship, communal conflict, and repentance; the Besht's sharp rebuke to the Mochiach ('Fool! You too hear heavenly proclamations?') is a reprimand, not a witticism or joke that is the point of the story.
The story is a miracle/power narrative about the Besht spiritually overcoming a hostile sorcerer-priest; it contains no witticism or joke.
The story is a serious mystical narrative about the Besht claiming to be a reincarnation of Saadia Gaon and delivering a solemn, trembling sermon — no joke or witticism is present.

The story is a wonder-tale explaining the Besht's seemingly angry behavior toward his servant as a hidden spiritual act to save a Jew from bandits — there is no witticism, punchline, or joke at its core.

The story is a serious reincarnation (גלגולים) narrative about a tax collector executed for destroying an idol whose soul is revealed to be that of Manasseh undergoing tikkun — there is no joke or witticism anywhere in the text.

The story is a moral/mussar narrative about the Besht stripping a disputatious judge of his Torah knowledge to teach him proper motivation for learning — there is no witticism or joke as the story's central point.

The story explicitly states the Hasid perceived that R. Pinchas possessed ruach hakodesh ("וירא כי בעל רוח הקודש הוא"), and the Shpoler Zeyde demonstrates prophetic foreknowledge when he warns the Hasid unprompted that "your end will be bitter" — both are direct, central depictions of supernatural perception.

The Rizhiner (R' Yisrael of Ruzhin) recounts in detail the deliberations of the heavenly court (פמליא של מעלה) — who argued what, how the verdict was reached — knowledge of celestial proceedings he could not possess through normal means, constituting a clear instance of ruach hakodesh/supernatural knowing.

R' Leib's declaration that the Zohar's 'guardians of the covenant' refers to Reb Boruch and Reb Efraim implies spiritual discernment, but the story does not explicitly depict a supernatural perception event (vision, ruach hakodesh, knowing the unknowable); the focus is on moral rebuke for mocking true tzadikim.

R' Leib explicitly testifies ('אני מעיד') that R' Boruch and R' Efraim embody the level described in the Zohar — implying he perceives their hidden spiritual status through ruach hakodesh, not through ordinary knowledge.

The Rebbe of Apt is described as 'knowing clearly as the sun' (בידעו ברור כשמש) that the innkeeper's power did not come from the sitra achra — a metaphysical determination about spiritual provenance that goes beyond normal observation and implies supernatural discernment, even though he still needs to ask the innkeeper directly to learn the full explanation.
The Rebbe's knowledge that the innkeeper's power is not from the sitra achra is explicitly grounded in three days of direct disguised observation, not ruach hakodesh or supernatural vision.

The Besht learns of the problem through direct conversation with the innkeeper (not ruach hakodesh), and his role in the story is that of a spiritual warrior combating sorcery rather than a seer who perceives hidden things.

The Besht learns of the sick brother's condition by asking the townspeople directly — there is no moment where he sees, knows, or discerns anything supernaturally; the miracle in the story is a healing, not a perceptual one.

The rabbi's calm assurance ('do not fear the dog behind me') occurs while all parties — including the informer — are openly standing together in the same outer chamber, so his knowledge of the informer's presence and hostile intent requires no supernatural perception; the story's focus is on divine retribution against informers, not on a ruach-hakodesh moment.

The Besht receives a heavenly command (שצווי מן השמים) directing him to the village, and he then perceives from the innkeeper's face that money is available — correctly, even though the innkeeper explicitly says he has none — both instances of knowing things he could not know through normal means (ruach hakodesh).

The Rabbi claims he has the power to know all the innkeeper's deeds from birth ('יש לאל ידי לידע כל מעשיך ודרכך מיום הולדך עד עתה'), but this is deployed as a rhetorical threat to compel honesty, not an actual demonstration of supernatural perception — the Rabbi learns everything through the innkeeper's own voluntary confession, and the story's focus is on the innkeeper's bitachon, not on any ruach hakodesh episode.

The Besht explicitly tells R' Yosef that what he experienced was not a dream but a true vision ('חזיון'), and adds 'גם אני ראיתיו ובעיני נתגשם' — affirming that he himself also perceived the same heavenly scene, demonstrating supernatural perception (ruach hakodesh).

The Besht explicitly demonstrates supernatural perception twice: he knows future events from hearing someone study (מה שיהיה בסוף השנה), and he discerns hidden meaning in R' Gershon's Mishnah study to reveal knowledge of two enemies and their fates — knowledge he could not have obtained naturally.

The Besht orchestrates the entire sequence — choosing this specific inn, ordering the doors sealed, and commanding the woman to raise her hands at the exact moment the nobleman draws his sword — implying he foreknew events he could not have known naturally (ruach hakodesh), even though his supernatural perception is shown through action rather than explicit narration.

The story centers on the Besht's authority to command and expel a demon; his running to the bridge is incidental and not framed as supernatural perception or ruach hakodesh, even if timely awareness is implied.

The Besht sees the 'להט החרב המתהפכת' (flaming rotating sword) blocking the passage — a supernatural vision invisible to his companions — which causes him to turn back, depicting classic ruach hakodesh perception.

The story contains no supernatural perception event — it depicts R. Zusha's intense devotion (dveikut) and uses a king/craftsman parable to explain his approach to divine service, but no character sees or knows anything beyond natural means.

R. Yitzchak repeatedly perceives the deceased man's soul after death (נראה להר' יצחק), receives from him knowledge of heavenly realms and the correct interpretation of Zohar passages as studied in Gan Eden — supernatural seeing and knowing that others cannot access.

R' Yitzchak repeatedly sees and converses with the deceased man's soul after his death, receiving revelations about Gan Eden and heavenly Torah study — a clear depiction of supernatural perception of the otherworldly.

The story explicitly attributes ruach hakodesh to the sage twice — he supernaturally knows about distant Poland and about R' Baruch of Mezhbizh, confirmed at the end with 'ראיתי אותו ונהירין ליה שבילין דשמיא כשבילין דארעיה.'

The rabbi locates his first wife through deliberate human planning (arranging charity distributions and checking a register), not through supernatural vision or ruach hakodesh — there is no moment where he perceives something others cannot.

The Tzaddik (R. Elimelech of Lizhensk) demonstrates ruach hakodesh by knowing the exact date of the gentile lord's holiday and prescribing a precise, supernaturally-guided plan — details he could not have known through ordinary means — which results in the miraculous identity-swap that restores the expelled innkeeper.

The Besht explicitly sees the 'להט החרב המתהפכת' (flaming revolving sword) at the crossing — a supernatural vision invisible to the robbers — which causes him to turn back, making supernatural perception a central plot element.

The Rema's deduction that his household was fasting is explicitly attributed to his natural wisdom ('ברוב חכמתו') from observing his servants' behavior, not to ruach hakodesh or any supernatural perception.
The Besht supernaturally discerns the cause of his amulets' failure — 'חקר ודרש … ונודע לו הדבר' — learning through spiritual inquiry what he could not have known by normal means.

The Besht explicitly describes a soul-ascent (עליית הנשמה) in which he perceives wondrous things, sees countless souls of the living and dead, enters the Messiah's palace, and receives knowledge unavailable through normal means — classic ruach hakodesh / supernatural perception.

The story centers on the overwhelming awe and fear R' Avraham inspires in those present at the circumcision; no character is depicted as seeing or knowing something through supernatural perception (ruach hakodesh).

The Besht receives all information through normal channels — townspeople tell him the brother is ill, and he hears the death-wailing himself; the miracle in the story is healing, not supernatural knowledge or perception.

No character genuinely perceives supernaturally — the bishop's claimed prophetic flights to Muhammad are explicitly exposed as fraud, and the tailor's clever scheme is presented as human ingenuity aided by divine providence, not ruach hakodesh or supernatural vision.

The story centers on the Besht healing (and then withdrawing) a blind boy's sight through holy names; the Besht reacts to the wife's mockery which he heard directly — there is no depiction of him perceiving hidden information or seeing things others cannot.

The story explicitly states that Rabbi Yonatan of Prague 'recognized through his ruach hakodesh (ברוח קדשו) all the evil deeds' the student had committed, a direct and central instance of supernatural perception.

The Besht perceives a heavenly decree (kitreg) threatening Israel that no one else can see, then undergoes a mystical journey through celestial palaces during the Neilah prayer — seeing, hearing, and interacting with figures and realities entirely inaccessible to ordinary perception.

The living chassid prays to be told from heaven the hidden reason for his friend's unusual death, and the dead chassid supernaturally appears to him in his room and reveals the otherworldly judgment — conveying knowledge the living man could not have accessed by normal means.

Rabbi Leib is explicitly described as knowing all the hidden tzadikim ('ידע מכולם') through supernatural means, and he perceives/senses (הרגיש) that the young man is secretly following him at the Duke's court — both are clear instances of knowing/sensing things beyond normal human capacity.

The rabbi explicitly states moments before death that he sees nothing but the divine force (כח האלקי) animating all physical matter — a direct depiction of supernatural perception surpassing normal human sight.

The Besht directly perceives a heavenly accusation (קיטרוג) against Israel, travels through celestial palaces during the Neilah prayer, and receives knowledge from the Messiah — all supernatural perception inaccessible to ordinary people.
The Besht enters his house, sees no one, yet immediately tells the narrator 'you are wearing tefillin' — perceiving hidden ritual behavior without any normal means of knowing it.

The Rebbe prescribes the exact future day he will need to leave for kiddush levana (foreknowledge), and the story explicitly concludes that he is 'רואה את הנעשה מרחוק' — one who sees what happens from afar — marking his supernatural perception as the story's central wonder.

The story opens with the Besht receiving a divine command from heaven (צוו לו מן השמים) to travel to the village, constituting supernatural perception/ruach hakodesh, and he also reads the innkeeper's face to perceive his inner state of trust — though the face-reading is ambiguous between natural and supernatural discernment.

The Besht supernaturally confirms that the Rav's Torah teaching was transmitted 'letter by letter' exactly as received — knowledge he could not have had by normal means, a clear instance of ruach hakodesh perception.

The Besht demonstrably knows in advance that a specific stranger will arrive at the inn (ordering the doors shut for all comers), signals the precise moment to admit him, and cries out to the sick woman at exactly the right instant — all indicating foreknowledge (ruach hakodesh) that drives the entire plot.

The gatekeeper at Gehenna supernaturally knows that the dreamer forgot Ya'aleh v'Yavo in Maariv and reveals his secret sinful thoughts from a charity-collecting journey — knowledge he could not possess through natural means.

The Besht's letter includes a specific predictive sign — naming the exact pregnancies and sexes of children of two men who weren't yet communal leaders — which is fulfilled precisely upon the letter being read 17 years later, constituting a clear and central depiction of ruach hakodesh.

The story depicts multiple clear instances of supernatural perception: the Rav instantly senses (הרגיש) from a distance that R. Shlomo of Karlin has already blown the shofar; he prophetically declares (ומראש ומקדם) that the cold will destroy the French army; and moments before death he states he perceives only the divine life-force animating all matter and nothing else — all classic ruach hakodesh knowing/seeing beyond normal human capacity.

The miracle in this story is divine providence and rescue (a mysterious old man diverts the rabbi, causing the bishop to fall into his own trap), not a character supernaturally perceiving or knowing things beyond normal means.

The Besht explicitly describes an 'עליית נשמה' (soul ascent) during sleep in which he hears a heavenly decree against the Jews of the city — knowledge he could not have obtained by natural means, confirmed when the decree's cancellation is publicly revealed the next morning.

R' Abraham explicitly states he sees and speaks with his deceased father (the Maggid) both in dreams and while awake (בהקיץ), a central supernatural perceptual event; additionally, R' Abraham foreknows that his father would summon the nagid, demonstrating knowledge he could not have had by normal means.

R' Gershon discerns in a dream that the apparition is a klipa rather than his brother, and R' Leib Mochiach perceives via a sign (הציץ על הסימן) that the figure truly is R' Aharon — both are acts of supernatural discernment; moreover, the dream reveals hidden physical objects (two thalers, wrapped hairs in a box) whose existence is confirmed upon waking, and the narrator explicitly frames the story as proof that 'their dreams were clear and verified.'

The story explicitly depicts R. Nachman of Horodenka possessing a divinely granted supernatural auditory perception — he cannot hear praises spoken about him even nearby, yet can hear Torah teaching from a distance — a God-given selective hearing that others plainly lack.
The story centers on the Rav praising R' Mendeli's prayer and humbly saying it reminded him of the proper path of prayer — no character sees, knows, or perceives anything supernaturally.
The Besht sees a pillar of fire over a grave (supernatural vision) and knows/predicts who will be buried there decades later — classic ruach hakodesh perception that others cannot access.

The story depicts the Besht performing a miraculous healing of a blind boy using holy names, with no scene of him supernaturally seeing, knowing, or perceiving something he could not have known normally.

The Maggid of Mezeritch demonstrably foreknew that Rabbi Aaron of Karlin would die if he traveled home — he twice sent disciples to detain him and later explicitly confirms he knew of the impending death ('שלא רציתי שיסתלק'), a clear depiction of ruach hakodesh.

The story depicts R. Zusha receiving an overwhelming divine fear in answer to his prayer — a mystical/experiential event — but no character perceives hidden information, sees visions, or demonstrates ruach hakodesh.

The Saba of Spola perceives supernaturally — he tells the agunah three months in advance the exact night her husband will return, and he appears in Danzig to the husband (whom he has never met) to compel him to go home, demonstrating classic ruach hakodesh knowledge of distant events unknown to him through normal means.

R' Avraham perceives his deceased father (the Maggid) both in dreams and while awake, the nagid sees the deceased Maggid physically present in the study hall, and R' Avraham knows without being told that his father had summoned the nagid — all clear instances of supernatural perception.

The rabbi receives supernatural knowledge through a repeated dream (שאלת חלום) revealing that the tax collector possessed the soul of Manasseh — information he could not have known by normal means, constituting classic ruach hakodesh-type perception.

The Rema's insight into the secret fast is explicitly attributed to natural wisdom ('ברוב חכמתו') from observable cues (household members not eating, sad faces), not supernatural perception; the rest of the story focuses on his scholarly prowess in disputation with no ruach hakodesh element.
The text is a homiletical Torah teaching interpreting a psalm verse and advocating before God on Israel's behalf — no character perceives or knows anything supernaturally.
The story is a purely ethical-homiletical teaching about charity and the purposeful use of even negative traits; no character perceives, knows, or sees anything supernaturally.

The story explicitly depicts heavenly commands and visions shown to R. Yechiel Mikhl — he is shown supernatural streams of wisdom flowing toward the Besht and later toward the Maggid, a classic ruach hakodesh/prophetic perception motif.

The story concerns the Besht questioning a mnemonic (siman) for remembering names in prayer and offering a gematria-based alternative — no character perceives hidden information or sees what others cannot.

The story reports someone hearing a statement attributed to a deceased preacher (ז"ל), but this reads as quoting something the preacher said while alive, not as supernatural communication with the dead or any ruach hakodesh perception.
The story depicts R. Shmelke of Nikolsburg performing a theatrical act of self-address to teach humility; there is no instance of supernatural seeing, knowing, or ruach hakodesh.
The story is a teaching exchange where the Besht explains a kabbalistic concept by analogy — no supernatural perception, vision, or hidden knowledge is depicted.

The Rebbe of Ruzhin simply looks out his window and sees the carriage passing — ordinary visual perception; the cryptic remark about 'na'ar' involves wordplay/kabbalistic allusion, not supernatural sight or ruach hakodesh.

No character perceives supernaturally or demonstrates ruach hakodesh; the Sadilkov Rebbe's 'test' of Mendel is a shrewd psychological observation (judging a person by what he praises) rather than supernatural knowledge, and the story contains no visions, hidden knowledge, or miraculous perception.
The story contains only a teaching about the nature of prayer and devekut (cleaving to God); no character perceives things supernaturally or demonstrates ruach hakodesh.
The Besht reveals hidden past-life knowledge (that the scholar is a reincarnation of a wicked man who died 60 years ago), not a prediction of a future event that subsequently comes true.
The story narrates the Maggid's illness, his journey to the Besht, his healing, and a mystical Torah-transmission experience — no character predicts a future event that subsequently comes true.

The rabbi receives a heavenly dream-revelation (שאלת חלום) that discloses past events — the informer's repentance and Heaven's prior arrangement of his death — but no character predicts a future event that subsequently comes true.

The story centers on the Besht's ability to elevate souls of the dead during prayer; his statement that the souls will come to R. Gershon too if he learns the kavvanot is a conditional instructional promise about a teachable technique, not a tzaddik predicting an independent future event.

The story centers on the Maggid's illness, healing by the Besht, and a mystical Torah-transmission experience; the Besht's remark that he had 'seen' the Maggid for years expresses longing/awareness, not a prediction of a future event that is then fulfilled.

The story depicts Rabbi Leib severing his relationship with a young man who mocked Reb Baruch and Reb Ephraim, and includes his testimony that the Zohar's 'notrei brit' refers to them — but no future event is predicted or shown to come true.

The story centers on the concept of true self-sacrifice (mesirut nefesh) as illustrated by Rabbi Baruch of Mezhbizh humiliating a Hasid to protect his financial interests; while Rabbi Baruch claims retrospective knowledge of the non-Jews' plans, no future event is explicitly predicted by a tzaddik and then shown to come true.
The Besht teaches and then erases forbidden wisdom from the preacher's mind, but no future event is predicted or comes true.

The Besht explicitly predicts that if Rabbi Gershon stays another Shabbat and receives the written kavanah, the souls of the dead will come to him too — and this prediction is fulfilled when Rabbi Gershon performs the kavanah the following Erev Shabbat and the dead do indeed arrive.

The story depicts Rabbi Leib rebuking and cutting ties with his adopted son for mocking the holy rabbis, with no prediction of a future event that comes true — only a present-tense judgment and punishment.

The Besht makes a promise to pray for the petitioner but does not predict a future event; in fact, he later expresses uncertainty about the outcome ('as if they answer me and as if they don't'), and no fulfilled prediction is narrated.

The Besht's cry that the Sabbath might be desecrated is an expression of fear/grief in the moment, not a prediction of a future event that then comes true — they in fact arrive in Grodno in time; the central supernatural act in the story is the Besht removing Rabbi Michel's Torah knowledge as a moral lesson.

The Besht issues commands and threats to the Satan and warns his household, but nowhere in the story does he predict a future event that subsequently comes true — the grain-heap fire is a result of him directing the Satan there, not a foretold prophecy.

The story describes the Besht's intense trembling during prayer and a mystical Yom Kippur vision of the Satan blocking the path of prayers (which he reroutes), but no future event is predicted and then fulfilled.

The Besht perceives and removes a spiritual obstacle (the priest's never having experienced קרי) in real time — this is supernatural perception and intervention, not a prediction of a future event that comes true.
The tzaddik (R. Wolf of Zbarazh) reveals hidden past and present truths about the rich man's reincarnation history, but makes no prediction about a future event that then comes true.

The Besht's declaration that the children will recover ('תיכף תראו שהילדים יבריאו') functions as an authoritative decree to the subdued mazzikim rather than a prognostication, and the story never narrates the fulfillment of the recovery — the narrative closes on the spirits' origin story and their banishment, not a confirmed future event coming true.

The Besht's actions (arriving late, shaming R' David) stem from a visionary perception of a present spiritual threat (the Satan intercepting prayers), not a prediction of a future event that subsequently comes true.

Rabbi Baruch of Mezhibuzh effectively predicts that the plague will end if the hidden drunk man utters the decree — the mission succeeds exactly as directed and the epidemic stops at the moment of the man's words — but the story's primary frame is the power of a single mitzvah (pidyon shvuyim), making the prognostication structural rather than the narrative's central focus.

The story depicts a near-death heavenly-court vision and intercession by tzaddikim (including the Besht) on the boy's behalf, but no tzaddik explicitly predicts a future event that then comes true; the father's instruction to delay burial is a precaution, not a prognostication.

The story contains no prediction of a future event by a tzaddik or any character; it is entirely about the innkeeper's trust in God (bitachon) and his 'partnership' with the divine as the source of his wonder-working, with past providential events recounted retrospectively rather than foretold.

The story depicts miraculous travel, support of hidden tzaddikim, and a soul encounter at a fair, but no character predicts a future event that subsequently comes true — the remark about the young Rabbi Mordechai being 'at the last of the hidden tzaddikim' is a present-tense spiritual assessment, not a prediction.
The story contains no prediction of a future event; Rabbi Elimelech's repeated question 'מה יאמר ד׳' is a spiritual/moral teaching device about pride, not a prognostication that comes true.

The story depicts a supernatural rescue — the Maggid repeatedly teaches Torah until a miraculous wind carries the captive boy to safety — but no character predicts a future event that subsequently comes true.

The Besht demonstrates foreknowledge (knowing R' Leib stopped on the way back, saying 'ידעתי' after the debt is resolved) and acts with confident supernatural knowledge, but he never explicitly voices a prediction about a future event before it occurs — the supernatural element is clairvoyance/remote knowing, not prognostication in the defined sense.

The rebbe's supernatural feat is clairvoyance of hidden past events and secret agreements (knowing the nagid mocked him, knowing about the handshake oath), not a prediction of a future event that subsequently comes true.

The story's central supernatural element is the Seer of Lublin's clairvoyant knowledge of hidden matters (the nagid's wickedness, the secret handshake agreement between the nagid and the Hasid), not a prediction of a future event that subsequently comes true.

The Besht's supernatural knowledge here is clairvoyance (knowing remotely that R' Leib stopped on the road, saying 'ידעתי' about the outcome), not an explicit prediction of a future event that the narrative then confirms came true.
The story depicts a tzaddik investigating an innkeeper's miraculous power and explaining it through bitachon (trust in God), but no character predicts a future event that subsequently comes true.

The Besht warns that a nobleman will come in two hours to take the woman by force and that she will die if she does not repent, but neither prediction is shown to come true in the narrative — the first is averted by his intervention and the second is conditional; the story's supernatural core is miraculous travel (קפיצת הדרך) and clairvoyant knowledge, not a fulfilled prognostication.
The Besht perceives a spiritual accusation (קטרוג) blocking prayer and acts to neutralize it; there is no prediction of a future event — only supernatural discernment and intervention.

The Besht explicitly promises the innkeeper that his newborn son will survive the dangerous wacht-nacht unharmed ('אנכי בעש״ט מבטיחך כי יחיה הנער בלי שום אסון'), and this prediction is fulfilled when the child lives through the night and the brit milah takes place the next day.
The Besht exercises supernatural knowledge by revealing the scholar's past-life identity (gilgul), but he makes no prediction about a future event that subsequently comes true.

The tzaddik (R. Mordechai of Lechovitch) tells his frightened followers not to fear 'the dog standing behind me,' implicitly predicting the denouncement will fail — and this is confirmed minutes later when the governor dismisses the informer unprompted, marking a fulfilled prognostication, albeit implicit rather than an explicit prophecy.

The story contains no prediction by a tzaddik about a future event; the Besht commands R' Nachman to travel to the Land of Israel (a directive, not a prognostication), and R' Nachman's death is narrated retrospectively as a fact, not foretold.

The Maharsha (tzaddik) explicitly tells the ailing man 'אל תפחד לא תמות' (do not fear, you will not die), and the prediction is fulfilled when the man recovers ('ויהי כאשר עבר הזמן והאיש נתרפא וחזר לאיתני').
The story contains posthumous dream appearances and a heavenly court judgment, but no character predicts a future event that subsequently comes true.
The story depicts the Besht overpowering a witch's demon and imprisoning it, with no prediction of a future event that subsequently comes true.

The Besht tells Rabbi David 'you will know by yourself the day of your return home' — a vague but genuine prediction that comes true when supernatural voices calling his name signal to him that his atonement is complete and he may return.

The tzaddik performs a dream-inquiry (שאלת חלום) to learn about a past event (the informer's repentance), not to predict a future one — the supernatural element is retroactive revelation, not prognostication.

The story centers on a tavern-keeper whose blessings and prayers are answered by Heaven due to his strong faith and his partnership pact with God, but no specific future event is predicted by a tzaddik — the 'prognostication' element is absent; what occurs is efficacious blessing, not foretelling.

The Besht explicitly tells his circle that the sign of his death will be the stopping of two clocks ('אמר להם הסימן שכאשר יפטור יעמדו השני זייגרעס'), and this comes true — the large clock stops first and then the small clock stops at his passing.

The Maggid tells the Vilna man that the greatest doctor travels with the angel Raphael himself — a statement the man later recognizes as a fulfilled prediction when the king's doctor miraculously heals him without medicine, and the Maggid further demonstrates foreknowledge by telling the doctor upon his arrival 'I have long been watching/waiting for you.'

R' Nachman reveals what each person needs to rectify from past sins (via dream-vision of an excommunicated spirit), which is hidden-knowledge disclosure rather than a prediction of a future event that subsequently comes true.
The story depicts a miraculous light/aura surrounding the Besht and Rabbi David's subsequent devotion to him, but no character predicts a future event that then comes true.

The story depicts a visionary encounter (Elijah and 'Rabbi' appearing to the Besht) and a mystical revelation about his daughter Adel's soul-root in a Torah verse, but contains no prediction of a future event that subsequently comes true.

The Besht receives a supernatural warning (כרוז) about a present danger to the rabbi's mind, prompting him to rush to intervene — this is clairvoyant perception of a current situation, not a prediction of a future event that subsequently comes true.
The story depicts a miraculous healing by the Besht, not a prediction of a future event — there is no prognostication made by any character that subsequently comes true.

The Besht's confident assertion that he will reach Berlin by Shabbat is fulfilled through miraculous travel (kfitzat haderech), not foreknowledge of an external future event; the story's supernatural core is the miraculous journey and the revival/adjudication of the bride, not prognostication.

The story centers on Rabbi Yitzchak's supernatural decree (גזר אומר) that strips the Besht's amulets of their power — an act of rabbinic/mystical authority, not a prediction of a future event — and the subsequent restoration of that power through negotiation; no character foretells a future event that then comes true.
The story involves the Besht supernaturally knowing the visitor's hidden intention (clairvoyance), and teaching then erasing mystical knowledge — but no future event is predicted or fulfilled.

The Besht demonstrates clairvoyant knowledge of the melamed's dream and summons him at the right moment, but he makes no explicit prediction of a future event that then comes true — the supernatural element is telepathic/clairvoyant knowledge, not prognostication.

The story centers on the innkeeper's trust in divine providence (bitachon) rather than any tzaddik predicting a future event; the Besht observes the innkeeper's calm demeanor and remarks on it, but makes no prophecy about what will happen, and the money arrives through natural (if providential) means without a prior prediction.

The Besht weeps out of fear of potential Sabbath desecration but makes no explicit prediction of a future event that comes true; the central supernatural act is the Besht stripping R' Mikhel of his Torah knowledge, not prognostication.

The story depicts a supernatural rescue (the Maggid's Torah-learning causing a miraculous wind that carries a Jewish boy to safety) and a parallel implied present-time intervention by the Rav, but no character predicts a future event that subsequently comes true.

Elijah the Prophet explicitly predicts that when the hidden tzaddik arrives in Preszowce, the children will cry out announcing him as maggid and the townspeople will appoint him — and the story confirms this prophecy is fulfilled exactly as foretold.

The story contains no prediction of a future event; it depicts an unauthorized prayer leader whose words captivate the congregation, and his only notable utterance is a rhetorical challenge about whether past sages are in Heaven — not a prognostication.
The story describes the tzaddik's extreme humility and self-deprecating signature formula, with no prediction of a future event.

The Besht's remark that he would move his house away from the idolatrous shrine functions as a veiled prediction, and the story immediately confirms it by reporting that the shrine burned down — a classic Hasidic prognostication fulfilled.
The story is a teaching anecdote about the word 'Anochi' and humility — no character predicts a future event.
The Besht's supernatural act here is clairvoyance — perceiving the son's imprisonment at a distance in the present moment — not a prediction of a future event that then comes true.

The story contains no prediction of a future event; it is about a Hasid misjudging a tzaddik's apparent lack of enthusiasm, and his rebbe rebuking him with a mystical explanation — no prognostication occurs.

The Besht perceives a hidden present/past transgression ('I see upon you licentiousness') and promises to reveal details tomorrow — this is supernatural knowledge of a concealed fact, not a prediction of a future event that comes true.

The story depicts two supernatural episodes — the tzaddik sensing approaching greeters before seeing them, and waking precisely at midnight despite being plied with wine — neither of which involves predicting a future event that comes true.

The Besht explicitly predicts before acting that he will heal the blind boy completely and immediately ('ירפאנו רפואה שלימה' / 'יראה תיכף ומיד'), a claim the narrative highlights as daring and which comes true — though the prognostication is inseparable from the healing miracle itself rather than a standalone prediction of an independent future event.
The tzaddik perceives a hidden past/present sin by reading the man's face — this is supernatural discernment of an existing state, not a prediction of a future event that comes true.

All communication in the story occurs between living persons: the Maggid instructs his student before dying, and Moshe Shtagel teaches the student nightly in a hidden ruin — there is no depiction of communication with souls, angels, a bat kol, or any invisible being.

The Besht demonstrates clairvoyant knowledge of a man's sin (striking his father), but this is portrayed as direct perception/spiritual insight, not as communication with angels, souls, or a heavenly voice.

The Besht reveals hidden knowledge about the scholar's reincarnation through apparent ruach ha-kodesh, but no scene depicts him (or anyone else) explicitly communicating with an angel, soul, or heavenly voice.

The story centers on reincarnation (gilgul) and heavenly judgment, and the BST supernaturally reveals past-life events to the Risha magnate, but no character is depicted actually communicating with angels, souls, or a bat kol — the heavenly court's ruling is narrated as backstory, and the poor man's 'pouring out his bitterness before God' is standard prayer, not a dialogue with an invisible being.
The story depicts a human interpersonal conflict and rebuke between Rabbi Leib and his foster son — no communication with angels, souls, heavenly voices, or any invisible beings occurs.

The Besht's supernatural act is clairvoyant vision through the Zohar (seeing the son's location across the world), not communication with any invisible being such as an angel, soul, or heavenly voice.

The story depicts the Besht performing yichudim and teaching kabbalistic wisdom about the merkavah as the metaphysical root of animal speech, but no character actually communicates with angels, souls, or a bat kol — the mystical element is esoteric knowledge transmission, not dialogue with invisible beings.

The story depicts the Besht weeping over potential Shabbat desecration and supernaturally removing Rabbi Michil's Torah knowledge, but no character communicates with angels, souls, a bat kol, or any other invisible being.
The story depicts prayer, a rebuke, and a falling-out between the rabbi and his ward, with no scene in which any character communicates with angels, souls, a bat kol, or any other invisible being.
The only supernatural element is the Besht's report that he saw the Shekhinah resting on the kloyz — a passive mystical vision, not a communicative exchange with an invisible being.
The story depicts entirely human interactions — public humiliation by Rabbi Baruch of Mezbizh to protect a Hasid's finances — with no communication with angels, souls, or heavenly voices.

The story depicts miraculous supernatural intervention (a powerful wind that carries the boy to safety) achieved through the Maggid's repeated Torah teachings, but no character explicitly communicates with angels, souls, a bat kol, or any other invisible being.

The Besht perceives a heavenly accusation (קטרוג) blocking the prayers and supernaturally senses its resolution, but no explicit communication with invisible beings (angels, souls, bat kol) is depicted — his intervention is entirely through earthly speech with the priest.

The story depicts the Seer of Lublin demonstrating supernatural clairvoyance (ruach hakodesh — knowing the secret bet and the rich man's character), but nowhere does it depict him actually communicating with angels, souls, a bat kol, or any other invisible being; his knowledge is attributed to holy spirit as an innate power, not a dialogue with an invisible entity.

The story depicts the Seer of Lublin's supernatural clairvoyance (ruach hakodesh) — knowing the secret handshake and the nagid's deeds — but no character is shown communicating with angels, souls, or a bat kol; the knowledge simply manifests as the Rebbe's revealed awareness.
The story depicts miraculous divine providence and the downfall of informers, but no character explicitly communicates with angels, souls, a bat kol, or any other invisible being.
The story depicts the Besht giving directives and a secret whisper-charm to R. Yechiel Michel, with no explicit communication between any character and angels, souls, or heavenly voices.

The Besht displays supernatural knowledge of the scholar's past incarnation, but the story depicts no actual communication with angels, souls, or heavenly voices — his knowledge is presented as inherent prophetic/mystical insight, not as an exchange with an invisible being.

The story depicts the Besht displaying supernatural knowledge (ruach hakodesh — knowing the rabbi forgot yaaleh veyavo, perceiving the bed-owner's sin), but no character is shown actively communicating with angels, souls, or a heavenly voice; the knowledge arrives as clairvoyant insight, not as an exchange with an invisible being.

The story features an afterlife court scene (within an embedded parable) where souls interact with a heavenly tribunal, but no living character is shown directly communicating with angels, souls, or a bat kol — the rabbi's supernatural knowledge of the rich man's past life is implied through spiritual perception, not depicted as explicit dialogue with invisible beings.

The story contains no communication with angels, souls, or a bat kol; R' Yehoshua's prayer for illness is ordinary petition to God, and R' Mordechai's perception of Gan Eden fragrance is a miraculous sensory gift, not a dialogue with any invisible being.

The story depicts a magical duel between the Besht and a sorcerer, with protective circles, demons disguised as animals, and divine intervention (ravens), but at no point does any character communicate with angels, souls, or a heavenly voice.

The Rebbe displays supernatural knowledge of heavenly proceedings (the court's verdict, the mamzerim's fate), but no scene of actual communication with invisible beings — angels, souls, or a bat kol — is depicted in the narrative.

The rabbi's repeated question 'מה יאמר ד׳' is a rhetorical/homiletical device leading to a biblical verse interpretation (Jer. 23:24), not a depiction of actual communication with God, angels, souls, or any invisible being.

The soul of the deceased R. Ezra appears twice in dreams to his friend R. Gedalia and speaks to him directly, which is a central and fully depicted instance of communicating with an invisible being (a departed soul).
The story depicts the Besht's clairvoyance (knowing remotely what R' Leib is doing and saying), but no character communicates with angels, souls, a bat kol, or any other invisible being.

The story contains supernatural elements (a great light, a stone opened by oath, manuscripts 'revealed' five times to the Besht), but nowhere does a character explicitly converse with or receive messages from angels, souls, or a bat kol — the revelations concern texts/lights rather than dialogue with invisible beings.

The story depicts the Besht's clairvoyant knowledge of distant events (knowing R' Leib was delayed on the road), but no character communicates with angels, souls, a bat kol, or any other invisible being.

The story depicts the Besht weeping over potential Shabbat desecration and supernaturally removing a scholar's Torah knowledge, but no character communicates with angels, souls, a bat kol, or any other invisible being.
The tzaddik explicitly 'hears in the heavenly court' (שמע בפמליא של מעלה) the decree against the miser, constituting direct perception of/communication with invisible heavenly beings.

The story depicts the Besht's supernatural hidden knowledge (ruach hakodesh/clairvoyance — knowing the rabbi forgot ya'aleh ve-yavo and would forget again, and perceiving the innkeeper's son-in-law's nature), but no character is shown communicating with angels, souls, a bat kol, or any invisible being.

The Maggid describes angels accompanying doctors as theological teaching, but no character in the story is depicted actually communicating with angels, souls, or heavenly voices — the angel Raphael's role is inferred from miraculous healing, not from any dialogue or direct interaction.

The story depicts a miraculous healing through the Besht's prayer and direct interaction with a sick opponent, but no communication with angels, souls, heavenly voices, or any other invisible beings is depicted.

The Besht is overheard directly addressing the child's soul ('הכנס לגוף כי תוכרח להיכנס'), commanding an invisible נשמה to re-enter the body — an explicit depicted communication with an invisible being.
The story is entirely about human characters — a separation, reunion, and legal deliberation — with no depiction of communication with angels, souls, divine voices, or any invisible beings.

The story centers on kabbalistic instruction about animal/bird speech (שיחת בהמות ועופות) rooted in Merkavah theory; no character communicates with angels, disembodied souls, or a heavenly voice — the mystical content is a teaching transmission between two human figures.

The story depicts the Maggid's foreknowledge of Rabbi Aaron of Karlin's impending death, but no character is shown explicitly communicating with angels, souls, a bat kol, or any invisible being — the Maggid's awareness is presented as mystical perception, not dialogue with an invisible entity.

The tsadik displays supernatural perception (ruach hakodesh) to detect the fraudulent get and know the husband's whereabouts, but the story nowhere depicts him communicating with angels, souls, a bat kol, or any other invisible being — the knowledge arrives as inner vision/holy spirit, not as dialogue with an invisible entity.

The story depicts the Besht orchestrating a miraculous healing through foreknowledge and a dramatic command to the sick woman, but no scene of communication with angels, souls, or a heavenly voice appears anywhere in the narrative.

The Besht explicitly states he heard a teaching 'בג״ע בשמי' (in the heavenly Garden of Eden), and R' Avraham Abba recounts a dream in which 'שר התורה' (the angelic Prince of Torah) appeared and delivered Torah in the Besht's name — both are direct communications with invisible/heavenly beings.

R. David explicitly states 'אמרו לי מן השמים' (they told me from Heaven) referring to the mysterious disembodied voice that knocked on the windows and called his name three times — a clear bat kol communicating directly with him.

The story depicts the Rav miraculously identifying blood on a sheet as dove's blood through supernatural perception, but no communication with angels, souls, or heavenly voices occurs — he explicitly denies being a prophet ('לא נביא אנכי').
The Besht is explicitly heard commanding the child's soul ('לנשמת הילד') to re-enter its body — direct speech addressed to an invisible spiritual being.

The rabbi narrates supernatural knowledge of heavenly proceedings (the soul's detention, the heavenly court verdict), but no character in the story is depicted actually communicating with angels, souls, or a bat kol — the rabbi's clairvoyance is presented as prophetic knowledge, not as an exchange with invisible beings.

The hidden tzaddik is visited by Elijah the Prophet (אליהו), who communicates with him directly — revealing a heavenly decree about his future role as maggid and interpreting a midrash — a clear instance of communication with a supernatural/invisible being.

The story depicts only human-to-human interaction: the Maggid sends his disciple on an errand, the disciple intuits the spiritual purpose himself, and the Maggid confirms it face-to-face upon his return; 'המלאך' (the Angel) refers to Abraham the Angel, a human disciple with a nickname, not an actual angel.

The story centers on a prophetic dream in which the melamed sees and hears the living Besht teaching Torah, and the Besht's supernatural knowledge of that dream — but there is no communication with angels, disembodied souls, or heavenly voices; all supernatural elements involve a living person (the Besht), not invisible beings.

The Besht narrates a story revealing hidden knowledge of past gilgulim and heavenly court proceedings, but no character in the story is depicted actively communicating with angels, souls, or a bat kol — the heavenly tribunal scene is reported as backstory, not as a direct interaction with invisible beings.
The story depicts awe and fear inspired by R' Avraham's holy presence at a circumcision, with no communication between any character and an invisible being (angel, soul, heavenly voice, etc.).

The story is a legal-homiletical interpretation of a Mishnaic case applied to the Temple and redemption, with no depiction of a character actually communicating with angels, souls, or heavenly voices.

The story depicts a rabbi's decree being fulfilled ironically through natural events (wife's death, innkeeper's death), with no communication between any character and angels, souls, or heavenly voices.
The story depicts the Besht's clairvoyance (seeing from afar) and a predictive letter, but no communication with angels, souls, or heavenly voices occurs.

The Besht's foreknowledge of the priest's blood-libel plot reflects clairvoyance or mystical insight, but the story depicts no communication with angels, souls, a bat kol, or any invisible being — only a face-to-face conversation with the priest himself.

The story recounts the Shpoler Zeyde's early biography — visits to the Besht, work as a shochet and dayan, and a mystical recognition by R. Michel of Zlotchov — but nowhere depicts any character communicating with angels, souls, a bat kol, or other invisible beings.

The story depicts the Besht performing a miraculous healing/transfer (חילופין) through a timely command to the sick woman, but no communication with angels, souls, bat kol, or other invisible beings is depicted or implied.

The story depicts the rabbi's dramatic holy death — he announces he will 'be bound with the Living of All Worlds' and expires — but no actual communication with angels, souls, or heavenly voices occurs; his final words are addressed to a living person (Rabbi Avraham Shines).

The story depicts a practical hiding episode (R' Shaul concealed behind a curtain during the Kaiser's visit) with no communication between any character and an invisible being such as an angel, soul, or heavenly voice; the closing remark about trusting in God is a general expression of faith, not a depicted supernatural interaction.
The story describes ascetic practice (cold mikveh) and struggle with intrusive thoughts, with no depiction of communication with angels, souls, or any invisible beings.

The story centers on hidden-tzaddik revelation and Torah transmission; the Rebbe of Rizhin's identification of the teachings' source is supernatural perception (excluded as ambient miraculousness), and no discrete before/after interventional miracle occurs.

The story is a moral-social narrative about Rabbi Leib severing ties with an ungrateful ward; the brief mention of 'light' emanating from the Besht's grandsons is ambient spiritual aura, not a discrete supernatural event with a clear before/after attached to a recipient.

The story centers on the Besht's revelation of the scholar's past gilgul (reincarnation), which is an instance of the tzaddik's supernatural perception/knowledge — explicitly excluded ambient miraculousness — not a discrete interventional miracle with a clear before/after attached to the recipient.

The Besht's act is clairvoyant perception at a distance (seeing the son's location via the Zohar), explicitly excluded from supernatural:miracle by the tag definition; no discrete before/after intervention occurs — the son's wagon simply breaks down naturally and he arrives on his own.

The story's only supernatural element is the Besht's remote perception of the man's sin — explicitly excluded from this tag — while the implied premature death is a consequence, not a depicted discrete miraculous event with a clear before/after.

The story depicts a rebuke and severing of the father-son bond between R' Leib and the orphan he raised — there is no discrete supernatural event with a clear before/after; the reference to 'light appearing' from R' Baruch and R' Efraim is ambient/metaphorical praise, not a miracle narrative.

The supernatural elements are the tzaddik's clairvoyant perception of the man's past-life identity (ambient miraculousness, explicitly excluded) and the theological framework of gilgul/heavenly court — neither constitutes a discrete miraculous intervention with a clear before/after physical change for a recipient.

The story depicts divinely ordained poetic justice (informers' ruin, the Governor's spontaneous dismissal of Avigdor) framed as providential reward and punishment, but no discrete supernatural event with a clear before/after occurs — the Rabbi's composure and the Governor's ruling are presented as natural acts carrying moral significance, not as a נס/מופת.

The story's central supernatural element is the tzaddik's hidden knowledge of the nagid's secret wager with the Hasid — a classic case of the tzaddik perceiving concealed matters, which the tag definition explicitly excludes as 'ambient miraculousness'; the nagid's subsequent spiritual transformation is moral/psychological, not a discrete supernatural event with a physical before/after.

The supernatural content consists of posthumous dream-appearances and a heavenly-court vision (ambient/revelatory supernaturalism), while the closing claim that the community's prayers were 'accepted' and no trouble befell them after hiring the grammarian is too vague and diffuse to constitute a discrete miracle event with a clear before/after attached to a specific recipient.

The story depicts the Besht's death surrounded by supernatural atmosphere — prophetic visions, perceiving at a distance, rectifying a wandering soul, the Angel of Death, the soul's departure as a blue flame — but none of these constitute a discrete נס/מופת with a clear before/after benefit to a recipient; they fall under ambient miraculousness, spiritual perception, and the tzaddik's soul-work.

The story's wondrous elements are the Besht's clairvoyance/foreknowledge (explicitly excluded by the tag definition as ambient miraculous perception) and a providential financial reversal achieved through mundane means (R' Leib's clever argument prompting the nobleman to recheck his accounts), not a discrete supernatural event acting on a recipient.

The story's primary supernatural elements are the tzaddik R' Mordechai's spiritual discernment — smelling Gan Eden in the food and perceiving the shochet's worthiness through conversation — which fall under ambient miraculousness explicitly excluded by the tag; the answered prayer for illness (R' Yehoshua falls ill as requested) has a before/after structure but functions as a minor narrative device rather than a foregrounded discrete miracle.

At the story's climax, fire descends and burns the grain pile (וירד אש וישרוף את כל הכרי), and the Besht and his entire household are explicitly saved (ניצולו) — a discrete supernatural event with a clear before/after (household endangered by Samael's disguised plot → household delivered), though the story is dominated by supernatural conflict and use of holy names that sit closer to the magic register.

The story depicts a discrete healing miracle with a clear before/after: the gravely ill man (gasping, speechless, beyond all doctors' help) recovers spontaneously and completely without any medication while the royal physician watches in astonishment — an event the patient himself attributes to the Maggid's prediction about the angel Raphael accompanying the doctor.

The story features the Besht and various other figures (R' Zev, R' Leib Kreminer, a nobleman, a commissioner), including an orphan girl raised in the Besht's house, but no member of the Besht's immediate family (wife, sons, daughters, parents) appears as a character.

No members of the Besht's immediate family (wife, sons, daughters, parents) appear as characters; the family members mentioned (father-in-law, daughter-in-law, grandfather) belong to other characters in the story.

No immediate family members of the hidden tzaddik (the tailor) or of any other tzaddik figure appear as characters; the rabbi of Ostroha merely mentions his deceased forefathers in passing as a reason for reluctance to relocate, which is incidental rather than a character appearance.

No members of Rabbi Meir of Przemyślany's immediate family appear in the story; the only familial reference is to the narrator's own uncle (דודי ר׳ נחמן), who serves as a transmission source, not as a character in the narrative itself.

The Maharsha (tzaddik) appears as a rabbinic judge, but no members of his family (wife, sons, daughters, parents) appear as characters; the only household members mentioned ('בני ביתו') belong to the sick man, not the tzaddik.

The story features the Besht, his disciple/servant R' Yaakov, a wealthy Italian patron (the former bishop), and an old woman — no wife, sons, daughters, or parents of the tzaddik appear as characters.

The story features only the Maggid of Mezeritch and his disciples interacting with R. Aaron of Karlin — no family members (wife, sons, daughters, parents) of any tzaddik appear as characters.

The story depicts the Besht's death surrounded by disciples and a servant, with no members of his immediate family (wife, sons, daughters, parents) appearing as characters; the only daughter mentioned ('בתו של השמש') is the shamash's daughter, not the tzaddik's.
The family members mentioned (the sick child and his mother) belong to the householder, not to the Besht (the tzaddik); none of the Besht's own family members appear in the story.

No family members (wife, sons, daughters, parents) of any tzaddik appear as characters; the story features the Besht, R' Yechiel Michel, R' Mikhel the dayan, and R' Ze'ev Kotzes, with the mention of a brit simply as a travel destination, not as an appearance of a family member.
The story features the Besht, his disciples, innkeepers, a wealthy man, a bride, and a sorcerer, but no members of the Besht's own family (wife, children, or parents) appear or are mentioned.

No family members of the tsaddik (wife, sons, daughters, parents) appear as characters; the figures present are the Rav himself, his teacher the Maggid (מורי), the Maggid's servant Polit, a widow and her son, and the Hasidic disciples.
The story features only the Baal Shem Tov and R' Moshe of Shatnov — no family members (wife, children, parents) of the tzaddik appear as characters.

The story features only the Besht, a doctor, and a duchess — no family members of the tzaddik appear as characters.
The story depicts only Rabbi Pinchas of Koretz addressing questioners about his prayer style; no family members (wife, sons, daughters, parents) appear as characters.
The story features only the Besht, R' Leibsh, R' Yitzchak, and the Angel of Death — no family members of the tzaddik appear.
The story mentions a rabbi's grandfather (זקינו) as a witness who visited the Besht, but no members of the tzaddik's own immediate family (wife, sons, daughters, parents) appear as characters.

No nistar/hidden righteous figure appears in the story; the only צדיק is the town rabbi (הרב דמתא), an openly known communal leader, not a concealed or hidden righteous person.
The story features the Besht (openly revealed) and a poor Torah scholar who is a gilgul of a sinner; no character is depicted as a nistar or hidden righteous person (lamed-vavnik).

The story's central figures (R' Baruch and R' Ephraim) are explicitly publicly known and becoming famous; the brief citation of the Zohar's 'notrei brit' concept by R' Leib is a passing honorific, not a depiction of an actual nistar/hidden righteous character.

The only tzaddik in the story is the Besht, who is explicitly described as publicly known and visited by people from all corners of the world — the opposite of a nistar/hidden righteous figure; no lamed-vavnik or concealed righteous character appears.

The story features the publicly known and celebrated Seer of Lublin as its central holy figure — an openly recognized tzaddik, not a nistar or hidden righteous person — and no lamed-vavnik or concealed holy figure appears anywhere in the narrative.

The poor brother (העני הצדיק) is righteous and his spiritual stature is unrecognized by others, but he functions as a moral-parable figure in a story about divine justice and gilgul (reincarnation), not as a nistar/lamed-vavnik in the classic Hasidic sense of a hidden tzaddik who deliberately conceals his identity in the living world.
The story features publicly known rabbinic figures (R. Mordechai of Lechowitz and the 'holy rabbi') and informers (מוסרים); no nistar/hidden righteous person appears in the narrative.

The only tzaddik figure beyond the Besht is the woman's deceased grandfather, described explicitly as 'צדיק מפורסם וגדול הדור' (a famous, publicly known tzaddik), the opposite of a nistar; no hidden righteous person appears in the story.

The story explicitly presents ר' משה מנאיימיל, a simple villager, as a hidden righteous figure: R' Israel Polotsky testifies to the Maggid of Mezeritch that he saw in this plain villager 'את הצלם אלקים בשלמות כאשר ניתן לו' — the divine image in its perfection — a classic nistar motif of an unassuming person recognized by spiritual leaders as secretly possessing extraordinary holiness.
All named characters (the Besht, the Maggid, R' Mendel, R' Yaakov of Neapolis, R' Eliyahu) are identified, prominent figures — no nistar or anonymously hidden righteous person appears in the story.

The story features the publicly known Maggid of Mezeritch and a secular Jewish doctor (Gurde/Gordi) who undergoes a revealed transformation into a great tzaddik; no character is depicted as a nistar or hidden righteous person concealing their spiritual status.
The story features the Besht as a known, publicly recognized rebbe, and the other character is an ordinary sinner seeking repentance — neither is a nistar or hidden righteous figure.

The story features publicly known, celebrated tzadikim (R' Baruch and R' Efraim, grandsons of the Besht); the phrase 'נוטרי ברית' from the Zohar is invoked as a laudatory epithet for them, not to depict an actual nistar/hidden righteous character.

The story features Rabbi Shimshon Wiener, an openly recognized gaon/tsadik, and his first wife (a faithful but ordinary woman); no character is depicted as a nistar or hidden righteous person (lamed-vavnik).

R' Ezra is an openly celebrated, publicly recognized pious man whose deeds were observed and documented by his community during his lifetime — the opposite of a nistar whose righteousness is concealed; there is no motif of hidden identity or lamed-vavnik concealment in the story.
The story features the publicly known and celebrated Seer of Lublin (R. Yaakov Yitzchak) as its central righteous figure — an openly recognized Rebbe, not a concealed/hidden nistar or lamed-vavnik.

The story's spiritual protagonists are the Maggid of Mezeritch and the Rebbe — both publicly known figures, not hidden righteous persons — and the mysterious man who exits the Rebbe's room at dawn is never identified as a nistar or lamed-vavnik.
The story features the Baal Shem Tov acting openly and publicly as a recognized spiritual figure; no nistar/hidden righteous person (lamed-vavnik) appears or is referenced.

No character in the story is depicted as a nistar or lamed-vavnik; the central holy figure is the openly known Baal Shem Tov, and R' Leib Kreminer functions as a timely messenger/agent but is never framed as a hidden righteous person.

The story features the Baal Shem Tov, who openly identifies himself by name ('אנכי בעש״ט מבטיחך') — he is a revealed, publicly known tzaddik, not a nistar/hidden righteous figure.
The story features the Besht and Rabbi Gershon of Kutov, both publicly known figures; no nistar or hidden righteous character appears anywhere in the narrative.

The Besht briefly introduces himself as a 'great preacher' to the innkeeper but is openly identified as the Besht throughout the narrative and is publicly recognized by the end — there is no nistar/hidden-righteous character in the story.

The story features the Besht and the deceased grandfather (a renowned tsaddik) who appears from the upper world to request help, but the grandfather is explicitly described as a 'famous tsaddik and great man of the generation' — an openly known righteous figure, not a nistar/hidden righteous person (lamed-vavnik).

All righteous figures in the story (the Alter Rebbe, R' Baruch of Medzhybizh, the Baal Shem Tov referenced posthumously) are publicly known, prominent Hasidic leaders — no nistar or hidden lamed-vavnik character appears.

The story features the Baal Shem Tov and R' Leib Trestinetzer as named, publicly-known tsadikim — neither is a hidden righteous person (nistar/lamed-vavnik); there is no nistar character depicted in the narrative.

The central religious figure is the Baal Shem Tov (רבינו הגדול), a publicly known and recognized leader, not a hidden righteous person; no nistar or lamed-vavnik character appears in the story.
The story features the Baal Shem Tov and an unnamed tzaddik, neither of whom is depicted as a nistar/hidden righteous person — the Besht is a publicly known figure, not a concealed one.

The story features only the Besht, Rabbi Gershon of Kutov, and Rabbi Falk (as narrator/source) — no maggid appears as a distinct character.

The story features a rebbe (R' Leib) and a young man raised in his home, with no maggid/preacher appearing as a distinct character.

The Maggid of Kozhnitz (הה״מ מקאזניץ) is a central, named character throughout the story, explicitly titled 'מגיד' and acting in a distinct role from the other tsadik, R' Yaakov Yitzhak of Lublin.
The story features only the holy rabbi (הרב הקדוש) and a peasant; no maggid/preacher appears as a character.

The story involves reincarnation (gilgulim) and the Baal Shem Tov's guidance, but no angels appear or are depicted anywhere in the narrative.

The supernatural beings in the story are demonic/evil spirits (חיצונים, מחבלים, חבילי טהירין) appearing in nightly visions to torment the young man — no angels (מלאכים) appear or are mentioned.

No angels (מלאכים) appear in the story; the heavenly elements are limited to an abstract 'kitrug' (accusation from above) and an intercession accepted on high, with no angelic figures depicted as characters.

The story depicts a conflict between two Hasidic leaders and includes miraculous/mystical elements, but no angels (מלאכים) actually appear in the narrative.
The story features only human characters (the Besht, a man who struck his father, a servant, the man's mother) with no angels mentioned or depicted.

The story references 'פמליא שלמעלה' (the heavenly retinue) and 'ב"ד שלמעלה' (the heavenly court) as background legal/theological context, but no angels appear as depicted characters acting in the narrative.
