The story's central narrative arc revolves around the master-disciple transmission relationship: R' Adam's son seeks out Israel ben Eliezer (the Besht), tests him, and formally transfers the sacred kabbalistic writings to him, after which they study together in seclusion — this discipleship bond is the structural spine of the story.

The central relationship in the story is a childhood friendship and collegial bond between two rabbinic peers (Rabbi Moshe Isserles and Rabbi Chaim ben Betzalel), not a Hasidic rebbe–disciple relationship.
The story depicts a peer friendship between two equal rabbinic colleagues (the Rema and R' Chaim bar Betzalel), explicitly described as childhood friends and חברים, not a rebbe–disciple relationship.

The relationship between ben HaRav (son of R' Adam, acting as transmitter/teacher) and the Besht (ostensible servant but true destined recipient) is central to the narrative — they study the secret writings together in a covert master-disciple arrangement — though the dynamic is an unusual founding-myth transmission rather than a canonical rebbe-hasid bond.

The Besht's disciples appear only briefly at the end to hear his explanation; the master-disciple relationship is incidental, not central — the story focuses on the Besht's interaction with the priest to remove a spiritual accusation blocking the Kol Nidre prayers.

The story centers on the Besht's confrontation with communal/rabbinic authority (the Council of Four Lands) and demonstration of supernatural knowledge to a skeptical parnas — there is no master-disciple relationship depicted.
The story centers on the Besht's encounter with a pious laywoman (die frume Rivele) and her spiritual virtues; no master-disciple relationship between a rebbe and his disciple is depicted or central.

The story centers on a skeptical merchant who visits the Besht as a supplicant seeking news of his missing son, not as a disciple; the relationship depicted is rebbe-to-opponent/believer, not a master-disciple bond.

The story depicts the Besht being summoned before a rabbinic assembly and impressing a skeptical communal leader (Reb Avraham Aba), who then secretly observes him — there is no master-disciple relationship between a rebbe and his disciple; the central dynamic is between the Besht and an external rabbinical authority.

The story centers on a hidden tsadik (the tailor) and community rabbinic succession, with no depiction of an ongoing rebbe-disciple relationship; the tailor's single appearance to the Ostroha rabbi was as a divine messenger, not as master to disciple.

The story centers on the Besht's spiritual intervention to neutralize a קטרוג before Kol Nidrei by leading a priest to a seminal emission; his disciples (אנשי סגולתו) appear only briefly at the end as recipients of his post-hoc explanation, making the master-disciple relationship incidental rather than central.

The story centers on a repentant informer, not on a rebbe-disciple relationship; the local rabbi appears only peripherally to counsel against disgracing the corpse and to perform a dream-inquiry, with no master-disciple dynamic depicted.

The story centers on the pious woman 'die fromme Rivele' and her interactions with the Besht (charity negotiation, intercession for the sick doctor) and with her sons — no rebbe-disciple relationship is depicted or central.

The story centers on the Rebbe of Apt's investigation of a pious Jewish innkeeper (a non-disciple ordinary Jew), not on a master-disciple relationship; the Rebbe's followers who report to him are entirely peripheral.
The story centers on the Besht and R' Leib Trestinitzer acting as peer collaborators ('חבירו' — companion) in a joint mystical confrontation with the Samael, not as master and disciple.

The story centers on the Rebbe of Apt's encounter with a non-disciple Jewish innkeeper whose miraculous powers he investigates; disciples appear only incidentally at the opening as reporters of the innkeeper's fame, not as participants in a master-disciple dynamic.

The story centers on fraternal separation, wandering poverty, and a hospitality-themed miracle (hakhnasat orchim), with no depiction of a Hasidic rebbe-disciple relationship; the holy brother with ruach hakodesh is a sibling, not a master to the narrator.

The story centers on R' Moshe's ecstatic prayer episode witnessed by R' Yaakil; the Besht is mentioned only briefly as an analogical reference at the end, with no master-disciple relationship depicted.

The story centers on a rabbi's vision of a heavenly court judging a tax collector for stealing from non-Jews, and the rabbi's subsequent warning and ransom of that tax collector — there is no depiction of a rebbe–disciple relationship as a central or significant element.

The story centers on a wealthy merchant who reluctantly seeks the Besht's miraculous help for childlessness — their relationship is that of petitioner and wonder-worker, not master and disciple, and no ongoing discipleship bond is depicted.

The story centers on a hidden tzaddik (nistar) concealing his identity among ordinary people and his eventual revelation as a maggid — the sole significant relationship is between the rebbe and his host (baal habayit), not a rebbe–disciple bond.

The two disciple brothers appear only incidentally as hosts; the story's central action is the Besht miraculously healing his opponent (the mitnaged brother), not depicting or exploring the master-disciple relationship.

The story narrates the miraculous origins and early childhood of the Besht, with no depiction of a Hasidic master–disciple relationship as a central element; the mention of 'אדמו״ר' is merely a source attribution, not a narrative relationship.

The story centers on the Besht's interactions with wealthy patrons and community members in Slutsk; the brief mention of R' Tzvi the scribe accompanying him is incidental, and no rebbe-disciple bond is depicted or explored.

The relationship between the Besht (master) and R' Tzvi the Scribe (disciple/companion) is central to the story's climax: the Besht entrusts R' Tzvi with a sacred name to concentrate on, and when both are captured and the Besht loses his powers, it is R' Tzvi's recitation of the aleph-bet at the Besht's urging that restores the master's strength and enables their rescue.

The story depicts a peer relationship between R' Nachman of Kosov and the preacher (mukhiach) of Polonnoye, both members of the same Hasidic fellowship (chevra), not a hierarchical master-disciple bond.
The story centers on a skeptical merchant petitioner who consults the Besht about his missing son and is converted to belief — not on a master-disciple bond; no disciple figure appears.

The disciples (תלמידים) appear several times as peripheral witnesses who accompany the Besht and are astonished by his actions, but the story's central focus is the Besht's spiritual mission to rescue a woman from apostasy — the master-disciple relationship is incidental, not the subject being depicted.

The story features R' Wolf of Zbarazh interacting with a wealthy community member (עשיר גדול) who comes to greet him — not a disciple — and the narrative centers on a parable about reincarnation/moral retribution, with no master-disciple relationship depicted.
The story centers on the Besht's spiritual battle with a sorceress and her demon, with no depiction of a master-disciple relationship.
The story centers on the Besht's father and the Besht's early childhood/youth, with no depiction of a rebbe-disciple relationship as a central element.

The story centers on a blood-libel crisis and the Besht's spiritual intervention to protect the community; R' Tzvi the scribe appears only briefly as a background detail (reading Zohar aloud), and no master-disciple dynamic is developed or thematically central.

The story depicts the Besht's interaction with his biological son (Tzvi) on his deathbed — a father-son relationship, not a rebbe-disciple bond, even though a brief transmission of a divine name occurs.
The story centers on the Rebbe of Apt's use of spiritual power to expose an adulteress and vindicate his wife's honor; no disciple-master relationship is depicted or central to the narrative.
The story centers on the rebbe's use of supernatural authority to expose adultery and deliver justice, with no depiction of a master-disciple relationship as a central or meaningful element.

The story centers on the downfall of informers (moserim) who persecuted Hasidic communities; a disciple is fleetingly mentioned at the very end ('תלמידו אשר היה יקר בעיניו כבנו') but the master-disciple relationship is not depicted or developed as a theme.

The story centers on the personal piety of R. Ezra and a posthumous dream vision to his companion (חבירו) R. Gedaliah; their relationship is described as friendship between peers, not a rebbe–disciple bond, and no Hasidic master-disciple dynamic is present.

The story centers on the Besht arranging a wedding and a miraculous financial resolution involving R' Leib Kreminer and a nobleman; no master-disciple relationship is depicted or thematically foregrounded.

The Tzaddik (Rebbe Elimelech of Lizhensk) functions as a miracle-worker and intercessor, but the story's central drama is the conflict between the nobleman, his wife, and the Jewish leaseholder; the rebbe-disciple relationship is a background element (the leaseholder believes in the rebbe, Hasidim lend money at his command) rather than the story's thematic focus.
The story centers on the Besht's deathbed interaction with his son (מ׳ צבי), a familial father-son relationship, not a rebbe-disciple bond.
The story centers on the Besht's interaction with an innkeeper (arendarr) seeking children, with miraculous deeds; no master-disciple relationship is depicted or central.
The story centers on the Besht and his brother-in-law (גיסו) Reb Yaakov — a family relationship, not a master-disciple bond — with no disciple figure playing a central role.

The story centers on two merchant partners and a posthumous legal dispute adjudicated by the Maharsha, with no depiction of a rebbe-disciple relationship — the Maharsha appears solely as a rabbinic judge/halakhic authority, not as a spiritual master to a disciple.
The story centers on a matchmaking, marriage, widowhood, and family dynamics involving the Maggid of Mezeritch's son and his wife; no rebbe–disciple relationship is depicted or thematically central.

The story centers on the relationship between the rebbe R' Meir of Przemyślany and a wealthy miser (גביר) whom he twice saves from death — a rebbe-supplicant dynamic, not a master-disciple one; the chain of transmission through the narrator's uncle is incidental framing, not a depicted disciple relationship.

The story centers on the Saba Kadisha's holy conduct and his intercessory prayer overheard by an anonymous man from Reissen, who is a casual observer/visitor rather than a disciple, and the master–disciple relationship is not the focus.

The story centers on an encounter between the Rebbe of Apt and a pious tavern-keeper (lay innkeeper), not a disciple — the Rebbe investigates and ultimately praises the man, but no master-disciple bond is depicted.
The story centers on the Rema's appointment as rabbi of Kraków and his victory in a public disputation with the bishop; no rebbe-disciple relationship is depicted or central to the narrative.
The story centers on the Besht healing a sick woman and a dramatic encounter with a nobleman, with no depiction of a master-disciple relationship as a central or significant element.

The disciples appear as background witnesses and travel companions whose astonishment frames the narrative, but the story's central concern is the Besht's miraculous rescue of a young woman from apostasy — the master-disciple relationship is incidental, not the focal dynamic.

The two disciple-brothers appear only as background hosts facilitating the Besht's visit; the story's central drama is the Besht's miraculous healing of the mitnaged (opponent) brother, not the master-disciple relationship itself.

The story centers on Rabbi Abba of Krivosh becoming a devoted follower of the Besht after a single Torah exchange, then traveling to him every month for Shabbat — a textbook depiction of the Hasidic master-disciple bond.

The relationship between the Besht (master) and R' Tzvi Sofer (disciple/scribe) is central to the plot: they travel together, the Besht entrusts Tzvi with a secret divine name, and Tzvi restores the Besht's lost powers by reciting the alphabet at his command.

The story depicts a rivalry and negotiation between two peer Hasidic masters—Rabbi Yitzchak of Drohobitch and the Besht—not a master–disciple relationship; the Besht even acknowledges R' Yitzchak as 'tzaddik hador' and an equal whose decree he cannot override.

The story centers on the Besht (master) interpreting a Mishnah studied by R' Gershon (his disciple and brother-in-law) and revealing prophetic knowledge to him — a paradigmatic master-disciple exchange.
The story depicts the Besht in his pre-revelation period acting as a servant/wagon driver for his brother-in-law (Reb Gershon), not in a rebbe–disciple relationship; no disciple figure appears.

The story centers on the Besht arranging a marriage match between two orphans and a miraculous debt resolution via R' Leib Kreminer, who acts as a messenger/associate; no rebbe–disciple relationship is depicted as central or even clearly framed as such.

The story is narrated in first person by a disciple who observes, follows, and interprets the actions and teachings of his Rebbe (the Av Beit Din of Nemyrov), making the master-disciple relationship the central dynamic of the entire account.

The story centers on R' Leib acting as a devoted emissary-disciple of his Rebbe (the Gaon/Rav), traveling to intercede with the director on his master's behalf, weeping and pleading in service of his Rebbe's cause — the master-disciple relationship drives the entire narrative.
The story centers on the rebbe judging a group favorably after a complaint is brought by a Hasid, not on the master-disciple relationship itself.

The story is structured around disciples pressing their master to reveal the purpose of his fair-travels, the master demonstrating it to them, and the disciples finally understanding his intention — the rebbe–disciple relationship is the narrative frame throughout.
The story concerns a cantor (R' Moshe) who circumcised a child and glowed with light; the rabbi is cited only as a source/narrator, with no master-disciple dynamic depicted.
The story depicts the Besht performing healing miracles for a wealthy man and a nobleman's daughter before his public revelation, with no disciple figure present or any master-disciple dynamic.

The story explicitly depicts the protagonist's discipleship under the Baal Shem Tov as formative and central to his spiritual biography — he secretly visited the Besht, 'received what he received,' and deeply regretted being prevented from a second visit — though the narrative also covers his wanderings and occupations beyond that relationship.
The text is a standalone homiletical Torah commentary on a Psalms verse with no depiction of interaction between a Hasidic master and a disciple.
The story depicts the Rav's interactions with the Kaiser and imperial court (tests of perception, a royal encounter, a legal judgment), with no master-disciple relationship present.
The story centers on the Besht's domestic interaction with his wife over money and charity distribution, with no depiction of a master-disciple relationship.
The story depicts the Tzaddik of Premishlan guiding a sinner toward repentance using a clever homily; the relationship is between a rebbe and a wayward stranger, not a master and his disciple.
The story depicts two hidden tzaddikim recognizing each other through Torah wisdom and a chain of discipleship, with no antagonist, opponent, or conflict of any kind.

The story centers on poverty, reincarnation (gilgul), and spiritual repair; all interactions between the scholar and the Besht are cooperative and instructive, with no antagonist or conflict depicted.
The Besht directly confronts the Samael (סמ״ם) who attempts to snatch the Mokhiach from his home, rebuking the demon and praying against it — a clear tzaddik-vs.-demon conflict.

The non-Jews' plan to steal the wine is mentioned only in retrospect as background explanation for R' Baruch's conduct, and no scene of direct conflict between the tzaddik and an antagonist is actually depicted; the story's dramatic core is a theological discourse on true mesirat nefesh, not a confrontation.

The story contains tense exchanges between the Besht and the pious woman (he accuses her of stealing charity funds; she pushes back when he dismisses the sick doctor), but these are not adversarial conflicts — the woman is a sympathetic, righteous figure, not an antagonist, and the exchanges resolve cooperatively without genuine opposition.

The story contains a brief initial rebuke of the Maggid by the Besht and the Maggid's indirect retort via R' Yaakov, but this friction is immediately resolved (הבעש״ט פייס אותו) and is incidental to the story's main focus on healing and mystical transmission — not a sustained conflict with an antagonist.

The merchant is framed as an opponent of the Besht (נגד הבעש״ט) and speaks lashon hara about him, but the story contains no actual conflict scene — the merchant comes seeking help, privately doubts the Besht's answer, and then returns to apologize; the dynamic resolves into reconciliation rather than depicting a sustained antagonistic confrontation.

The story depicts a friendly exchange and gentle teasing between the Besht and his brother-in-law R. Gershon, with no antagonist or conflict — the interaction is collegial and even playful throughout.

The story depicts a clear conflict between the tzaddik (Reb Aharon of Strashelye) and demonic antagonists — two supernatural figures who nightly torment a young man — with the tzaddik actively combating them by providing protective incantations, causing the demons to recoil and flee.

The story depicts the Besht recognizing and testing a pious woman (die frume Rivele) and a heavenly accusation (קיטרוג) against a sinning doctor that she advocates away — but there is no sustained conflict between the tzaddik and an antagonist; the provocative exchanges are framed as tests of her humility, not as opposition.

The story depicts a difference of halakhic/spiritual judgment between two respected tzaddikim (R. Yehoshua and R. Mordechai of Chernobyl) that is resolved amicably — there is no antagonist, no adversarial dynamic, and no real conflict; the tension is a professional disagreement that R. Yehoshua immediately accepts when R. Mordechai explains himself.

The Besht is in direct conflict with an old priest whose spiritual 'accusation' (קטרוג) blocks the Kol Nidre prayers, and the tzaddik must actively neutralize this non-Jewish antagonist to allow worship to proceed.

The only adversarial element is the heavenly מדת הדין prosecuting against the drunkard in the celestial court — a metaphysical background detail explaining his condition — not a direct, depicted conflict between the tzaddik and a human or demonic antagonist.
The story depicts the Besht traveling to Brod and Radwil, receiving charity and giving it away, with no antagonist or conflict — only anecdotes about money and faith.

The story is a warm, collegial exchange between the Besht and his brother-in-law Rabbi Gershon, with no real antagonism — Rabbi Gershon's questioning is curious rather than oppositional, and the closing rebuke is explicitly framed as jest (בדרך שחוק).

The story depicts the Maggid's approach to and acceptance by the Besht, with only a fleeting moment of cold reception (the cryptic remark about horses) that is quickly resolved — there is no sustained conflict with an antagonist, Jewish opponent, non-Jew, or demon.

The story depicts the tzaddik delivering a rebuke/teaching to a proud young scholar, not an adversarial conflict — the son-in-law is a passive recipient of correction, not an antagonist in genuine opposition to the tzaddik.

The tzaddik R' Wolf directly confronts and accuses the wealthy man ('הוי בוגד ומרצח. אתה הוא האכזר'), turning his back on him in rebuke — a clear face-to-face conflict between tzaddik and Jewish antagonist.

The story depicts a pious man's posthumous heavenly judgment (letters accusing him of mispronunciation in prayer) and community response — there is no social conflict between the tzaddik and a human or demonic antagonist.

The tzaddik travels to investigate the innkeeper out of uncertainty rather than antagonism, quickly accepts his explanation, and the story ends in affirmation — no sustained conflict with any opponent unfolds.

The entire story depicts a sustained, direct confrontation between the Besht (and his companion R' Leib) and the Samael (demon), who withholds a secret, attempts to tempt and deceive them, physically injures R' Leib, and later tries to harm the Besht's household — a central, unambiguous conflict between the tzaddik and a demonic antagonist.

The story centers on repentance and divine providence — there is no depicted conflict between the tzaddik (or his disciple) and any antagonist; the innkeeper's struggle is entirely internal, and the non-Jews appear only incidentally as the mamzerim who die.

The story explicitly depicts the Besht in conflict with the Sabbatian sect (כת הש"ץ) — whose attempt to burn the Talmud triggered a divine decree against him — and also shows him rebuking a wandering soul and confronting the Angel of Death, making conflict a substantive narrative element.

The story explicitly depicts the Besht in conflict with the Satan (הס״ם), who is blocking the path of prayers on Yom Kippur, forcing the Besht to divert the prayers via an alternative route — a direct tzaddik-vs.-demon confrontation.

The story explicitly and extensively depicts a prolonged conflict (מחלוקת) between the tzaddik (the Alter Rebbe/Baal HaTanya) and R' Baruch of Medzhybizh, a Jewish opponent, including direct confrontations, disputes over charity funds and phylacteries, and mutual accusations.

The story depicts a teacher-student dynamic between the Besht and the preacher (מוכיח): the Besht withholds and then imparts mystical knowledge, but there is no antagonist, dispute, or adversarial confrontation — only a pedagogical act of granting and then erasing wisdom.

The threatened book-burning by external authorities is background motivation rather than a depicted conflict; the narrative centers on a heavenly soul-journey and celestial intercession, with no direct confrontation between a tzaddik and an antagonist.

The Besht and R' Tzvi are seized and bound by Haidamak robbers (non-Jewish bandits) who sharpen their knives to kill them — a direct, sustained conflict between the tzaddik and non-Jewish antagonists.

The story explicitly depicts the Besht in conflict with the Satan (שטן/סמ״ם), who blocks the path of the Yom Kippur prayers; the Besht must find an alternative route to thwart him — a direct tzaddik-vs-demon confrontation.

The story depicts a poor scholar seeking the Besht's guidance and a reincarnation revelation; there is no conflict between the tzaddik and any antagonist — the 'wicked man' is a long-dead historical figure used as backstory, not an active opponent.

The story centers on a hidden tzaddik (poor tailor) whose dying words guide the community to a new rabbi, with no antagonist — the rabbi's initial refusal to relocate is a negotiation/persuasion dynamic, not a conflict, and no Jewish opponent, non-Jew, or demon figures as an adversary.

The story depicts miraculous travels, care for hidden tzaddikim, and rectification of a wandering soul, but no actual conflict between the tzaddik and a Jewish opponent, non-Jewish antagonist, or demon — the Duke's court visit is a covert spiritual mission with no depicted confrontation.

The tzaddik goes to investigate the innkeeper suspecting a possible connection to the sitra achra, but no actual conflict materializes — the innkeeper proves righteous and the story resolves in mutual admiration, with no antagonist ever confronted.

The story contains an explicit confrontation between the Besht and the angry father-in-law (בעה"ב), who charges at him in fury demanding to know why he is taking his daughter-in-law without permission — a direct social conflict between the tzaddik and a Jewish antagonist.

The story centers on a legal-halakhic dilemma (bigamy prohibition) resolved cooperatively with the father-in-law and a rabbinical court; there is no antagonist — Jewish, non-Jewish, or demonic — in conflict with the tzaddik.

The story centers on a 'קטרוג גדול' (great heavenly accusation) blocking the Yom Kippur prayers, and the Besht actively combats the prosecuting forces (מקטרגים) by neutralizing the old priest — a clear tzaddik-vs.-supernatural-antagonist conflict that drives the entire narrative.
The story centers on divine providence, repentance, and the tzaddik's spiritual guidance — there is no depicted conflict between the tzaddik and any antagonist (Jewish, non-Jewish, or demonic).

The story depicts the Maggid attracting a wayward Jewish doctor to repentance through a miraculous healing — there is no antagonist opposing the tzaddik, no confrontation, and no conflict; the narrative arc is one of wonder, recognition, and spiritual transformation.

The story depicts two concrete conflicts: R' Zev Kitzes (Jewish opponent) blocking the wedding until the Besht pays the 200-gold dowry, and a non-Jewish nobleman threatening to have R' Leib brought in iron chains over a disputed 1,000-gold debt attributed to the Besht.

The story depicts a tragic narrative of R' Aaron of Karlin's departure and death, with gentle persuasion by disciples at the Maggid's behest — there is no confrontation with an antagonist, only a poignant dynamic of foreknowledge and missed intervention.

The story depicts clear conflict between the rabbi (R' Moshe, Av Beit Din of Kotiv) and an antagonist (the butcher Leib Glitz) who tries to disrupt the sacred Sabbath gathering and is excommunicated, dies, and whose spirit is left in limbo — a conflict central to the story's plot; there is also secondary conflict between R' Nachman and his brotherhood colleagues over his act of prophesying.

The story centers on an explicit and dramatic legal conflict (din Torah) between two Jewish partners — one living, one dead — brought before the Maharsha for adjudication; while the tzaddik is mediator rather than a party to the conflict, the conflict between Jewish opponents is the story's entire plot and far exceeds a faint association.

A non-Jewish nobleman, backed by a commissioner who threatens to put the Besht in iron chains, claims the Besht owes him 1,000 gold coins — a direct antagonistic conflict between the Besht and a non-Jewish authority figure that drives the entire plot.

The story depicts a tzaddik (the Apter Rav) testing and ultimately affirming a simple innkeeper — there is no antagonist, dispute, or conflict between parties; the encounter is one of mutual recognition and blessing.

The story depicts an active conflict between hostile authorities seeking to burn Rabbi Yaakov Yosef of Polnoye's holy books and the tzaddikim (Rabbi Mikhl, Rabbi Yaakov Yosef, and the Besht), who protest and intercede against this antagonistic act in the heavenly court.

The tzaddik R' Meir of Premishlan is not in conflict with an antagonist — he intercedes in prayer on behalf of the stingy rich man, rebukes him spiritually, and saves his life; the drama is between the miser and his own moral failing/divine decree, not a confrontation between the tzaddik and an opposing party.
The story depicts the Besht instructing R' Yechiel Mikhl to travel home and blessing him with a son — there is no antagonist, dispute, or confrontation of any kind.
The story centrally features the Besht and a named Jewish opponent (מתנגד) — the third brother — whose antagonism toward the tzaddik is the driving premise of the entire narrative.

A conflict between the Besht and R' Gershon (a Jewish opponent who rebukes him with insults/גדופים) is briefly mentioned at the story's end, but it is incidental background rather than a depicted or developed conflict narrative.

The initial social slight (Besht and disciples not invited to sit) is incidental background; the central encounter between the Besht and the rabbi is a private healing/reconciliation interaction, not a sustained conflict with an antagonist.

The story depicts a sustained, explicit conflict between R' David of Mikliev (the tzaddik) and the wicked preacher (a Jewish antagonist) who falsely accuses him, has him bound, and orders him flogged before the community.
The story depicts a hidden tzaddik working incognito as a distillery craftsman and being revealed through a miraculous vision of Elijah — there is no antagonist or conflict of any kind.

The woman's refusal and curse of the Besht is a brief, incidental moment quickly resolved in remorse; the story's central drama is a spiritual battle to restore the dying child's soul, not a sustained conflict with a human or demonic antagonist.

The story culminates in a direct, physical confrontation where a non-Jewish nobleman (שר) draws his sword against the Besht and attempts to kill him, constituting a clear tzaddik-vs.-non-Jew conflict.

The story depicts the Maggid's spiritual influence leading a secular Jewish doctor to repentance; there is no confrontation or sustained conflict between the tzaddik and any antagonist — the doctor moves from skepticism to discipleship without opposition.
The story depicts a cooperative, entirely benevolent relationship between Rabbi Yitzhak and the anonymous donor — there is no antagonist, dispute, or conflict of any kind.

The Besht is in direct conflict with the spirit of the dead woman (a supernatural antagonist) who tries to kill the bride; he adjudicates between the parties and rebukes the spirit to release the bride.

The story depicts a sustained, explicit conflict between R' Meir and his father and father-in-law, who vow to cut him and his wife off entirely over his becoming a Hasid — a real social conflict that drives much of the narrative, though it involves a Hasid rather than a tzaddik as the conflicted party.

The story centers on an explicit, sustained conflict between the tzaddik (Gaon R. Shimshon) and two antagonists — the Bishop, who schemes to have him killed, and the king's daughter, who manipulates the king into ordering his execution by lime kiln.

The story centrally depicts a conflict between the tzaddik (the Maggid of Mezeritch) and non-Jewish antagonists who lure and imprison a Jewish boy in their church to force his conversion, with the tzaddik combating them through repeated Torah teaching that supernaturally frees the boy.
The story is an interior spiritual/psychological reflection by R' Mendel of Bar about a troubling thought during prayer — there is no antagonist or conflict with another person, group, or entity.
The story depicts Rabbi Shmelke's private spiritual exercise in humility upon arriving in Nikolsburg — there is no antagonist, dispute, or interpersonal conflict of any kind.

The story explicitly depicts the tzaddik under interrogation by imperial (non-Jewish) officials and scholars who threaten him, contradict his testimony, and deploy learned arguments to make him abandon rabbinic law — a sustained conflict between the tzaddik and non-Jewish antagonists.

A demonic figure (a disembodied head with hands) appears at the river explicitly claiming the boy as its own, constituting a real conflict with a supernatural antagonist — though the tzaddik is not directly present, his prophetic warning is what enables the family to thwart the demon's claim.
The story depicts the tzaddik Reb Leib recognizing hidden tzaddikim and his relationship with R. Azriel, with no antagonist or conflict present.

The story depicts a direct confrontational encounter between the Besht and a prominent (non-Jewish) doctor who challenges his medical credentials, culminating in the Besht exposing the doctor as a thief and the doctor departing in shame — a clear tzaddik-vs-antagonist conflict.

The tzaddik of Lublin confronts and defeats demonic entities (רוחין שדין ופגעין) who had disguised themselves as a bride and in-laws, tearing up the engagement contract — a clear tzaddik-vs.-demon conflict.

The story centrally depicts a conflict between the Jewish tax collector and a non-Jewish priest (כומר) who catches him destroying an idol, drags him before a court, and has him executed — a direct, lethal antagonism between the protagonist and a non-Jewish adversary.
The story depicts a friendly, collegial exchange between R' Chaim Krasner and the Besht about a mnemonic for their names — there is no antagonist, opposition, or conflict of any kind.
The story contains no conflict — it is a brief report of the Besht's teaching about praising the righteous and a cosmological saying, with no antagonist or confrontation of any kind.

The scholar belatedly learns from the Besht that he is the reincarnation of the notorious sinner he had been searching for, retrospectively revealing the hidden meaning of his poverty-stricken life as atonement for a past incarnation's sins.

The Hasid visits Karitz for a fair and, observing R' Pinchas's conduct, belatedly recognizes him as a hidden tzaddik with ruach hakodesh — explicitly noted as occurring before R' Pinchas's public revelation ('טרם התגלות הרב הקדוש').

Rabbi Gershon initially questions and implicitly mocks the Besht's prolonged, shaking prayer, not understanding it — and only after the Besht's explanation (and his own terrifying experience with the souls) does he belatedly recognize the true spiritual nature of what he had dismissed, confirmed by the Besht's closing joke that Gershon deserves to be struck for having laughed at him.

The man initially denies committing any sin, but when the Besht reveals the truth about his striking his father, the man belatedly recognizes and acknowledges the gravity of his transgression — a clear retrospective recognition of his own wrongdoing.

R' Mikhel the Dayan only belatedly realizes that the Besht has stripped him of his Torah knowledge, and through the Besht's rebuke retrospectively recognizes that his learning had been motivated by disputation rather than piety — a clear retrospective recognition of both the Besht's power and his own spiritual failure.

The Besht sees a holy light on an approaching figure before entering town, and only afterward — by questioning the townspeople — does he identify and confirm that the light belonged to the righteous woman Frume Rivele, a classic retrospective recognition of a hidden tsadeket.

The wealthy listener only realizes at the story's climax — when the Rabbi directly confronts him — that the cruel rich man in the parable is himself, that the beggar who died at his feet was his reincarnated brother, and that he is the one who owed him the thousand ducots from a previous life.

After Yom Kippur, Rabbi David belatedly learns that the Besht's public humiliation of him was not a condemnation of any sin but a deliberate act to break his heart and protect the prayers from the Satan — a clear retrospective recognition of the hidden meaning behind a witnessed event.

Rabbi Gershon initially questions and implicitly mocks the Besht's prolonged prayer, but only after the Besht's explanation — and especially after he himself attempts the same kavvanah and is overwhelmed by the onrushing souls — does he belatedly recognize the true nature of what the Besht was doing, a recognition the Besht underscores with his closing remark about Gershon having 'mocked' him.

The story contains two clear retrospective recognitions: the hasid identifies R' Pinchas of Korets as a holy man of great spiritual stature before his public 'hitgalut' (revelation), and the Rizhiner concludes by revealing that the grandson of that same hasid turned out to be the informer who betrayed the sons of the Slavuta Rebbe — a belated disclosure that the hasid's lineage harbored a sinner.

After Yom Kippur, Rabbi David the maggid only learns retrospectively why the Besht publicly shamed him during prayer — the humiliation had a hidden spiritual purpose (breaking his heart to ward off foreign thoughts while the Besht rerouted prayers away from the Satan), a classic belated revelation of hidden meaning behind an event.

The innkeeper explicitly does not know who the Baal Shem Tov is at the outset ('הוא לא ידע את הבעש״ט'), and only after the miraculous rescue of the infant and the defeat of the sorcerer do both the innkeeper and the nobleman belatedly recognize the Besht's true identity and holy power.

At the story's end the Hasidim stand watch all night and at dawn see a man emerge from the Rebbe's room with his head bandaged, at which point they retrospectively understand ('הבינו כל החסידים') that the Rebbe's earlier claim — 'credit me for saving a Jewish soul' — referred precisely to this rescued boy, now grown, whose salvation had been wrought that very night through the seven Torah discourses.

Rabbi Yehoshua, who repeatedly doubted the shochet's fitness, belatedly realizes — through Rabbi Mordechai of Chernobyl's pronouncement — that the shochet was in fact righteous and his slaughter kosher all along, a clear retrospective recognition of hidden truth about another character.

At the story's end, the narrator (R' Yisrael Dov) reveals that the mysterious captain who suddenly appeared and rescued the Besht and R' Tzvi from the bandits was in fact Elijah the Prophet — a clear retrospective recognition of a stranger's true identity.

The story contains two clear retrospective recognitions: the father-in-law only learns mid-story that his guest is the Baal Shem Tov, and the woman herself learns before the chuppah that her 'uncle' was never her uncle at all — the Besht had assumed that identity as a ruse to rescue her, a truth withheld from her (and the reader) until that climactic confession scene.

Rabbi Pinchas of Koretz belatedly realizes — during prayer on the 7th day of Passover — that his earlier decision not to go to the mikveh was fatally significant: he now sees the decree of the Besht's imminent death and recognizes that had he gone to the mikveh he might have averted it, but the Besht confirms 'the matter is already done and nothing can change it after the fact.'

The scholar belatedly learns — only upon the Besht's revelation at the story's end — that he himself was the notorious sinner of Kazimir in a previous incarnation, constituting a clear retrospective recognition of hidden truth about his own identity and the meaning of his suffering.

The Baal Shem Tov perceives from a distance that the passing stranger has struck his father, and when confronted, the man initially denies it — only upon hearing the Besht's precise account does he belatedly recognize and acknowledge the truth of what he did, constituting a clear retrospective recognition of a hidden sin.

The story is explicitly framed around retrospective recognition: 'ראו הכל בעינא פקיחא' — everyone, even opponents, came to acknowledge in hindsight that the informers were wicked and that divine justice had vindicated the tzaddikim.

The Rebbe of Apt travels incognito to investigate the innkeeper, uncertain whether his powers come from holiness or the sitra achra, and only after three days of observation and the innkeeper's self-disclosure does the Rebbe belatedly recognize him as a genuine hidden tzaddik whose power stems from his sincere partnership with God — a classic retrospective recognition of hidden righteousness.

R' Abraham of Khmelnyk witnesses R' Baruch publicly humiliating the Hasid and only afterward learns (retrospectively) that the apparent cruelty was in fact a hidden act of total self-sacrifice — sacrificing both worlds to spare the man financial ruin — a clear case of a character belatedly recognizing the hidden truth behind another's actions.

The story contains a brief moment where the Baal Shem Tov recognizes the Samael disguised as a peddler, but this is incidental to the main plot, which centers on a confrontation and spiritual battle rather than on any belated realization of hidden truth about a person or situation.

The entire story builds toward a retrospective revelation: the son-in-law only understands at the very end why the tzaddik cold-shouldered him and repeatedly asked 'What does God say?' — the rabbi had all along perceived his hidden pride, which the son-in-law himself had not recognized as a flaw.

The narrator and the rabbi of Khmelnik explicitly state that observers only afterward recognized ('יודעים ומכירים') that the bitter quarrel between the two leaders contained hidden supernal secrets and was not a simple dispute — a classic retrospective realization that an event had concealed sacred meaning.

The two scholar-Hasidim belatedly discover that the seemingly ordinary man they met in Kapost had a deep, hidden spiritual history with the Rebbe (the Rebbe saved him from demonic forces), and the community only learns retrospectively of the Rebbe's hidden powers through this revelation.

The Besht perceives the light on the woman in real time and immediately investigates who she is; the townspeople already know her as 'die fromme Rivele' — there is no belated or retrospective realization, only contemporaneous confirmation of what the Besht sees supernaturally.

The preacher's astonishment upon seeing the Besht in mystical unification is an immediate encounter rather than a belated realization, and the Besht's closing explanation (why the knowledge was erased) is a direct teaching rather than a retrospective recognition by the preacher of a hidden truth.

R' Leib and the other characters belatedly realize that the Besht already knew the 200 gold coins would materialize from the nobleman's debt reversal — the Besht's confident promise, R' Leib's amazement throughout, and the closing 'ידעתי' all mark a retrospective recognition of the Besht's foreknowledge and the hidden meaning of the entire chain of events.

R' Leibish and the onlookers only realize after the fact that the Besht orchestrated the entire mission — sending him urgently to the nobleman — precisely so the recovered 202 gold coins would cover the wedding dowry debt, a hidden meaning that becomes clear only in retrospect when the Besht says 'ידעתי' and demands the money immediately.

R' Meir worries during the journey that the Besht has spent all his money with nothing left for expenses, only to realize retrospectively — after people come seeking healings and money arrives — that the Besht's trust in divine providence was entirely justified, constituting a belated recognition that the situation held a hidden meaning he had misread.

The innkeeper travels with a man he believes is merely a wandering preacher, only gradually recognizing through the miraculous journey and resurrection of the bride that the stranger is in fact the Baal Shem Tov — a textbook case of a stranger revealed to be the tzaddik.

The climax of the story is the husband's belated recognition of his long-lost first wife among the crowd of paupers, confirmed through the registry and then through mutual identification via identifying signs — a textbook retrospective recognition of a known person whose fate was unknown.

R' Gdalya and the community retrospectively discover that R' Ezra, whom they had assumed was a perfect tzaddik whose portion was 'stored with the forefathers,' was in fact judged in the heavenly court and sentenced to reincarnation — a belated recognition that overturns their prior understanding of his righteousness.

At the climactic confession scene, the Besht explicitly reveals to the woman that he was never her uncle — his entire claim was a ruse — and that her deceased grandfather's soul had sent him to save her, constituting a clear retrospective recognition of his true identity and the hidden meaning of the entire journey.

The story narrates a near-death heavenly-court experience reported by the boy upon revival, but no character belatedly realizes the truth about another person or situation — there is no moment of retrospective recognition, only an otherworldly journey and intercession.

The Apter Rebbe travels incognito to investigate the innkeeper and only retrospectively realizes — after the innkeeper reveals his 'partnership with God' theology — that this apparently ordinary man is a hidden tzaddik whose miraculous powers stem from genuine bitachon, explicitly articulated in the Rebbe's concluding explanation to his disciples.

R' Mikhl the Dayan belatedly realizes ('הבין') that the Besht stripped him of his Torah knowledge as a rebuke, and retrospectively grasps that his learning was motivated by contentiousness rather than piety — a clear retrospective recognition of hidden meaning in an event and of another character's (the Besht's) true power and intent.

The story centers on a posthumous din Torah over a 'sale of sin' and moral culpability; no character belatedly discovers a hidden truth about another person or situation — the identities, deeds, and deal are known to all parties throughout.

The wealthy miser belatedly realizes that the poor man he had thrown out of his house was Elijah the Prophet himself, and simultaneously recognizes that the tzaddik's parable was about him — a textbook retrospective recognition of both a hidden identity and the hidden meaning of past events.

The story is a prophetic-fulfillment narrative — the Besht's predictions about the wagon and the birth come true — but no character undergoes a belated realization about another person's identity or a situation's hidden meaning; R' Mikhl knows who the Besht is throughout, and the confirmations unfold prospectively rather than as retrospective revelations.

After Kol Nidre the disciples retrospectively learn the hidden meaning of the Besht's inexplicable delay: the old priest had been the source of a great heavenly accusation, and the entire strange encounter was the Besht's deliberate strategy to neutralize it — a clear retrospective recognition of a situation's hidden meaning.

The Besht is sent from heaven in advance explicitly to observe this man's bitachon, so there is no belated realization — the disciples witness the innkeeper's trust and its miraculous vindication unfold in real time, and the Besht's closing remark is a pedagogical summation, not a retrospective recognition of a hidden identity or previously misunderstood situation.

The nobleman's recognition of the Besht's identity and power occurs in real time during the confrontation and miracle, not belatedly after the fact — there is no retrospective realization that a past event or person was other than they appeared.

Multiple retrospective recognitions occur: the merchant only belatedly recognizes the humble man on the floor as a great Torah scholar after he corrects his learning, and the rabbi himself retrospectively understands (confirmed by the Maggid) that the night's humiliation was no accident but a divinely-intended rectification his rebbe had sent him to receive.

R' Nachman and the visiting preacher retrospectively learn the true source of the 'prophecies': a dream encounter with the excommunicated butcher Leib Glitz's spirit, who revealed each person's sins — and R' Nachman himself belatedly recognizes a past transgression (the nocturnal emission incident at the gentile's inn) as something still requiring rectification.

Multiple retrospective recognitions occur: the Polnai rabbi's son belatedly realizes the Besht had known of R. Eliezer's death weeks earlier (before any human messenger); the rabbi later recognizes the book on his table as the very forbidden volume the Besht had forewarned about; and R. Eliezer of Tamaspal retrospectively understands that his secret reading of Chemdas Hayamim was what the Besht's dream had revealed.

The king belatedly realizes that the Gaon he had been mourning as dead is in fact alive — and that the bishop (not the Gaon) was thrown into the kiln — a clear retrospective recognition of the true outcome of events.

The dead man (and the living community) belatedly realize after death—through the heavenly tribunal—that the 'sale of the sin' was spiritually invalid, and the living man retrospectively recognizes he had been falsely absolved; this retrospective recognition of the hidden truth of the transaction is the story's central dramatic and moral pivot.

Rabbi Pinchas of Korets belatedly realizes — only after seeing the decree in prayer — that his earlier decision not to go to the mikveh was a fateful mistake that cost him the ability to avert the Besht's death; additionally, the wandering soul is rebuked for spending eighty years without recognizing the Besht's existence in the world, a classic retrospective recognition of a tzaddik's stature.

Only at the very end, when the replacement guards arrive confessing they had wandered lost all night, does the soldier (and reader) belatedly understand that the Rebbe had supernaturally arranged his protection all along — the life-threatening danger was retrospectively revealed to have been no danger at all, and the hidden meaning of the entire episode (the Rebbe 'seeing what happens from afar') only becomes clear at that moment.

The bishop initially scorns the young, unimpressive-looking Rabbi Moshe (Rema) as unworthy of the honor given him, but after the disputation he explicitly acknowledges that he was wrong and that 'not even half of your wisdom was told to me' — a clear retrospective recognition of the tzaddik's true greatness.

The bishop explicitly and publicly confesses retrospective recognition that his persecution of the Jews was sinful — attributing it to religious hatred rather than genuine wrongdoing — and acknowledges that God protects Israel, making this belated realization of moral truth central to the story's resolution.

After R' Aharon of Karlin dies, the disciples retrospectively understand that the Maggid's repeated, cryptic insistence on keeping him was because he foresaw R' Aharon's death — the hidden meaning of his behavior is only revealed (and recognized by the disciples) after the fact.

The story contains a clear retrospective recognition on two levels: the innkeeper only learns after two days that his guest was the Apter Rebbe (the Rabbi had disguised himself), and the Rabbi retrospectively recognizes the innkeeper's hidden righteousness once the secret of the two boxes is explained.

The surviving pious man retrospectively learns the hidden reason for his martyred friend's gruesome death — a forgotten failure to answer 'Amen' to his young son's blessing — only when the dead man's spirit appears and reveals the truth, resolving what had seemed an inexplicable divine injustice.

The story centers on a near-death heavenly-court vision recounted after recovery, but no character belatedly realizes a hidden truth about another person or situation — the child witnesses events in real time and simply reports them upon awakening; there is no retrospective realization of identity or hidden meaning.

The community and the wronged woman belatedly discover that the sister-in-law, who had concealed her hatred and posed as a friend, was secretly smearing dove's blood on the sheet each immersion night — a clear retrospective recognition of a character's true (malicious) nature and hidden deeds.

After the man's death, R' Yitzchak learns through the posthumous visitation that this formerly unlearned man who gave charity in secret and then studied Torah has earned a place among the righteous in Paradise — a clear retrospective recognition of his hidden righteousness.

The story focuses on the Besht's miraculous healing of his mitnaged, and while the mitnaged's compliance (giving his hand, following instructions) implies tacit acceptance, the narrative never depicts an explicit moment of belated realization or acknowledgment that the Besht was right or holy — the story ends simply with the recovery, not with any retrospective recognition scene.
The story straightforwardly describes Rabbi Shalom's miraculous vocal power during Megillah reading with no moment of belated realization or retrospective recognition by any character.
The story depicts a deathbed consolation scene with a forward-looking prophecy about future tzaddikim; no character belatedly recognizes a hidden truth about another person or past event.

The lamdan doubts the Maggid's ruach hakodesh after not being warmly received, but the Maggid then addresses the lamdan's unspoken inner doubts with precision — implying retrospective recognition that the Maggid did indeed perceive his thoughts all along, though the lamdan's explicit reaction is not narrated.

The story centers on the Besht's real-time clairvoyant vision (seeing distant oxen and the Amsterdam event through Torah's hidden light, identifying the bridle thief in the moment) — there is no belated realization by any character about another person's true identity, righteousness, or hidden meaning of a past event.

After the Gaon destroys the metal disc, the righteous men of the generation retrospectively recognize that it was a remnant of Jeroboam's idols and that the Gaon himself carried Jeroboam's soul — his entire purpose in this world being to uproot that idol — a clear belated realization about the hidden meaning of both the object and the person.

The story closes with the narrator revealing that the son born through the Besht's blessing and instruction is none other than the holy R' Yosef of Yampol — a classic retrospective recognition where the significance of the birth event is disclosed only after the fact.

When the Rav hears the name 'Veitl' at the door, he immediately recalls her past covert assistance during his imprisonment and orders her admitted — a clear retrospective recognition of a person and her hidden good deed, which directly motivates his response to her.

The shopkeeper retroactively realizes that the year of miraculously inexhaustible salt was in fact the hidden repayment of the debt — an event whose true meaning (the promissory note in the barrel working its own payment) is only understood after the fact, when the Rabbi explains it.

The story contains two retrospective recognition moments: the Besht's mother misidentifies the conflict as a mundane debt dispute rather than a spiritual battle with a witch, and later the Besht's companions only learn the full backstory of the imprisoned demon when he stops at the forest and recounts the earlier events to them.

The story is about the tzaddik's technique of indirect rebuke to induce repentance; no character belatedly realizes a hidden truth — the tzaddik immediately perceives the sinner's state and the sinner's own awareness of his sin is not framed as a retrospective discovery.

The story takes place entirely within the Hasidic world — a student of the Kozhnitzer Maggid seeks and finds a hidden tzaddik (nistar); the street mockers are incidental background figures, not identified as mitnagdim or maskilim, and no interaction with non-Hasidic opponents is depicted.

The story takes place entirely within the Hasidic world (Shpoler Zeyde, R. Pinchas of Korets, R. Israel of Ruzhin, and a Hasidic follower); the only antagonist mentioned at the end is an informer (moser) to secular authorities, not a mitnaged or maskil.

The story centers on the Besht identifying a man's sin (striking his father) and prescribing repentance; all characters are within the Hasidic community with no mitnagdim, maskilim, or non-Hasidic opponents depicted.

All characters in the story are within the Hasidic world — the mocker is an orphan raised by R. Leib himself, and those mocked (R. Barukh and R. Efraim) are grandsons of the Besht; there is no mitnaged, maskil, or non-Hasidic opponent present.

The story centers on a Jewish informer (מסור) who repents, interacts with a non-Jewish lord and his guards, and saves a Jewish woman — there is no depiction of mitnagdim, maskilim, or non-Hasidic Jewish opponents.

The story takes place entirely within the Hasidic world (R. Menachem Mendel of Lubavitch, R. Baruch of Medzhibozh, a Hasidic follower); the non-Jews (ערלים) mentioned are incidental background figures, and there are no mitnagdim, maskilim, or other non-Hasidic Jewish opponents depicted.

The young man who mocks R' Baruch and R' Efraim (grandsons of the Besht) is a ward raised within the Hasidic household of R' Leib — the conflict is intra-Hasidic, not an interaction with mitnagdim, maskilim, or other non-Hasidic opponents.
The story centers on the Baal Shem Tov confronting a man who struck his father and prescribing repentance; no mitnagdim, maskilim, or non-Hasidic Jews appear or are referenced.

The story depicts a Hasidic preacher visiting the non-Hasidic Hamburg community, but the local rabbi ultimately becomes an enthusiastic supporter of the Besht rather than an opponent, and no mitnagdic/maskil opposition or conflict is foregrounded as a theme.

The story is set entirely within the Hasidic world — a Hasid seeks blessings for children from the Shpoler Zeyde and Rebbe Pinchas of Korets — with no depicted interaction with mitnagdim, maskilim, or non-Hasidic Jewish opponents.

The story depicts a learned Torah scholar (lamdan) visiting R' Elimelech of Lizhensk; the tension is about the scholar's spiritual pride, not any interaction with mitnagdim, maskilim, or opponents of Hasidism.
The story depicts an entirely intra-Hasidic interaction between the Besht and his brother-in-law R' Gershon of Kutov, with no mitnagdim, maskilim, or non-Hasidic opponents present or mentioned.
The story involves non-Jews (עכו"ם) who attempt to convert a Jewish boy — there is no interaction with mitnagdim, maskilim, or any non-Hasidic Jewish opponents.

The story depicts interactions entirely within a Hasidic/traditional Jewish context (the Besht, a pious woman, her sons, townspeople) with no mitnagdim, maskilim, or non-Hasidic opponents present or referenced.

The story centrally depicts Rabbi Michel the Dayan, a sharp non-Hasidic scholar, who publicly challenges and debates the Hasidic preacher, stubbornly refuses to concede the truth, and is ultimately rebuked and transformed by the Besht — a classic mitnaged-to-Hasid conversion narrative.
The story concerns two brothers (rich and poor) across two reincarnations and their moral/spiritual reckoning, with no interaction involving mitnagdim, maskilim, or non-Hasidic Jewish opponents.
The story depicts only an interaction between the Baal Shem Tov and a preacher (מוכיח) who is a Hasidic devotee seeking mystical knowledge; no mitnagdim, maskilim, or non-Hasidic opponents appear.
The story depicts only interactions among Hasidic figures (R' Mordechai of Chernobyl, R' Yehoshua the Maggid, and a pious shochet); no mitnagdim, maskilim, or non-Hasidic Jews appear.

The story depicts the Apter Rav investigating a Jewish innkeeper (arrendator) of simple piety — there is no interaction with mitnagdim, maskilim, or opponents of Hasidism; the innkeeper is a plain God-fearing Jew, not an adversarial non-Hasidic figure.
The story depicts only Hasidic figures (the Besht, his disciples, and followers) in prayer and mystical experiences, with no interaction involving mitnagdim, maskilim, or non-Hasidic Jews.

The story depicts an intimate spiritual exchange between the Besht and his brother-in-law Rabbi Gershon of Kitov, who is himself a Hasidic figure — there are no mitnagdim, maskilim, or non-Hasidic opponents present or relevant to the narrative.

The story depicts an entirely intra-Hasidic dynamic: R' Ze'ev Kitzes and R' David Purkes are themselves Hasidic figures who are skeptical of the Besht's title, not mitnagdim or maskilim, and no non-Hasidic Jews appear in the narrative.

The story concerns a hidden tzaddik (tailor), community leaders (parnasim), and a rabbi — all within an intra-communal Hasidic narrative with no depiction of or interaction with mitnagdim, maskilim, or non-Hasidic opponents.
The story depicts the Besht interacting with a pious woman and her sons in a Hasidic community context; no mitnagdim, maskilim, or non-Hasidic opponents appear or are referenced.

The story centers on a blood libel (עלילה), the Besht's grief and mystical intercession for the martyrs, and a heavenly dialogue — the briefly mentioned preacher (דרשן) in Brod who stirred strife is incidental and is not framed as a mitnaged or maskil opponent of Hasidism.
The story exclusively depicts the Besht and his Hasidic disciples during prayer; there are no mitnagdim, maskilim, or non-Hasidic figures present or interacted with.
The story depicts the Besht and Rabbi Leib Trestinitzer confronting the Samael (demonic figure) in a mystical encounter — no mitnagdim, maskilim, or non-Hasidic Jews appear or are referenced.

The story depicts the Besht traveling with R. Meir, interactions with a wealthy patron, and giving money to the kloyz and the poor — there is no depiction of or substantive engagement with mitnagdim, maskilim, or non-Hasidic opponents.

The story depicts the Besht's final hours and death on Shavuot; the passing reference to his opposition against 'כת הש"ץ' (Sabbateans) is brief background context, not a depicted interaction with mitnagdim or maskilim.

The story revolves entirely within the Hasidic world (Rabbi Leib Sarahs, hidden tzadikim, Hasidic disciples); the only non-Hasidic figure mentioned is a non-Jewish duke, and there is no depiction of interaction with mitnagdim, maskilim, or other Jewish opponents of Hasidism.
The story is entirely internal to Hasidic piety — it depicts a saintly Hasid, his friend, and their community, with no interaction with mitnagdim, maskilim, or any non-Hasidic Jews.

The skeptics (R' Zeev Kutzes and R' David Purkes) are explicitly described as חסידים, making this an intra-Hasidic dispute about the Besht's title/standing — not an interaction with mitnagdim, maskilim, or non-Hasidic opponents.

The story depicts interaction between the Rebbe of Apta and a simple Jewish innkeeper whose piety is rooted in bitachon; neither the innkeeper nor any other character is identified as a mitnaged, maskil, or opponent of Hasidism.

The story centers on a blood libel, the martyrdom of the Pavlivtsi community, and the Besht's mystical grief; the only internal Jewish tension is a passing mention of a preacher in Brody who stirred strife, but no substantive interaction with mitnagdim or maskilim is depicted.

The story centers on the Besht's visions, the death of R' Eliezer of Amsterdam, and warnings about Sabbatean heresy (Chemdas Yamim, Shabbtai Tzvi) — the antagonists who insulted R' Eliezer are unnamed community figures in Amsterdam with no identification as mitnagdim or anti-Hasidic opponents.

The story depicts the Besht's journey to Constantinople and attempted aliyah, involving a Polish Jewish benefactor, sea adventures, and bandits — no mitnagdim, maskilim, or non-Hasidic opponents appear.
The story centers on a miserly wealthy man from Moldavia and his interactions with the Hasidic master R' Meir of Premishlan; no mitnagdim, maskilim, or non-Hasidic Jewish opponents appear.
The story depicts only interactions among Hasidic figures (Rabbi Aharon of Karlin, the Maggid of Mezeritch, and their disciples) with no mitnagdim, maskilim, or non-Hasidic Jews present.

The story takes place in a Lithuanian town and involves a wicked itinerant preacher, but the conflict is entirely moral/personal (false accusation, attempted adultery) with no depiction of ideological opposition to Hasidism or interaction framed around mitnagdic/maskil identity.
The story depicts an intra-community dispute between a town rabbi and a local adversary (איש בליעל), with no mitnagdim, maskilim, or identifiable non-Hasidic opponents as a group involved.

The story concerns a Hasidic rabbi's personal marital situation — separation, remarriage, and reunion with his first wife — with no depiction of interaction with mitnagdim, maskilim, or non-Hasidic opponents.

The story centrally depicts R' Mikhl the dayan, a sharp mitnaged-type lamdan who publicly challenges and debates the Hasidic rabbi, refuses to concede defeat, and is only transformed into a tzaddik after the Besht strips him of his learning as a rebuke — a sustained, plot-driving interaction with a non-Hasidic opponent.

The story depicts the Besht rescuing a Jewish woman from apostasy and marriage to a non-Jewish nobleman; all Jewish characters are within the Hasidic world, and there is no interaction with mitnagdim, maskilim, or other non-Hasidic Jewish opponents.
The story depicts the Rema's interactions with a Christian bishop and philosophers in a disputation, not with mitnagdim or maskilim (non-Hasidic Jews).

The story depicts interactions between a Jewish leaseholder (פיסעסיר) and a Polish nobleman (פאטאצקי) and his wife — these are Jewish-gentile relations, not interactions with mitnagdim or non-Hasidic Jews.

The story centers on a Jewish informer's repentance and his interactions with a non-Jewish lord and gentile guards — there are no mitnagdim, maskilim, or non-Hasidic Jewish opponents depicted or even mentioned.

The story's central interaction is with a distant 'chakham' portrayed as a holy man with ruach hakodesh who warmly receives the Hasidic traveler and spends three days in mystical communion with him — he is neither a mitnaged, maskil, nor opponent, so no substantive Hasid/non-Hasid social dynamic is depicted.
The only antagonist in the story is a non-Jewish nobleman (שר) who threatens the Besht with a sword; there are no mitnagdim, maskilim, or non-Hasidic Jews depicted.
The story depicts the Besht confronting a non-Jewish village nobleman/sorcerer (האדון מהכפר), not mitnagdim or non-Hasidic Jews such as maskilim or opponents within the Jewish community.

The story centers entirely on the Baal Shem Tov and his Hasidic circle interacting with a simple Jewish bookbinder (R' Shabtai) and his wife — there are no mitnagdim, maskilim, or non-Hasidic opponents depicted.

The story takes place entirely within a Hasidic community (Mezeritch), depicting a pious man, Rabbi Yitzchak, and the chevra mishnayot — there are no mitnagdim, maskilim, or non-Hasidic opponents present or referenced.

The story depicts the Rav's visit to Mezeritch and his resolution of a halakhic purity case involving a woman and her jealous sister-in-law — all characters operate within the Hasidic/traditional community, with no mitnagdim, maskilim, or non-Hasidic opponents appearing or referenced.

The story depicts a Hasidic rabbi (student of the Maggid of Mezeritch) interacting with a Jewish merchant at an inn, but the merchant is portrayed simply as a learned traveler with no indication he is a mitnaged, maskil, or opponent of Hasidism — the encounter is about humility, Torah correction, and spiritual rectification, not inter-sectarian relations.
The story involves the Besht, R' Zeev Kitzes, R' Leib Kreminer, a Polish commissioner, and a non-Jewish nobleman — there are no mitnagdim, maskilim, or non-Hasidic Jewish opponents depicted.

The story depicts interactions entirely within the Hasidic world — between Menachem Mendel of Vitebsk, the Maggid of Mezeritch, and the Baal Shem Tov — with no mitnagdim, maskilim, or non-Hasidic opponents present or referenced.

The story depicts a hidden tzaddik interacting with a pious Jewish host family and non-Jewish distillery workers (ערלים/gentiles), with no mitnagdim, maskilim, or non-Hasidic Jewish opponents appearing at any point.
The story depicts interactions between a Jewish sage (Gaon) and a non-Jewish king and bishop — not with mitnagdim, maskilim, or non-Hasidic Jews.

The story involves Jewish merchants interacting with a non-Jewish duchess and a posthumous legal dispute adjudicated by the Maharsha, with no depiction of mitnagdim, maskilim, or non-Hasidic Jewish opponents.

The story involves the Besht, his Hasidic circle, and a non-Jewish nobleman (Adon) in a debt/redemption-of-captives dispute — there are no mitnagdim, maskilim, or non-Hasidic Jewish opponents depicted.
The story depicts only interactions among Hasidic figures (the Besht and R. Yechiel Mikhl); there are no mitnagdim, maskilim, or non-Hasidic Jews present.

The story centrally depicts R' Ze'ev the Av Beit Din, a skeptical Torah scholar (classic mitnaged figure), who doubts and actively challenges the Besht's prolonged prayer, then undergoes a dramatic conversion to belief — a paradigmatic Hasid–mitnaged encounter narrative.
