The story explicitly depicts a group of Hasidim (חברה חסידים) traveling together after Rosh Hashanah, stopping at an inn, drinking, and discussing Torah — clear interaction among Hasidim beyond the master-disciple frame.

The story depicts extended social interaction among multiple Hasidim (R' Yechiel Michel, R' Zev Kutzyes, R' Michel the Dayan) at communal gatherings — a public sermon, a third-meal debate, and a journey to a brit — with disputes, pilpul, and social dynamics among peers, well beyond the master-disciple frame.

The story centers on the Besht's grief over martyrs and his mystical encounter with heavenly souls, with Rabbi David functioning as a witness to the Besht — a master-disciple dynamic; the brief mention of guests (אורחים) waiting at the Shabbat table is entirely incidental and not the story's focus.

The story centers on an informer's repentance and the townspeople's collective reaction to his death, with no depicted interaction specifically among Hasidim as such — the community figures (townspeople, rabbi, cohen) function as a generic Jewish kehila backdrop, not as Hasidim interacting with one another.

The story explicitly depicts multiple inter-Hasidic interactions: the rebbe's Hasidim intercede collectively on the avrech's behalf ('ויפצירו אותו אנשיו'), the Hasidim physically go and sell off his merchandise ('תיכף הלכו החסידים וביערו את הסחורה'), and the avrech's companions sustain him materially throughout his difficult year ('וחביריו כלכלוהו והספיקוהו די צרכו').

The story centers on the Rebbe of Apt investigating a simple Jewish innkeeper (rindár), not on interactions among Hasidim; the brief mentions of the Rebbe's followers reporting to him and receiving his explanation are incidental master-disciple framing, not peer Hasidic interaction.

The story is entirely a master-disciple encounter between the Besht and the Maggid of Polonnoye; the 'several other people' present are unnamed, silent background figures with no social function in the narrative.

The central interaction is between the Rebbe of Apta and a simple Jewish innkeeper (rindár) who is not presented as a Hasid; the Hasidim's role is limited to briefly reporting rumors to their rebbe (a master-disciple framing), not substantive inter-Hasidic interaction.

The story opens with a gathering of all the tzaddikim of the generation at the yahrzeit in Ropshitz, where they collectively address a woman's plea and the Gaon of Kaminke stands among his peers to challenge their promises and relate the Besht tale — a clear, central depiction of interaction among Hasidic masters.

The story explicitly depicts townspeople coming to greet R. Yitzchak and R. Yosef, recognizing them as old companions with rekindled affection ('חברים מקודם ונתעורר אהבה הישנה'), which is a genuine inter-Hasidic social interaction, though incidental to the main narrative about the pious woman.

The story centers on the Maggid of Mezeritch's interactions with a Vilna nobleman and with the doctor Garde (master-disciple conversion narrative), with no depicted interaction among Hasidim themselves; the single phrase 'I heard from the Rav of Khatin' is merely a transmission formula, not a portrayed social interaction among Hasidim.

The story repeatedly depicts the Hasidim acting collectively together — waiting together late at night, repeatedly donning and removing their Shabbat garments in unison, and standing guard overnight at the Rebbe's door — constituting genuine group interaction among Hasidim, though the narrative is primarily Rebbe-centered.

The central interaction is between the Besht and his brother-in-law R' Gershon of Kutov, but the dynamic is essentially master-disciple — R' Gershon repeatedly asks questions and the Besht explains his superior spiritual practice — rather than peer interaction among Hasidim.

The story depicts multiple interactions among Hasidim as a community — Hasidim celebrating the 10th of Kislev together, the Hasidic group accompanying Rabbi Mordechai of Lechevitch before the governor, and Hasidim in Ladi serving as intermediaries — none of which are specifically master-disciple relations.

The story centers on the tsadik Rabbi Meir of Premishlan and a wealthy visitor from Moldavia (whose Hasidic identity is unestablished); the assembled Hasidim at the court are background witnesses, not participants in an inter-Hasidic interaction.

The story depicts multiple Hasidic figures interacting as a group — R' Gershon of Kutov, the rav-hasid of Kutov, and an unnamed cohort of Hasidim who accompany the Besht and collectively press him to release the spirit — constituting inter-Hasidic social interaction beyond the simple master-disciple dyad.

The story explicitly depicts the two Hasidic brothers' fellow townsmen coming to greet them, recognizing one another as old friends, and the rekindling of their fraternal bonds — a clear scene of social interaction among Hasidim unrelated to the master-disciple relationship.

The story depicts multiple Hasidic figures — R. Mikhl of Zlotshev, R. Yaakov Yosef of Polnoye, and the Besht — interacting with one another and with R. Mikhl's son in the heavenly court, making inter-Hasidic interaction a central element of the narrative.
The story centrally depicts the Besht and R. Leib Trestinitzer acting together as a pair of Hasidic masters on a joint mystical mission, which is a direct interaction among Hasidim.

The story is built around interactions among Hasidim — R' Zelik hears the account from R' Meir, R' Meir travels alongside the Besht and acts as intermediary between the Besht and the two notable men in Brod, all of whom are Hasidic figures engaging with one another beyond a simple master-disciple frame.

The story centers on R' Gershon of Kitov learning from the Besht about kavvanot and raising souls — a master-disciple dynamic explicitly excluded by the tag definition, even though the two are also brothers-in-law.

The story centers on the Besht healing a mitnaged (opponent), not on interaction among Hasidim; the two Hasidic brothers appear only incidentally to grant permission for prayer, which is far too peripheral to warrant the tag.

The story centers on the Besht's interaction with a non-Jewish priest; the only Hasidic group interaction is the Besht reporting back to his 'אנשי סגולתו,' which is a master-disciple scene, explicitly excluded by the tag definition.

The story centers on R' Yaakov (a Besht disciple) interacting with a wealthy Italian man who turns out to be a former bishop saved by the Besht — the social framing involves a community that gathers for Besht stories, but the core narrative is a teshuvah/miracle tale, not a depiction of interaction among Hasidim as such.

The story depicts genuine interaction among Hasidim — notably R' Wolf Kutzes and R' Avraham coordinating together to physically guide the Besht to the ark — though these inter-Hasidic moments are secondary to the central master-witness framing.
The story depicts a confrontation between the Hasidic Rebbe (Shneur Zalman of Liady) and a mitnagdic Gaon (R' Yehoshua Zeitles of Shklov), not an interaction among Hasidim with one another.

The story depicts the Besht's 'chevra kadisha' collectively observing, deliberating among themselves, and appointing R' Zeev Kutzes as their spokesman — genuine peer interaction among Hasidim — though it is secondary to the dominant master-disciple frame.

The story depicts multiple scenes of Hasidim interacting with one another beyond the master–disciple dyad: R' Gershon of Kitov coordinating with the Rabbi of Kutov, a group of Hasidim accompanying and pressuring the Besht during the dybbuk episode, and the explicit note that the dybbuk's existence was caused by a scoffer (ליצן) among the Hasidim of Kutov.

The story centrally depicts a group of Hasidim (the Besht, the mokhiach, the rabbi of Shargorod, and the Besht's inner circle) traveling together, spending Shabbat together in a village, and supporting one another during a communal conflict — clear interaction among Hasidim beyond a simple master-disciple dyad.

The story depicts the Besht being summoned before the Council of Four Lands (an established communal authority) and examined by a skeptical parnas/nagid — this is an encounter between the Besht and the Jewish communal establishment, not interaction among Hasidim.
The story centers on a Hasidic master (R. Wolf of Zbarazh) delivering a moral parable to a wealthy man; there is no depicted interaction among Hasidim as a social group.

The social interactions are primarily between R' David and the Besht (a master-centered relationship) or between the Besht and heavenly figures; the other guests appear only as passive bystanders waiting at the Shabbat table, which is too incidental to constitute meaningful inter-Hasidic interaction.

The story explicitly depicts interactions among multiple Hasidic masters — Rabbi Mikhl of Zlotshev, Rabbi Yaakov Yosef of Polnoye, and the Besht — both in the earthly frame (Mikhl traveling to defend Yaakov Yosef's books) and in the heavenly vision, where they encounter and address one another as peers within a shared Hasidic community.

The story centers on the Rav solving a halakhic mystery for a woman (dove's blood on her sheet, planted by a jealous sister-in-law) and briefly mentions his visit to Mezeritch — there is no depicted interaction among Hasidim as a social group.

The story explicitly depicts interaction between two Hasidim — R' Ezra and his companion (חבירו) R' Gedaliah, both described as Hasidim — when R' Ezra appears in a dream to R' Gedaliah and addresses him as 'חבירי חבירי', and R' Gedaliah then relays the vision to his household and community; this is peer interaction among Hasidim, not a master-disciple relationship.
The story depicts the Besht's father in captivity and the Besht's own childhood, with no interaction among Hasidim as a community.

The story depicts multiple substantive interactions among Hasidim — R' Yoel (maggid of Nemirov, a member of the Besht's circle) repeatedly conversing with the narrator, the son of the Rabbi of Polonnoye questioning R' Yoel about R' Eliezer's death, and the minyan members being gathered and questioned — though these exchanges serve primarily as a narrative frame for the Besht's visions rather than as the central focus.

The story depicts multiple interactions among Hasidic figures — the Mokhiah, the rabbi of Sharigrad, the Besht's traveling companions, and community members — including the Besht rebuking the Mokhiah and the group journeying together to comfort the expelled rabbi, all of which are inter-Hasidic social interactions beyond a simple master-disciple frame.

The story explicitly depicts a visiting tzaddik (another holy master) coming to observe the Saba Kadisha, whose attendants then question the visiting tzaddik about what he understands of the Saba's actions — a clear, substantive interaction among Hasidic figures beyond the master-disciple frame.

The story centers on R' Yoel the shamash reporting conversations and shared experiences among Hasidim (himself, the Besht's minyan members, R' Eliezer of Tomashpol, the rabbi of Polonnoye), depicting clear interaction among Hasidim as a group.

The story centers on a tsadik (R. Shimshon Wiener) adjudicating a domestic/legal dispute involving a wealthy householder, his daughter, and various individuals, with no depicted interaction among Hasidim as a social group.

The story depicts interactions among multiple named Hasidic figures (R' Pinchas of Koretz visiting the Besht, R' Nachman of Horodenka praying on his behalf, R' Leib Kessler and the chevra kadisha members, the 'anshei segulato' gathering collectively), but the dominant social frame is master-disciple around the Besht's deathbed, making the inter-Hasidic peer dimension present but secondary.

The story explicitly depicts interaction among Hasidim: the Mokhiakh secretly gathers a minyan of Hasidim, acts alongside his mechutan (Reb Zeev Kucyes) at the Besht's table, and the two Hasidim converse — all distinct from the master-disciple relationship with the Besht himself.

The story centers on the Rebbe–Hasidim (master-disciple) relationship — Reb Baruch sends his Hasidim on a mission; the Hasidim travel together but their group deliberation is entirely incidental, and there is no substantive depicted interaction among Hasidim as a social layer independent of the master.

The story centers on a single Hasid (R. Yitzchak) privately mentoring one individual and later communicating with his soul after death — not a depiction of broader interaction among Hasidim as a group.

The story depicts a genuine communal scene where Rabbi Abraham's minyan members accompany him to the field, stand together listening behind the door, and share kiddush together — collective Hasidic social interaction — though this is secondary to the central supernatural/rescue narrative.

The story centrally depicts a sustained social interaction between two Hasidic masters, the Besht and R. Nahman, including the Besht's attempt to outmaneuver R. Nahman, their mutual evasion and pursuit, and their face-to-face greeting and exchange — classic peer-level inter-Hasidic social drama.

The story centers on a social interaction between R' Yitzchak and a fellow community member (R' Birech) in a home minyan setting — not a formal master-disciple relationship — involving the discovery and correction of invalid tefillin, which is a clear instance of inter-Hasidic communal interaction.

The story explicitly depicts interaction among Hasidim — R' Shmuel and R' Ber traveling together, visiting the Maggid and R' Pinchas of Korets, negotiating stays, and supporting one another — all peer-level inter-Hasidic social interaction.
The only social interaction depicted is between the Besht and his close circle who question him about his grief — this is a master-disciple dynamic, not peer interaction among Hasidim.
The text is a solo mystical teaching by Rabbi Uri of Strelisk on prayer; no interaction among Hasidim is depicted.

The story depicts a rabbinic legal case (suspected sotah) handled by R. Chaim Yosef David Azulai (the Chida) in Livorno, with interactions between the rabbi, town judges, and townspeople — there is no portrayal of Hasidim interacting among themselves.
The text is a homiletical Torah teaching (derash on Psalm 94) with no depiction of interaction among Hasidim.
The story depicts a solitary tzadik confronting a seductive innkeeper's wife — there are no other Hasidim present or interacting.

The story depicts a follower visiting the Rebbe of Belz (master-disciple relationship) and a miracle involving a non-Jewish peasant; there is no interaction among Hasidim outside the master-disciple framework.

The story depicts relationships between a poor man, his deceased rebbe, a hidden tzaddik (Moshe Shtagel), and the Rebbe of Rizhin — entirely spiritual master-disciple bonds with no married couple or marital relationship depicted.

The Besht's wife (הרבנית) is mentioned only incidentally in a flashback — she scraped a servant girl and struck her — but the marital relationship between husband and wife is never depicted; the Besht's passive role is noted solely in the context of failing to rebuke his wife, not as a portrayal of their relationship.

The story centrally depicts R. Chaim's secret marriage to the baker's daughter, including the wedding arrangement and his description of his wife as a virtuous woman who fulfills her husband's will (זוגתי היא אשה כשרה ועושה רצון בעלה), making the marital relationship a substantive narrative element.
The wife is mentioned only incidentally as the bearer of the promised son ('תלד אשתך בן זכר'); no actual marital relationship between the couple is depicted in the narrative.

The innkeeper follows the Besht's instruction to keep the doors shut throughout; when the door is finally opened for the freezing nobleman, it is explicitly at the Besht's own signal (רמז הבעש״ט שיפתחו לו), not in defiance of it — no character disobeys a tzaddik's instruction in this story.

The story centers on themes of love of God, devotion, and mindful speech in Torah and prayer; the prince's banishment for foolish speech is a parable element illustrating proper conduct, not a religious/moral prohibition (אסור) being invoked or violated.

The story centers on a rabbinic decree (גזירת הרב) forbidding villagers from spending Passover outside the city, which the tax collector explicitly violates — the prohibition and its transgression are the narrative's driving engine.

While a cherem (excommunication) is issued and a moral wrong (grabbing a rabbi's beard) is depicted, the story's central focus is on afterlife visions and dream revelations, not on a prohibition being issued, deliberated, or violated as a thematic concern.
The story centers on a divine decree of death and the Besht's refusal to heal in deference to it, not on any religious/moral prohibition being issued or violated.
The story describes a mystical vision of black fire on white fire during a Torah reading — no prohibition is issued, violated, or discussed.
The Besht's reluctance to act against the priest is purely strategic (fear of detection by a powerful sorcerer), not framed as a religious or moral prohibition (אסור).
The story is entirely a praise of Rabbi Nachman's heartfelt prayer and the Besht's endorsement of it — no prohibition is mentioned, issued, or violated.

The wife's taking of money is treated as a spiritual disturbance the Besht detects, not as a named prohibition (אסור); the story's focus is on his supernatural perception and the restoration of funds for charity, not on a religious or moral prohibition being invoked or violated.

The word ויראתי appears once (Rabbi Chaim fearing he might fall into arrogance), and ויראת ה׳ appears as an epithet for the Rema at the close, but neither constitutes a depicted experience of fear (of God, a tzaddik, or danger) — the story's emotional core is humility, longing, and hidden piety, not fear.

The story centers on the pious woman's humility, charity, and intercession for a sinner; the single instance of יראתי ('I feared lest you disobey me') is mild parental concern, not the kind of fear (פחד/חרדה) of God, a tzaddik, or danger that the tag targets.

The story centers on humiliation/shame (בזיון, חרפה, כלימה) and self-sacrifice (מסירת נפש); no character is depicted as experiencing fear (פחד/חרדה) — the Hasid's impulse to flee to his rebbe is driven by repentance, not fear, and the dominant emotions are shame and love of Israel.

The story depicts family distress (נצטערו) over a missing son and the father's skepticism toward the Besht, but no character experiences fear (פחד/חרדה) — of God, the tzaddik, or danger — at any point in the narrative.

The story depicts the boy's inner anguish and moral struggle (מתמרמר, ויבך מאוד במר נפשו) and his determination to die rather than convert, but no character is described experiencing fear (פחד/חרדה) — of God, a tzaddik, or danger.

The story depicts amazement (ויתמהו), moral conscience, and heavenly juridical drama, but no character is shown experiencing fear (פחד/חרדה) — the phrase יראת אלקים appears only as a formal epithet for the drunkard's status, not as a depicted emotional experience.
The story depicts disbelief, worry (דאגה), anger, and joy, but no character experiences fear (פחד/חרדה) — of God, a tzaddik, or danger — at any point in the narrative.

The word יראתי appears once, in a colloquial sense ('I feared you would not comply with my wish'), but this is incidental social anxiety rather than the tag's intended fear of God, a tzaddik, or danger; the woman protagonist notably shows no fear even when the Besht rebukes her publicly.

The story depicts the Besht and R. Leib confronting the Samael with resolve — the Besht 'לא קם ולא זע' (did not flinch), and R. Leib's failing is pride (הרהור גדלות), not fear; no character is described as experiencing פחד or חרדה.

The story's dominant emotions are wonder/astonishment (תמיה גדול) at the Besht's heavenly knowledge and grief/distress (צער גדול) of the wandering soul; the one instance of 'אל תדאג' (don't worry) is a brief reassurance to the dying man, not a depicted experience of fear (פחד/חרדה).

The story centers on the power of a single mitzvah (pidyon shvuyim) and the drunkard's moral awakening; while danger (סכנה גדולה) is mentioned retrospectively by the Rebbe regarding the heavenly court, no character is depicted as experiencing fear (פחד/חרדה) — the emissaries display bitachon, and the drunkard's flight from sin is framed as moral conscience, not fear.

The story's dominant emotions are wonder (תמיה גדולה) and distress/sorrow (צער גדול) over the dead man's lack of rest in Gan Eden; no explicit fear (פחד, חרדה) — of God, the tzaddik, or danger — is depicted.

The story depicts genuine fear of mortal danger in two episodes: the storm at sea where 'וייצר להם מאד ויחלו פני ה׳' expresses acute distress and desperate prayer, and the capture by bandits (היידאמאקעס) who bind the Besht and R' Tzvi and sharpen their knives — a clear death-threat scenario, even if the word פחד is not used explicitly.

The story's central theme is bitachon (trust in God), explicitly the opposite of fear — the innkeeper is remarkable precisely because he does not fear or despair in dangerous situations; the single incidental use of 'אירא' refers to a minor practical concern about embezzling the divine partnership, not a genuine depiction of fear as defined by the tag.

The story's central theme is bitachon (trust in God); the single use of 'אירא' refers incidentally to the innkeeper's concern that a household member might misappropriate the partnership funds — not a depiction of fear of God, a tzaddik, or danger.
The story depicts communal weeping (בכיה) and the Besht's great perplexity (מבוכה רבה), but no character is described as experiencing fear (פחד/חרדה) — of God, a tzaddik, or danger.

The rich man listener explicitly experiences visceral fear upon hearing the parable: 'סמרו שערות בשרו פלצות אחזוהו' (his hair stood on end, terror seized him) — a direct depiction of fear (פחד/פלצות) in response to the tzaddik's implied rebuke.

The text explicitly states that the Rabbi's companions 'חרדו מאוד מאימת דינם' (were greatly afraid of the dread of judgment) when brought before the governor, and the Rabbi must reassure them 'אל תיראו' — fear is directly depicted as a central element of the scene.

The story contains multiple explicit depictions of fear: the family feared living in the new stone house (נתיראו לדור בתוכה), the woman was very frightened when the Besht exposed her husband's intent to test him (ונתיראה מאוד), and the Besht himself writes in his letter that he acted out of fear of spiritual harm (שיראתי שלא יזיק לי זה במקום אחר).

The story explicitly depicts intense fear (פחד, בהלה, מורא) when the rabbi first sees the stranger in the locked synagogue (ונבהלתי מאוד) and then faints from overwhelming fear upon beholding Elijah the Prophet (ונתעלפתי מרוב הפחד והמורא) — fear of a divine/prophetic figure is central to the narrative climax.

The story contains mild worry (וידאג) about the lost wagon driver and unease about extending credit to a stranger, but no prominent depiction of fear (פחד/חרדה) — of God, a tzaddik, or genuine danger — as a meaningful emotional experience.
The story's emotional register centers on amazement (תמיה), weeping (בכיה גדולה), desolation (משומם), and repentance — fear (פחד/חרדה) is neither named nor clearly enacted by any character.

The story centers on divine providence, financial sacrifice, and matchmaking orchestrated by the tzaddik; while the woman is compelled by an ultimatum in a vision ('הוכרחתי שלא מרצוני'), no character is depicted as experiencing explicit fear (פחד/חרדה) of God, the tzaddik, or danger — the dominant emotions are distress (ויצטער), compulsion, and wonder.

The rabbi weeps and confesses before the Besht, but this depicts repentance and contrition rather than fear (פחד/חרדה); no character is depicted as experiencing fear of God, the tzaddik, or danger as a primary element of the narrative.

Although a serious threat (iron chains, debt collection) is made against the Besht, he reacts with calm decisiveness rather than fear, and no character in the story is described as experiencing פחד or חרדה — the nobleman's concern about robbery (גזל) is framed as moral scruple and gratitude, not fear.

Although there is a threat of iron chains and a serious financial danger, no character is depicted as experiencing fear (פחד/חרדה) — R' Leib reacts with amazement (נפלא בעיניו) and the Besht responds with brisk, calm action, while the story's emotional register centers on wonder and trust rather than fear.

The homeowner witnesses the hidden tzaddik with Elijah through the wall cracks and is explicitly described as 'נשאר עומד נבהל ומרעיד' (stood stunned and trembling), then flees home 'בפחי נפש' and cannot sleep all night — a clear depiction of fear in the presence of a tzaddik and a holy apparition.

The text explicitly states the melamed 'מתיירא מאד' (feared greatly) that the Besht would reject him in waking life as the servant had in the dream, depicting a named, emotionally significant experience of fear.
The story depicts the Besht in great confusion (מבוכה רבה) and the congregation weeping (בכי), but no character is described as experiencing fear (פחד/חרדה) — of God, a tzaddik, or danger.

The text explicitly states 'ויחרד לב הרר"ד ויתר ממקומו כי הוא ירא' — R' David's heart trembled and he feared that his own deeds seemed right to him (i.e., that he was blind to his own sin), a direct depiction of fear (חרדה/ירא) in response to the Baal Shem Tov's cold treatment.
The text explicitly states 'פחדה פחד אולי התעללו עלילה עליה ח"ו' — the woman experiences clear, named fear (פחד) when she is detained and suspects a false accusation is being made against her.

Rabbi David the Maggid witnesses a supernatural radiance (אור גדול) surrounding the Besht and immediately faints (נתעלף) — a direct physical manifestation of overwhelming fear/awe before the tzaddik's holiness, requiring the Besht to be roused to revive him.
The story depicts obedience to the Besht's instruction and a miraculous outcome (wagon, son's birth), but contains no language or scene of fear (פחד/חרדה) — toward God, the tzaddik, or any danger.

The wealthy miser is explicitly described as being very frightened before the tzaddik ('ויבהל האיש הגביר עד מאוד וזיו אפוהי שנויא'), and the assembled crowd is shaken with fear at the tzaddik's account ('החרידו לקול הסיפור') — both constituting clear, depicted fear of the tzaddik's supernatural knowledge and divine judgment.

The story depicts anger, shame, grief, and spiritual struggle (nocturnal emission, excommunication, rectification), but no character is described as experiencing fear (פחד/חרדה) — not even during the dream encounter with the dead butcher's spirit, where the dreamer actively rushes toward it rather than recoiling in fear.

The story's emotional register centers on wonder and amazement (ויתפלא, וישתומם) and spiritual overwhelm described explicitly as sweetness/pleasantness (עריבות נעימות ומתיקות) — not fear (פחד/חרדה); the tzaddik Garde flees the room due to unbearable spiritual intensity, but the text frames this as ecstatic overwhelm, not fear of God or the tzaddik.

The living man is explicitly gripped by fear (חרדה) from the dead partner's nightly visitations — he falls ill, and the Maharsha's direct reassurance 'אל תפחד לא תמות' confirms his fear; 'ויחרדו לילך עמו' further underscores it.

When the young lord comes before the Tzaddik, the text explicitly states 'הכה לבבו אותו... אשר רוחו יהגה אימה' — his heart struck him with dread (אימה) lest the Tzaddik reproach him over the Pissesir, a clear depiction of fear of the tzaddik.
The text explicitly states the melamed 'מתיירא מאד' (greatly feared) approaching the Besht in waking life, dreading the shame of being turned away as the servant had rejected him in the dream.

The scribe explicitly fears (ירא פן ח״ו יסתכן הבעש״ט) for the Besht's safety due to the dangerous intensity of his prayer, and this fear directly motivates his action of opening the door — a clear, plot-driving instance of fear.

The nobleman enters in a rage and draws his sword to kill the Besht, prompting a cry of alarm — the scene depicts acute mortal danger, and the sick woman's sudden healing is triggered at precisely this moment of threat, making fear of danger central to the story's climax.
The living hasid explicitly experiences great fear (חרדה גדולה) when the soul of the martyred hasid appears to him in his room.

The story's entire point is the innkeeper's conspicuous absence of fear despite the threat of imprisonment — he shows no sadness or agitation ('לא נראה על פניו יגון כלל'), making bitachon (trust), not fear, the operative emotion.

Fear is depicted explicitly multiple times: the Maggid tells the boy 'אל תפחד', Menachem Mendel tells his followers 'אל תפחדו' during his illness, and the people of R. Yaakov Yosef 'חרדו מאוד' fearing their master's punishment when they saw him approaching without a hat.
The story depicts the rabbi weeping and confessing before the Besht (shame/remorse), but no character experiences fear (פחד/חרדה) — of God, of the tzaddik, or of danger — at any point.
The story is a theological teaching on charity and loving-kindness, with no depiction of fear (of God, a tzaddik, or danger) by any character.

The story centers on an intellectual/spiritual confrontation between the Besht and Rabbi Nachman Kassower; while a lethal threat is mentioned in passing (רודף אחרי להמיתני), no character is depicted as actually experiencing fear (פחד/חרדה) — the Besht expresses confidence rather than fear, and Rabbi Nachman's visit is driven by curiosity, not dread.

Moharan's evasive detour when he sees the Besht pursuing him implies concern about being shamed (lacking fish for Shabbat), but the text depicts strategic avoidance of embarrassment rather than explicit fear (פחד/חרדה) of the tzaddik or any danger.

The story's central focus is the mystical encounter and discipleship transmission between the Besht and the Maggid; virtuous acts (hospitality, invoking 'love the stranger') appear as narrative context rather than as the story's moral point.

The story's climax centers on the woman's genuine repentance and humble acceptance of severe penance, and the Besht's selfless, compassionate mission to save her soul — both functioning as illustrations of virtue, even if repentance and miracle-working are also foregrounded.

The story centers on the Besht's ecstatic, trembling prayer and his mystical perception of the Satan blocking the prayer path — the deliberate public humiliation of R' David is a strategic spiritual act to break his heart (purify his prayer), not an illustration of a general virtue.

The story's central point is the miraculous power of the Besht and divine providence (culminating in the supernatural Shabbat encounter with Jonah ben Amitai), not the illustration of a general virtue — the consequence of disobeying the Besht's instruction is a narrative device for the miracle, not a virtue lesson per se.

The story centrally illustrates the virtue of humility (and its opposite, pride) through the brothers' initial disdain for the Maggid's ways, and climaxes with R. Zushe's teaching about a true Torah leader being 'hidden like an angel' — the entire narrative arc is designed to demonstrate and extol spiritual virtue.

The story centers on the Besht's mystical perception and his unconventional act of inducing the priest's keri to remove a heavenly accusation (kitreg) blocking Kol Nidre prayers — it is a tale of spiritual power and esoteric intervention, not the illustration of a virtue.

The story's explicit closing moral ('ראה כמה גדלה כח התשובה') frames the entire narrative as an illustration of the virtue of repentance (teshuvah), making a general virtue the stated point of the story.

The story's central point is mystical recognition of holy men and spiritual/Torah transmission (three days of devekut), not the illustration of a virtue; the charitable framing (pidyon shvuyim) is background context, and the gratitude teaching within is a kabbalistic insight rather than a moral exemplum.

The story explicitly highlights the Rabbi's humility (ענותנות) as a central virtue — he repeatedly deflects rather than override other rabbis' rulings — and his extraordinary discernment (identifying the dove's blood) is presented as the story's moral point about his exceptional character.

The story centrally depicts and valorizes the virtue of honesty: R' Leib warns the nobleman that he may be taking money not rightfully his (gzl), the nobleman explicitly praises this act as saving him from theft and punishment in the afterlife, and the virtuous intervention drives the plot to resolution.
The story's point is to illustrate the Besht's extraordinary mystical capacity to elevate souls of the dead during prayer — a spiritual power, not a general virtue being held up as a moral exemplar.

The story closes with an explicit moral lesson urging caution about entering into dispute (מחלוקת), illustrating the virtue of peaceful restraint — even quarrels waged for heaven's sake cause great harm, so one should guard one's spirit against hasty conflict.

The story's central point is to illustrate virtue — specifically humility and openness to spiritual growth — through the Horowitz brothers' arc from proud skepticism to devoted discipleship, culminating in the Maggid's parable about unlit candles suddenly ignited.

The story's explicit moral is that R' Meir merited encountering the hidden tzaddik R' Leib Sarah's specifically because he exercised the virtue of obedience to his Rebbe's command — burning the cherished writings at great personal cost — making the illustration of this virtue the narrative's central point.

The story explicitly illustrates ethical virtues — Rabbi David's humble acceptance of undeserved punishment, silent endurance, and the closing moral directly instructs readers to weigh the reward of a mitzvah against its loss and not trust their own judgment over Divine Providence, making virtuous conduct central to the story's didactic point.

The story is a hagiographic death-scene narrative focused on mystical events surrounding the Besht's passing; while chesed and forgiveness appear incidentally, the story's point is not to illustrate a virtue but to recount the holy death itself.

The story's central purpose is to showcase the Besht's supernatural powers (clairvoyance, miraculous travel) and social authority, not to illustrate a general virtue; ethical elements (knife inspection, measured restraint in departing) are incidental to the narrative rather than its moral point.

The story's climax explicitly illustrates the virtue of faithfulness/discretion: the Maggid explains through the verse about Moses ('in all my house he is faithful') that a true leader does not reveal what he foresees, and this principle — not a side detail — is the story's stated moral point.

The story explicitly praises the Posesor's integrity and uprightness (תום ויושר) as the moral foundation that earns divine protection, and the narrative arc — virtue rewarded, wickedness punished — functions to illustrate virtuous conduct, even if faith in the Tzadik and divine justice are equally foregrounded.

The story's climax is the martyrs' explicit refusal to take revenge through reincarnation, choosing spiritual integrity over vengeance — a clear illustration of virtue (self-sacrifice and moral restraint) as the story's central point.

The story's central concern is the impossibility of transferring sin through a commercial transaction and the necessity of personal repentance (teshuvah), not the positive illustration of a virtue — the first man's regret is present but interrupted, and the narrative culminates in a legal-theological ruling rather than a virtue exemplified.

The story's central point is the demonstration of divine power and holiness over sorcery, not the illustration of a virtue; while the Besht displays courage and piety, these are incidental to the miracle-and-power narrative rather than the story's moral focus.

The story is built around the vice of envy (קנאה) as its central ethical motor — the second young man's jealousy over his friend's match drives the entire plot and leads him into a supernatural trap, making a moral lesson about envy unmistakably the story's point, though the virtue implicated is specific rather than 'general/unspecified.'

The story's central point is to illustrate the virtue of humility and the acceptance of spiritual authority: the skeptical rabbi witnesses the Besht's awesome state of prayer, is overcome, and consequently submits to his guidance — a classic Hasidic illustration of a general virtue (humility/reverence).

The story's central point is to illustrate the virtue of sincere, spiritually potent prayer: R' Nachman's unauthorized but divinely-inspired davening is 'sweet as honey,' implicitly valorizing genuine devotion over formal permission and established custom.

The story is a cautionary dream-vision about moral failings (lying about one's origin, impure thoughts during charity collection, unpaid Torah-study fees) and their consequences in Gehinnom — it illustrates vices and their punishment rather than depicting or exemplifying a virtue.

The story's central thrust is the supernatural clairvoyance of the tzadikim and the exposure/rectification of communal sexual sin, not the illustration of a positive virtue — vices are foregrounded, not virtues.

The story's central moral axis is R"G's insistence on truthfulness even under communal pressure and the heavenly validation that his stand was 'for God's sake' (קנא קנאת ה'), making the virtue of integrity/truth-telling the implicit lesson, though the narrative is complex enough that no single virtue is its explicit stated point.

The story's explicit moral lesson is about the virtue of honoring betrothal commitments and the wrongfulness of breaking engagements, directly illustrating this ethical principle as its central point.

The story's explicit moral ('הנהנה מיגיע כפו בכשרות') and its entire narrative arc illustrate the virtues of honest labor, commercial integrity, and simple piety through the craftsman whom the Besht declares 'the foundation of the synagogue.'
The story centers on the Besht's supernatural perception of a death via a physical sign (soiled tzitzit), not on illustrating a virtue.

The story's point is to demonstrate the Besht's supernatural clairvoyance and protective power against robbers, not to illustrate a virtue; the husband's initial reluctance and the wife's persistence are merely narrative setup, not ethical exempla.

The story depicts a hidden tzaddik (Moshe Shtagel) who feigns wickedness as a disguise, but no character actually turns from sin — the 'bad behavior' is a facade concealing secret holiness, not genuine transgression followed by repentance.

The story centers on humility (שפלות) and hidden piety — Rabbi Chaim requests humility preemptively to guard against pride, but no character actually commits a sin and turns from it; the accusation of sin against Rabbi Chaim is false and quickly dismissed.

The story centers on the Baal Shem Tov and his disciples suffering a punishment (desecrated Sabbath) for failing to rebuke wrongdoing; while there is atonement-like resolution, no character explicitly turns from sin in an act of teshuvah.

The brief mention of the Shpola townspeople having been sinners whom the Shpola Zeyde brought back to God is incidental background context explaining his nickname, not a depicted act of teshuvah; the story's actual plot concerns childlessness, competing oaths of two tzadikim, and a charitable deed.
The story depicts the Baal Shem Tov's hidden pious life and eventual public revelation, with no character turning from sin in a teshuvah arc.

The story centers on humility (shfeilut) and hidden piety, not repentance; the false accusation of sin against R. Chaim is immediately cleared, and no character is depicted turning away from wrongdoing.

No character genuinely turns from sin in the sense of teshuvah; the antisemitic nobleman's 'acceptance' not to hate Jews is coerced under threat, not a voluntary moral turning, and the brigands' oath is a pragmatic deal rather than repentance.

The story centers on a merchant's faith in the Besht, miraculous intervention, and a mysterious encounter with the prophet Jonah, with no character depicted as turning from sin in any meaningful teshuvah sense — the merchant's regret is about his life circumstances, not moral wrongdoing.

The story depicts the Baal Shem Tov's hidden asceticism, a dybbuk exorcism, rain prayer, and his emergence as a healer — R' Gershon's hope that the Besht might 'return to the good path' is a framing remark, not an actual depiction of a character turning from sin in repentance.

The story explicitly states that the Shpoler Zeyde brought the sinful townspeople (who desecrated the Sabbath and neglected Torah) back under the wings of the Shekhinah — 'השיבם אל בוראם ונעשו כגרים שנתגיירו' — a direct depiction of communal teshuvah.
The story centers entirely on the tavern-keeper's extraordinary bitachon (trust in God) as the source of his miraculous powers — no character sins or turns from sin at any point.

The story depicts intercession on behalf of an adulterous doctor (the woman argues he sins out of ignorance), but the doctor never turns from sin — there is no teshuvah scene, only advocacy that secures his healing.
The story centers on providential reunion, marital fidelity, and moral integrity — no character is depicted turning from sin in the teshuvah sense.
The story depicts the Maggid's illness, his coming to the Besht for healing, and a mystical Kabbalistic transmission — no sin or turning from wrongdoing is mentioned anywhere in the narrative.
The story narrates the righteous conduct of the Besht's father in captivity and the Besht's own early holy deeds — no character is depicted turning from sin in teshuvah.

The story centers on the pious Rivele's intercession on behalf of a sinful doctor (an adulterer), but the doctor himself is never depicted turning from sin — he is healed through her advocacy, not through any act of teshuvah.

The single mention of 'לשוב בתשובה שלימה' is a passing theoretical aspiration about spiritual deepening, not a narrative depiction of a character actually turning from sin; the story's protagonists are already righteous and the plot centers on spiritual seeking and Hasidic recruitment.

The story depicts a learned son-in-law rebuked by R. Elimelech for hidden arrogance (pride in his own scholarship), but no act of repentance or turning from sin by any character is shown — the narrative ends with the rebuke itself.
The story depicts miraculous travels, divine providence, and the Besht's joy at serving God without self-interest, but no character turns from sin or undergoes teshuvah.

The story centers on a man afflicted by demonic forces who seeks the rebbe's spiritual protection and receives a remedy (reciting words of faith); there is no depiction of a character turning from sin in the teshuvah sense — the affliction is presented as external malevolent attack, not as consequence of personal transgression, and the rebbe's response is a protective/mystical cure rather than a call to repentance.

The story is a financial/miracle narrative about the Besht arranging wedding funds; the nobleman's recalculation of a debt to avoid inadvertent theft is a momentary ethical correction, not a depicted act of teshuvah (turning from sin).

The story depicts divine punishment and downfall of informers (מוסרים), with Avigdor explicitly described as wicked from beginning to end (ברשעתו מתחילתו ועד סופו) and no character shown turning from sin in teshuvah.
The story centers on the Baal Shem Tov's mystical practice of elevating souls of the dead during prayer, with no depiction of a character turning from sin in repentance.
The story recounts the Maggid's first encounter with the Besht, his illness and healing, and a mystical Torah-reception experience — no character turns from sin or performs teshuvah.

The story centers on the Besht's mystical trembling during prayer and his spiritual manipulation of the prayer-paths on Yom Kippur; the public shaming of R' David is a tactical heart-breaking to block foreign thoughts, not a depiction of a character turning from sin in teshuvah.

The story depicts the Besht's deep mourning over martyred Jews and a visitation by their souls debating vengeance vs. reincarnation — there is no character turning from sin (teshuvah) anywhere in the narrative.

The story centers on a financial dispute resolved through honesty, with the nobleman grateful to be spared from unknowing theft (גזל), but no character is depicted turning from sin already committed — there is no teshuvah process.

The son's transformation from wild behavior to Torah devotion is presented as a miraculous result of the father following the Besht's instruction, not as an act of teshuvah — no character is depicted consciously turning from sin through repentance.

The story centers on a hidden tzaddik (tailor) who appoints a rabbi from his deathbed and a mystical vision of Elijah; while sins in the city are fleetingly mentioned as needing correction, no character is shown actually turning from sin in an act of teshuvah.

The story narrates a preacher's journey to Hamburg, the Besht's spiritual guidance and protection from the Satan, and communal reception — no character is depicted turning from sin in an act of teshuvah.

The story focuses on Rabbi Leib Sarahs's miraculous travels, care for hidden tzadikim, and a wandering soul receiving a tikkun — but no character is depicted as turning from sin in an act of teshuvah.

A dying man briefly confesses a sin to the Besht ('הודה') and expresses regret, but this is incidental to the story's focus on the Besht's intercessory powers, the broken promise, and the soul's wandering in Gan Eden — not a depicted arc of turning from sin.

The story depicts the Besht's death on Shavuot and his final hours, including a wandering soul episode where the Besht rebukes and expels the spirit rather than guiding it to repentance; there is no depiction of a character turning from sin.
The story depicts a hidden tsaddik whose power stems from extraordinary trust in God (bitachon) and charitable giving — there is no character who turns from sin or undergoes teshuvah.
The story centers on the Rabbi's halakhic discernment in exposing the sister-in-law's deception, but no character is depicted turning from sin or undergoing teshuvah.
The story depicts rabbinic validation of a shochet's credentials and a tzadik's spiritual discernment, with no character turning from sin or undergoing teshuvah.

The story focuses on the Besht's intense mystical prayer states and his spiritual tactic of publicly humiliating the precentor to break his heart and prevent extraneous thoughts — the Besht explicitly says he saw nothing disgraceful in the man, so there is no sin from which anyone turns.

Although the word teshuvah appears explicitly ('לשוב בתשובה שלימה') and R. Israel fulfills the penitential practices of Teshuvot HaKaneh, the narrative depicts teshuvah as a spiritual discipline and prerequisite for understanding Torah lishmah — not a character turning from sin; all protagonists are already portrayed as pious individuals throughout.

The story centers on demonic affliction and the Rebbe's spiritual rescue through sacred words and the unity of God — not on a character turning from sin; the man's later lapse is framed as negligence/inattention rather than moral transgression requiring teshuvah.
The story depicts a supernatural confrontation between the Besht and the Satan (Samael) to retrieve a hidden secret, with no character turning from sin in repentance.
The story depicts the Besht demonstrating spiritual powers, navigating social conflict, and fleeing potential humiliation — no character turns from sin in a teshuvah arc.
The story depicts a preacher seeking mystical knowledge of animal speech from the Besht, who teaches then erases it; there is no turning from sin or teshuvah at any point.

The story centers on devotion (dveikut), love of God, and persuading Elimelech to join the Hasidic path; while the embedded parable includes a prince banished for foolish speech who is counseled to reform his words, this functions as a metaphor for spiritual alignment rather than depicting teshuvah (turning from sin) as a central, substantive theme.

No character undergoes teshuvah — the king mourns believing the Gaon was killed, the princess feels brief regret (ותתנחם) but does not turn from sin, and the story's moral focus is divine providence protecting the righteous rather than any repentance arc.

The story centers on spiritual seeking, obedience to rabbinic authority (burning cherished writings to save his son), and recognition of a hidden tzaddik — the burning is framed as corrective obedience rather than teshuvah (turning from sin), and the one described as having 'wronged' (עוות) is the unnamed author of the writings, not Rabbi Meir himself.

The story depicts a heavenly trial weighing a boy's sins against merits and sufferings, with intercession by the Besht securing his release, but no character is shown actively turning from sin (teshuvah) at any point.

The mother's weeping over having cursed the Besht is a brief emotional reaction, not a depicted act of teshuvah — the story's focus is entirely on the miraculous healing, and no formal or narrative turn-from-sin is developed.

The story centers on the Besht's grief over martyrs and a heavenly visitation by the kedoshim who recount their post-death experience; while a brief mention of the evil inclination disturbing their thoughts is noted, no character undergoes teshuvah (turning from sin) at any point.
The story centers on trust in God (bitachon) and a unique partnership with the Divine; no character turns from sin or undergoes teshuvah.

No character actually turns from sin in this story; the closest element — Shabbetai Zevi approaching the Besht for a 'tikun' — ends in further seduction and damnation rather than any depicted act of repentance.
The story depicts the Besht neutralizing a heavenly accusation (קטרוג) by causing the priest to experience a seminal emission — there is no character who turns from sin in repentance.

The mother's tearful remorse for having cursed the Besht is a brief, incidental coda to the story; the narrative's focus is entirely on the miraculous healing of the child, not on any character's teshuvah process.
The story depicts a scholar's encounter with the Besht leading to Rabbi Yechiel drawing closer to him, but no character turns from sin — repentance (teshuvah) is entirely absent.

The nagid is supernaturally confronted by the deceased Maggid for his misdeeds and tries to make excuses, but no actual turning from sin (teshuvah) is depicted — he faints and the scene ends without repentance or resolution.

The story centers on a miraculous intercession for a woman in dangerous childbirth (mikveh immersion, prayer), with no depiction of a character turning from sin in the teshuvah sense; the brief request for forgiveness (מחילה) is incidental social repair after a financial dispute, not a repentance narrative.
The story depicts an act of compassion — the rabbi delays Kol Nidre to comfort a crying infant — with no character turning from sin or any repentance (teshuvah) theme present.

The story depicts the Besht's spiritual perception (discerning an invalid mezuzah through a sigh in prayer) and a social dynamic with his brother-in-law R' Gershon — no character turns from sin or undergoes teshuvah.
The story centers on inter-communal conflict between Hasidim and Mitnagdim and a rebuke by the Maggid for acting without his guidance, but no character undergoes teshuvah or turns from sin.

The husband returns home after 15 years only because he is repeatedly pressured and ultimately threatened with death by the mysterious old man (the Rebbe's emissary), and falls gravely ill — there is no depicted moral self-reckoning, confession of sin, or voluntary turning from wrongdoing that constitutes teshuvah.
The story praises a pious craftsman for his consistent prayer and honest labor — no character turns from sin or undergoes any act of repentance.
The story describes a war and the destruction of a city and its surroundings, with no depiction of a character turning from sin or any act of repentance (teshuvah).
The story depicts a betrothal ceremony, a mystical vision of a deceased tzaddik in radiant garments, and expressions of humility — there is no depiction of a character turning from sin (teshuvah).
The story centers on the piety, humility, and charitable advocacy of 'die fromme Rivele' with no depiction of sadness or melancholy as a theme or spiritual problem.

The story centers on mystical prayer, elevation of souls, and Rabbi Gershon's fright/fainting when encountering the dead — sadness or melancholy (עצבות/מרה שחורה) as a spiritual problem is entirely absent.

The story is a hagiographic narrative about miraculous deeds and the transmission of mystical writings to the BeShT; the wife's mention of צער (hardship/poverty) refers to material distress, not sadness/melancholy (עצבות) as a spiritual problem.
The story centers on the piety, humility, and spiritual greatness of the 'fromme Rivele' with no depiction of sadness or melancholy (עצבות/מרה שחורה) as a spiritual theme or problem.

The story centers on the Besht's mystical prayer practice of elevating souls of the dead and Rabbi Gershon's attempt to imitate it; there is no depiction of sadness or melancholy (עצבות/מרה שחורה) as a spiritual problem or otherwise.

Sadness (עצב) is mentioned once only in the negative — the innkeeper states 'there was no sadness within me' (ועצב לא היה בקרבי) as evidence of his strong bitachon; the story's central theme is trust in God, not sadness as a spiritual problem being depicted or addressed.

The story depicts the Mokhiach's travels, sermons, and spiritual protection by the Besht, with challenges of illness and forgetting his teachings, but sadness or melancholy (עצבות/מרה שחורה) as a spiritual problem is nowhere present or addressed.

The story's emotional register centers on distress from physical hardship, joyful celebration, and adventure — culminating in the Besht's remarkable joy (וישמח מאד) at losing his share in the World to Come; sadness/melancholy as a spiritual problem (עצבות) is neither depicted nor addressed.

The story centers on longing/desire (תשוקה) for esoteric knowledge and the Besht's lesson about staying true to one's spiritual path — sadness or melancholy (עצבות) neither appears as a motif nor is addressed as a spiritual problem.

The story centers on a spiritual quest for true Torah lishmah and the spread of Hasidism, with no depiction of sadness or melancholy (עצבות/מרה שחורה) as a theme or spiritual problem — the single emotional note close to longing is R' Azriel's געגועים (yearning) for the Maggid, which is a positive spiritual impulse, not melancholy.
The story centers on supernatural vision, humiliation-caused death, Sabbatean heresy, and spiritual downfall through pride (גדלות) and anger (כעס) — not sadness or melancholy as a spiritual theme.

The man's distress (ויקץ בחייו, טוב מותו מחייו) over his wayward son is situational anguish that frames the plot, but the story's spiritual focus is on obedience to the Besht's instructions and divine providence — sadness/melancholy (עצבות) as a spiritual problem is not depicted or addressed.

The story's emotional arc centers on hardship, trust in God, and joy (שמחה) — especially the Besht's elated reaction to losing his share in the world-to-come — with no depiction of sadness/melancholy (עצבות) as a spiritual problem being addressed.

The story revolves around a power struggle between R' Yitzchak and the Besht over the efficacy of amulets, with themes of spiritual authority and reconciliation — sadness/melancholy (עצבות) is not depicted or addressed.

The story centers on the Besht defeating a sorcerer who killed newborns; the innkeeper's distress is situational fear and grief (פחד), not sadness/melancholy (עצבות) as a spiritual condition the story addresses.

The word עצב appears only once and in negation ('ועצב לא היתה בקרבי'), illustrating the innkeeper's exceptional trust in God; sadness/melancholy is not a spiritual problem the story addresses but a foil to the central theme of bitachon.
The story focuses entirely on miraculous travels, hidden tzaddikim, and spiritual missions of Rabbi Leib Sarah's — there is no depiction of sadness or melancholy as a theme or spiritual problem.

The avrech's distress ('חייו אינם חיים') is a narrative plot trigger, not a spiritual problem of sadness/melancholy (עצבות) that the story thematically addresses or resolves; the story's focus is on the tzaddik's hidden providential matchmaking.
The story focuses on gratitude, miraculous healing, and a business warning — sadness/melancholy (עצבות) neither appears as a theme nor is addressed as a spiritual problem anywhere in the narrative.

The story centers on the Besht's prophetic warning and its tragic fulfillment (R' Moshe dying in Venetian imprisonment), with no depiction of sadness or melancholy (עצבות/מרה שחורה) as a spiritual problem being addressed.

The story centers on bitachon (trust in God) and the widow's misguided attempts to direct divine providence, not on sadness/melancholy (עצבות) as a spiritual problem; her distress is situational poverty, not the thematic focus.

The story centers on a dispute about falsehood, excommunication, and heavenly judgment, with no depiction of sadness or melancholy (עצבות/מרה שחורה) as a theme or spiritual problem; the single instance of weeping (התחיל לבכות) is a momentary reaction of fear upon realizing one is being condemned, not a portrayal of sadness as a spiritual issue.
The story depicts joyful Simchat Torah celebration with dancing and drinking; there is no sadness or melancholy present, only festivity and a minor domestic concern resolved miraculously.

The story centers on joy (שמחה) — R' Shbtai dances three times with his wife in gratitude, the heavenly court rejoices, and the Besht laughs; poverty and hardship are present but sadness/melancholy as a spiritual problem is entirely absent.
The story centers on arrogance and disrespect toward rabbinic authority followed by divine punishment, with no depiction of sadness or melancholy (עצבות/מרה שחורה) as a theme or spiritual concern.

The story depicts a hasidic master intervening to rescue a Jew from physical punishment by a nobleman — there is no mention of sadness or melancholy (עצבות/מרה שחורה) as a theme, experience, or spiritual concern anywhere in the narrative.
The story centers on fear/trembling (חרדה) and yirat chet in the face of spiritual danger from honor, not on sadness or melancholy (עצבות/מרה שחורה) as a theme or problem.

The phrase 'דוה לבי עליו' describes a momentary pang of grief the Besht felt upon suddenly seeing the Angel of Death — an incidental emotional aside, not the thematic treatment of sadness/melancholy (עצבות) as a spiritual problem the story is meant to address.

The scholar reacts to the Besht's revelation about his past incarnation with trembling (ויחרד) and contrition, seeking a tikkun — the text depicts fear/awe and penitence, not shame (בושה) as a spiritual or social consequence.

The merchant is proved wrong and seeks forgiveness for speaking lashon hara, and the Besht calls him a fool, but the story focuses on the Besht's miraculous perception and the merchant's conversion to belief — shame (בושה) is not depicted as a named spiritual or social consequence central to the narrative.

The story briefly mentions youths mocking the preacher in the street and a note that 'המוכיח נפל בגדלותו,' but shame is incidental — the narrative centers on the Besht's supernatural guidance, spiritual protection, and the successful preaching mission, with no character undergoing shame as a spiritual or social consequence.

The story depicts hardship, loss of spiritual power, and a heavenly decree against the Besht, but no character is shamed (בושה) as a spiritual or social consequence — the dominant themes are divine providence, pure service of God, and miraculous rescue.

The story centers on the Maggid's illness, healing by the Besht, and a mystical Kabbalah transmission; while the Besht's sharp rebuke ('my horses don't eat matzot') is momentarily jarring, no character is depicted as experiencing shame (בושה) as a spiritual or social consequence.

The story centers on poverty, loss, and providential matchmaking, but no character undergoes shaming (בושה) as a spiritual or social consequence — the matchmaker's initial reluctance and sarcasm is incidental comic friction, not a depiction of shame.

The story centers on mystical prayer, the raising of souls, and R' Gershon's fear/fainting when confronted with the dead — there is no shaming (בושה) of any character as a spiritual or social consequence.
The story centers on demonic affliction, the Rebbe's healing power, and his death — no character is depicted as experiencing shame (בושה) as a spiritual or social consequence.

The story contains no scene where a character experiences shame (בושה) as a spiritual or social consequence; the laughter directed at R' Yisrael for calling the Maharsha a mussar book is incidental mockery that he himself strategically provokes, and he shows no shame in response.

The story centers entirely on the innkeeper's extraordinary trust in God (בטחון) and the Rebbe of Apt's investigation of his powers; no character experiences shame as a spiritual or social consequence at any point in the narrative.
